protocols.title: Obtained results I presented in AACR:
protocols.category: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_1 - pilot experiment
steps: Long PFS showed Spatial distances This part is very interesting Resting and arrested CD8 T cells were closer to myeloids in postNACT Short-PFS, while proliferative were far away.

Similar pattern in Long-PFS but CD8 T cells seemed in general terms more distant to myeloids Spatial co-ocurrence in between cell-types change in after chemotherapy Long PFS seemed to be maintaining many neighbourhoods present in the pre, and showed increased interactions within proliferative cells of all types.
Cancer proliferative cells seem to be interacting with many immune cell types that are also proliferating.
Short PFS lost many of those initial interactions seen in Pre, especially within the proliferative compartment (CD4-CD8, CD4-Cancer, CD8-myeloid,CD8-Cancer, CD4-CD8).
Also, proliferative myeloid cells lose many of their interactions with Cancer and CD8.
p21 and Ki67 are co-expressed in a subset of atypical cells of high-grade/poorly differentiated, advanced human carcinomas .
p53-independent expression of p21 upregulates RLFs post-transcriptionally https://www.nature.com/articles/ncb3378 .
P53 knockdown Cellular models showed that after escaped’ clones demonstrated enhanced anchorage-independent growth and were more invasive (Fig.

Furthermore, they tolerated treatment with the genotoxic drugs doxorubicin and cisplatin much more efficiently (ki67 pink, p21 cyt, dapi, pre-NACT sample) panck magenta, green (CyclinA) red (p27) In ovca PAX8 is a positive regulator of TP53 expression in HGSC.
However, the oncogenic properties of mutant p53 in HGSC render PAX8 a positive regulator of transformation rather than a growth suppressor.

Furthermore, unexpectedly [28], the mutant p53 upregulates p21 in HGSC, which in this type of cancer plays a non-canonical cytoplasmic pro-proliferative role, further enhancing the cancer-promoting effect of PAX8.

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41388-017-0040-z Although the sample was not powered to detect a small difference, we did not observe any difference in p21 staining and localization between previously treated and non-treated tumors, implying that the ubiquitous p21 cytoplasmic staining was not restricted only to chemotherapy resistant cells.

protocols.title: Research Progress Report Template
protocols.category: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc
steps: Explore the composition of the TME by analyzing the abundance of phenotypes across the cohort.

Compare two different spatial analysis methods—spatial count and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA)—using different parameters (radius: 13, 25, 35 microns) and assessing their suitability for various types of analysis.
Determine which approach is better suited for specific analytical goals and compare the results between distinct clinical groups to explore potential differences and trends.

Finished figures for thesis stored in memory stick: PENDR-ADA:\Alva\Finished_figures Step 1: Composition analysis Description: Analyze the composition of (1) global cell types and (2) immune cell types across the cohort.
Step 2: Spatial analysis - computing spatial count and LDA Description: Compute spatial count and LDA and cluster the results into RCNs.
Spatial count and LDA are computed using the following radii: 13 microns (40 px), 25 microns (77 px), and 35 microns (108 px).
The results are clustered using KMeans clustering, with the optimal number of clusters (k) determined using elbow curves and the KneeLocator method from the kneelocator package.
Annotation of RCNs based on barplots and image verification.
Step 3: Spatial analysis - clustree Description: Clustree plots of spatial count and LDA results for the different radii used (13,25,35 microns).
Plotting the results as dotplots.

document_index.excerpt: 4.10 Expansion — Stitching Quality Control Notes Stitching and registration with Ashlar - Guidelines for reporting in Napari and visually check and Stitching verification: That all tiles are registered in all cycles 2) Marker order verification: The cycles are registered in the right order - correct looking marker expression in each cycle 3) Registration verification: tiles are aligned correctly: check 1st and last cycle In practice - can be done in your laptop using Napari open one sample, check the channels one by one and check that i) signal matches the marker ii) no tiles are missing in any marker Overlay DAPI signal from 1st to last cycle - 1st cycle in red, last in green and confirm that you have only yellow nuclei -repeat steps 1 and 2 for all sample one by one Some shift in cycle 7

document_index.excerpt: 27-02-2025 Ada - Anniina Considerations for metaclustering •Fluorescence Variability: Differences between samples complicate normalization and cross-sample comparisons. •Unknown Cluster Count: The number of clusters is not predefined, and some metaclusters may be unique to specific samples. •Cell cycle aberrancies: Tumors’ cell cycle is aberrant in a unique way, making the co-expression of certain markers possible •Validation Requirement: Image-based validation is essential to confirm accuracy and biological relevance. Current clustering strategies Current clustering strategies (I) – Sample level Our main goal is to define how to integrate the clusters Quite manual How do we classify the Hard to automate sample-specific clusters? document_index.excerpt: Research Progress Report Template Profiling of Spatial Tumor Microenvironment Architecture in Highly Multiplexed Images of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma Expected Completion Date: GitHub Repository (if applicable): Brief Description of Research Goals: Refine image-derived quantified data through data normalization and assignment of precise phenotype labels to the cells of the tumor microenvironment (TME). Explore the composition of the TME by analyzing the abundance of phenotypes across the cohort. Compare two different spatial analysis methods—spatial count and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA)—using different parameters (radius: 13, 25, 35 microns) and assessing their suitability for various types of analysis. document_index.title: We found differential NL (nuclear localized) and membrane-localized (MEM) patterns of Cyclin D1.
document_index.excerpt: We found differential NL (nuclear localized) and membrane-localized (MEM) patterns of Cyclin D1. The cyclin D1MEM was sufficient to induce G1–S cell-cycle progression, cellular proliferation, and colony formation The cytoplasmic sequestration of cyclin D1 is important to maintain the non-proliferative state as nuclear enforced expression using a nuclear-localized form of cyclin D1 forces the cell into a proliferative state Cyclin D1 has been identified in the cytoplasmic membrane and shown to bind and regulate the function of several cytoplasmic membrane-associated proteins including PACSIN II (Protein kinase C and Casein kinase Substrate In Neurons protein 2)23 also known as syndapin), Filamin A24 and paxillin21. document_index.title: Research Progress
document_index.excerpt: Project Title: ProteoPicking Student Name(s): Lina Maltrovsky Supervisor/Lab: Ada Junquera Expected Completion Date: 31.03.25 GitHub Repository (if applicable): https://github.com/farkkilab/ProteoPick_LinasThesis https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1FikgMgnoSuW8eaZljcNtUKKxV28b12tn?usp=sharing Brief Description of Research Goals: In this project, we want to establish a protocol for a new method that combines highly multiplexed imaging with proteomics analysis. For this we are using the picking function of the RarecyteFinder to isolate specific parts of the t-CycIF stained tissue. The cell samples are then sent to a collaboration partner for the proteomics analysis. Moreover, an analysis pipeline for the analysis of the data that is collected during ProteoPicking experiments was created

document_index.title: Sample sectioning
document_index.excerpt: Tissue samples were processed into paraffin blocks by the tissue preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Department in Biomedicum. Of those formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks 10 µm thick tissue sections were sliced with a Microm HM 355 rotary microtome, placed on SuperFrost micros 10cope glass slides and dried by first baking them in the oven at 56°C for 2 h for and then leaving them at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards they were stored at 4°C until further usage. Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre-staining First, slides were baked at 56°C for 30 min to ensure the tissue was properly attached to the slide. Then, all slides were deparaffinized and rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each followed by immer

This is essential for cells to replicate DNA efficiently during cell cycle progression. : Both pathways are upregulated, driving active progression from G1 to S phase. These pathways are associated with increased glucose uptake, altered oxidative phosphorylation, and the promotion of cancer stem-like properties. : Promotes cell growth and proliferation by regulating cell cycle progression. document_index.excerpt: LCM-Proteomics Pipeline: Troubleshooting and DVP Replication 1. Root Cause Analysis: Low Protein Yield and Polymer Contamination The previous experimental run experienced significant polymer contamination and suboptimal protein identification. When comparing the executed workflow against the Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) pipeline, several critical discrepancies emerge as the primary sources of these issues: Plate Material and High-Heat Decrosslinking: Standard 4titude 96-well PCR plates were utilized. These plates are manufactured with plasticizers and mold-release agents. Subjecting these plates to high-heat FFPE thermal decrosslinking in the presence of urea aggressively leached these polymers into the microscopic sample volume, causing massive mass spectrometer interference. Furthermore,

document_index.title: Student:
document_index.excerpt: Professor Anniina Färkkilä Single-cell spatial proteomic profiling of subsister cell phenotypes in HGSC Introduction & Preliminary Findings First-line therapies in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) often fail to completely eradicate the tumor, leaving a reservoir of cells that survive chemotherapy - termed " ". We hypothesize that these spatially distinct and phenotypically heterogeneous cell populations drive divergent clinical outcomes, including tumor relapse. To investigate this, we performed highly multiplexed t-CycIF (40 markers, n=27) Xenium spatial transcriptomics (430 genes, n=16) on Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS) samples. Our preliminary spatial analysis resolved these subsister cells into phenotypically distinct and spatially segregated niches, identifying Highly Proliferativ

document_index.excerpt: : Spatial profiling of therapeutically targetable, cell cycle-related molecules in Post-NACT HGSC HRP patients Cell cycle-related spatial biology has never been studied within HGSC Many vulnerabilities (CCNE amp, DDR) are key targets that could potentially succeed in the treatment Most of the usable markers are nuclear stainings, which eases the process Cell fate is found within the intersection of many other processes that can be also studied in the same cohort (metabolism, DNA damage etc) Potential risks of treatments of interest: 1) off-target inhibitory effect on close family members of target protein 2) acquired drug resistance due to mutants on target or upstream regulators 3) many of these drugs are still under development Technical considerations (DNA vs S phase marker, dimensional

document_index.title: cyclophosphamide with the anthracycline doxorubicin
document_index.excerpt: HGSC is often diagnosed at advanced stages due to the lack of effective screening strategies, together with unspecific symptomatology. First-line chemotherapy regimens have evolved over the past three decades. Single-agent melphalan chemotherapy was initially replaced, in the 1970s, by the combination of cyclophosphamide with the anthracycline doxorubicin and, in the 1980s, was replaced by concurrent cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin and cisplatin cyclophosphamide and cisplatin only was also introduced in the 1980s and was found to have comparable efficacy to cisplatin, but with significantly less toxicity. In the early 1990s, was shown to be the most effective agent in patients with relapsed platinum-refractory disease, and this established its role in the treatment of ovarian cancer. Since t

document_index.title: What is cell cycle?
document_index.excerpt: Cell cycle is the process through which a cell is able to effectively divide Cell cycle and antitumor immunity Multiple events in the microenvironment, as DNA damage or deprivation of nutrients can induce senesgence. Senesence is a form of arrest in with not only the cell division is shut down, but also triggers the SASP (senescence-associated secretory phenotype), linked to secretion of multiple characteristic proteins (such as chemokines, cytokines or proteases). Depending on the biological context, senescence could limit or support the tumor progression: SASP may facilitate the proliferative signalling of the surrounding cancer cells Senescence may be just a transitory state, possibly associated with therapy resistance. Since the treatments often target proliferating cells, this form of

document_index.path: README.docx
document_index.name: README.docx
document_index.title: Welcome to the Cell Cycle project!!
document_index.excerpt: Welcome to the Cell Cycle project!! The objective is to study the CellCycle IDS samples, primarily HRP, with some HRD samples included. CycIF experiments were conducted and analyzed for this cohort, enabling the identification of multiple cell phenotypes—immune, stromal, and tumor—based on specific markers. Additionally, functional markers related to the cell cycle, such as cyclins, p21, Ki67, and PCNA, were examined. The study also encompasses tumor meta-clustering and spatial analysis. Furthermore, we are developing a technique to isolate cells using the RareCyte scanner, followed by mass spectrometry analysis (results are available in this folder). Project lead: Ada Junquera Ada Junquera (CycIF and project coordination) Sara Palomino (bulk RNAseq + xenium + multiomics integration) Nika

 I
 Zusammenfassung

Bei hochgradig serösem Ovarialkarzinom handelt es sich um den häufigsten und
tödlichsten Subtyp von Eierstockkrebs. Es zeichnet sich durch eine hohe Hete-
rogenität, genomische Instabilität und beträchtliche Rückfallraten aus. Beson-
ders in fortgeschrittenen Stadien sind herkömmliche Behandlungen langfristig
oft wenig effektiv, was den dringenden Bedarf an neuen, gezielteren Therapie-
ansätzen unterstreicht. Ein grundlegendes Verständnis der Mechanismen, die
der Tumorentwicklung zugrunde liegen, ist dafür unerlässlich, insbesondere im
Hinblick auf das Tumormikroumfeld und den Zellzyklus, die beide entschei-
denden Einfluss auf Tumorprogression und Therapieerfolg haben. Multiplex-
Bildgebungstechniken und räumlich aufgelöste Omics-Methoden haben sich
hierfür als wertvolle Werkzeuge erwiesen. Trotz bedeutender Fortschritte be-
stehen jedoch weiterhin Herausforderungen in Bezug auf Skalierbarkeit, Sensi-
tivität und Komplexität der Arbeitsabläufe, was den Bedarf an methodischer
Weiterentwicklung verdeutlicht.
In dieser Masterarbeit wird mit ProteoPicking eine neue räumlich aufgelöste
Proteomics-Methode entwickelt. Sie kombiniert zyklische Immunfluoreszenz-
färbung mit massenspektrometrischer Proteomics-Analyse und ermöglicht die
Untersuchung von Gewebeproben hinsichtlich ihrer strukturellen, funktionel-
len und molekularen Merkmale. Im Rahmen dieses Projekts wurde die Kom-
patibilität beider Methoden gezeigt. Darauf aufbauend wurde ein mehrstufi-

nalyse und ermöglicht die
Untersuchung von Gewebeproben hinsichtlich ihrer strukturellen, funktionel-
len und molekularen Merkmale. Im Rahmen dieses Projekts wurde die Kom-
patibilität beider Methoden gezeigt. Darauf aufbauend wurde ein mehrstufi-
ger experimenteller Arbeitsablauf zur gezielten Isolierung und Analyse spe-
zifischer Geweberegionen entwickelt und durch die Untersuchung verschiede-
ner Einflussfaktoren wie Gewebemenge, Regionstyp und Probenpufferwahl op-
timiert. Der Workflow umfasst zyklische Immunfluoreszenzfärbung, Proben-
nahme ausgewählter Geweberegionen durch Mikrodissektion mit Mikronadel
und massenspektrometrische Analyse der isolierten Zellproben. Zusätzlich wur-
de eine robuste Datenanalyse-Pipeline etabliert, die Bildverarbeitung, Zell-
phänotypisierung, Analyse der zellulären Probenzusammensetzung, Protein-
expressionsanalyse sowie Korrelationsanalyse zwischen Proteinexpression und
Zellzusammensetzung vereint. Dies ermöglicht die Verknüpfung mikroskopi-
scher Bilddaten mit zellulären Proteomics-Profilen. Insgesamt adressiert diese
Arbeit zentrale technische Herausforderungen und schafft eine Grundlage für
die Weiterentwicklung räumlich aufgelöster Proteomics-Methoden.


 II
 Abstract

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma represents the most common and lethal
subtype of ovarian cancer, characterized by its significant heterogeneity, ge-
nomic instability, and high recurrence rates. Standard treatments are often
limited in their long-term effectiveness, particularly at advanced stages, stress-
ing the need for novel, improved therapies. For this, a thorough understanding
of the biology behind cancer progression is necessary, including key factors like
the tumor microenvironment and cell cycle progression. Over the past years,

ges, stress-
ing the need for novel, improved therapies. For this, a thorough understanding
of the biology behind cancer progression is necessary, including key factors like
the tumor microenvironment and cell cycle progression. Over the past years,
highly multiplexed imaging techniques and spatially resolved omics methods
have become valuable tools for studying complex, heterogeneous tissues, like
tumors and their environments. But despite significant advancements, spa-
tially resolved methodologies still face limitations in scalability, sensitivity, and
workflow complexity, highlighting the ongoing need for further development
and optimization in this field.
This thesis establishes ProteoPicking, a novel spatially resolved proteomics
method that integrates tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence imaging with
mass spectrometry-based proteomics analysis. The approach enables the in-
vestigation of tissue samples with respect to their structural, functional, and
molecular characteristics. As part of this project, the compatibility of both
techniques was demonstrated. Building on this, a multi-step experimental
workflow was developed for the targeted isolation and analysis of specific tissue
regions. It comprises cyclic staining, region selection based on imaging res-
ults, microdissection of regions of interest using a microneedle, and subsequent
mass spectrometry analysis of the collected cells. The process was optimized
by evaluating key factors influencing protein yield, including sample size, tissue
type, and lysis buffer used. In addition, a robust data analysis pipeline was
established, combining image processing, cell phenotyping, compositional ana-
lysis of tissue samples, differential protein expression analysis, and correlation

ze, tissue
type, and lysis buffer used. In addition, a robust data analysis pipeline was
established, combining image processing, cell phenotyping, compositional ana-
lysis of tissue samples, differential protein expression analysis, and correlation
analysis between protein expression levels and sample compositions. This in-
tegrative approach allows for the combined evaluation of microscopic imaging
data and spatially resolved proteome profiles. Altogether, this work addresses
central technical challenges and lays the foundation for the further development
of spatially resolved proteomics methods.

 VII
1 Introduction
1.1 Ovarian cancer
With more than 300.000 new cases and over 200.000 deaths in 2022, ovarian cancer
(OC) is the 18th most common cancer worldwide and the 8th most common one for
women (World Cancer Research Fund International, 2024).
OC is a histologically heterogeneous disease (Kurman & Shih, 2016; Scully et al.,
1998). It can be classified into subtypes with different cells of origin, molecular com-
positions, risk factors, clinical features, and treatment approaches. Approximately
90 % of emerging OC is identified to arise from epithelial cells, germ cell tumors ac-
count for another 5 % of cases, and the remaining 5 % are sex cord-stromal tumors
(Doubeni et al., 2016; Matulonis et al., 2016). Epithelial OC can further be divided
into five histological subtypes, namely high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC),
low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, mucinous carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and
endometrioid carcinoma (Lheureux et al., 2019). The latter four are type I tumors
according to the dualistic model of epithelial ovarian carcinogenesis. They are low-
grade, slowly proliferating neoplasms that are mostly genetically stable and differ in
their distinct molecular changes from type II tumors. Type II tumors are genetically
unstable, aggressive, high-grade neoplasms. HGSC makes up the majority of this
type (Shih & Kurman, 2004).

1.1.1 High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma

HGSC is the most common and lethal subtype of OC, accounting for 75 % of all
epithelial OC cases and 70 % of all OC related deaths (Lheureux et al., 2019; Peres
et al., 2019). Most cases originate from the epithelium of the distal fallopian tube
and not, as long assumed, from the ovarian surface epithelium (Labidi-Galy et al.,

ithelial OC cases and 70 % of all OC related deaths (Lheureux et al., 2019; Peres
et al., 2019). Most cases originate from the epithelium of the distal fallopian tube
and not, as long assumed, from the ovarian surface epithelium (Labidi-Galy et al.,
2017). It grows in a heterogeneous way, typically as a solid mass of cells with slit-
like fenestrations and frequent necrotic areas. Its structure is similar to the surface
epithelium of the fallopian tube with glandular, cribriform, or papillary architecture.
The cells usually display a high grade of nuclear atypia and have a high mitotic index,
which indicates high proliferation (Kurman et al., 2014).
HGSC is diagnosed among all ages, but the most frequently affected are women aged
55 to 74. The 5-year relative survival rate amounts to 50 %. However, it must be
taken into account that this rate greatly differs between different stages of HGSC.
There are four stages of OC based on the progress of the disease and defined by the
international federation of gynecology and obstetrics (FIGO). In early stages, the
tumor is limited to the ovaries (FIGO stage I) or involves one or both ovaries and

 1
has spread within the pelvic region or originates as primary peritoneal cancer (FIGO
stage II). More advanced stages are defined by an additional spread of the tumor
beyond the pelvis to the peritoneum and/or the retroperitoneal lymph nodes (FIGO
stage III) or even a spread to more distant sites in the body (FIGO stage IV)(Prat,
on Gynecologic Oncology et al., 2014). While over 90 % of women with cancer in early
stages survive more than five years after their diagnoses, the survival rate for distant
stage OC, FIGO stage IV, is only around 30 %. Moreover, the majority of OC cases

necologic Oncology et al., 2014). While over 90 % of women with cancer in early
stages survive more than five years after their diagnoses, the survival rate for distant
stage OC, FIGO stage IV, is only around 30 %. Moreover, the majority of OC cases
is diagnosed at advanced stages, FIGO stages III and IV (National Cancer Institute,
Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results Program, 2024). This is explained by
the fact that there is no effective screening strategy for the early detection of OC
and early stages of this disease are often asymptomatic. If there are any symptoms
they are usually non-specific for this type of cancer. Possible symptoms include back
pain, fatigue, abdominal pain, abdominal bloating, constipation, or urinary symp-
toms (Goff et al., 2004; Matulonis et al., 2016).

HGSC is a type II epithelial ovarian carcinoma. It displays a high degree of genetic
instability with two characterizing aspects - whole genome duplications and high-
frequency somatic copy number alterations. Additionally, genetic mutations in TP53
typically occur. Beside this, there are only a few other recurrent genetic mutations in
HGSC. A study conducted by the Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network in 2011
(Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network and others, 2011) showed that in more
than 96 % of the analyzed samples a TP53 mutation was present. Further recur-
rent mutations, even though less common than TP53, were germline and somatic
BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations in 22 % of the samples and mutations in RB1, NF1,
FAT3, CSMD3, GABRA6, or CDK12 in less than 10 %. Moreover, the study revealed
that in more than half of the analyzed HGSC samples the homologous recombination
(HR) pathway of DNA repair was defective. This can be associated with germline
and somatic BRCA mutations.

BRA6, or CDK12 in less than 10 %. Moreover, the study revealed
that in more than half of the analyzed HGSC samples the homologous recombination
(HR) pathway of DNA repair was defective. This can be associated with germline
and somatic BRCA mutations. HR repair is a key mechanism for HGSC to fix DNA
double-strand breaks (Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network and others, 2011).
It uses homologous DNA sequences as a template to accurately recreate the former
sequence and fix the double-strand break. Such breaks can derive from exogenous
anticancer treatments like topoisomerase inhibition, platinum-based drugs, or ioniz-
ing radiation and if they are not correctly repaired they can lead to mutations, cell
cycle arrest, or apoptosis. Besides HR, there are other pathways in cells that can be
alternatively used to repair DNA double-strand breaks. One example would be non-
homologous end joining (NHEJ). NHEJ is the direct ligation of blunt, complementary,
and non-complementary ends after certain enzymatic modifications without a homo-


 2
logous sequence as a template. During this process small scale mutations at the repair
site can occur, which is why NHEJ is more error-prone than HR (Pfeiffer et al., 2000).
Hence, a HR deficiency, which hinders cells from repairing DNA damage induced by
cancer therapy, strongly influences the treatment sensitivity of HGSC. Other com-
monly altered pathways are RB1 (67 %), RAS/PI3K (45 %), FOXM1 (87 %), and
NOTCH (22 %)(Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network and others, 2011).

1.1.2 Clinical treatment

For the primary treatment of HGSC after diagnosis there are two different approaches
- primary debulking surgery (PDS) with adjuvant chemotherapy or neoadjuvant
chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) (National Com-

eatment

For the primary treatment of HGSC after diagnosis there are two different approaches
- primary debulking surgery (PDS) with adjuvant chemotherapy or neoadjuvant
chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) (National Com-
prehensive Cancer Network, 2024). The treatment strategy is determined by the stage
of cancer, the resectability of the tumor, and patient variables such as the patient’s
age or the general health condition (Fagotti et al., 2020; Van Meurs et al., 2013; I.
Vergote et al., 2018).
If the patient’s tumor is deemed resectable, that means complete cytoreduction can
be achieved, and the patient is in suitable condition for surgery, primary debulking
surgery (PDS) is the preferred approach (I. B. Vergote et al., 2016). Afterwards,
adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended for all stages of HGSC (National Compre-
hensive Cancer Network, 2024). In practice, six cycles of platinum-based adjuvant
therapy are administered. The preferred regimen is intravenous administration of
a combination of carboplatin with paclitaxel every three weeks. In cases with high
risk of progression (FIGO stage III patients with R2 resection and inoperable FIGO
stage III and IV patients) bevacizumab is sometimes added to the therapy with car-
boplatin and paclitaxel and afterwards used for maintenance therapy (Burger et al.,
2011; National Comprehensive Cancer Network, 2024; Perren et al., 2011).
When patients are too ill for surgery or when their cancer burden is too extensive for
a macroscopic complete resection, typically in advanced disease stages (FIGO stages
III and IV), NACT and IDS are recommended instead of PDS. By this, the cancer
burden is reduced before surgery is performed (Gill et al., 2017). A combination of

for
a macroscopic complete resection, typically in advanced disease stages (FIGO stages
III and IV), NACT and IDS are recommended instead of PDS. By this, the cancer
burden is reduced before surgery is performed (Gill et al., 2017). A combination of
carboplatin and paclitaxel is administered for three cycles before IDS is performed.
Afterwards, six cycles of post-operative chemotherapy are recommended (National
Comprehensive Cancer Network, 2024).
Both surgical approaches aim for complete cytoreduction by removing the complete
disseminated tumor mass from the peritoneal cavity of the patient. Successful resec-
tion occurs when after surgery there is no macroscopic residual of the diseased tissue
left and is classified as complete resection (R0 resection). However, in practice often

 3
some tissue parts remain and the resection is classified according to the postoperative
amount of tumor. When the diameter of the residual cancer is less than 1 cm, it is an
optimal resection (R1 resection). Otherwise, it counts as a suboptimal resection (R2
resection)(Matulonis et al., 2016). The achieved level of resection after PDS or IDS
is the most important prognostic factor concerning patient survival (Du Bois et al.,
2009).

The majority of patients initially has a favorable response to platinum- and taxane-
based chemotherapies, as shown in several clinical studies (McGuire et al., 1996; Ozols
et al., 2003; Piccart et al., 2000). However, recurrence rates remain high, affecting
about 75 % of patients, with 23 % experiencing relapse within six months following
the completion of primary therapy (Du Bois et al., 2009). This demonstrates how
treatment remains challenging with standard approaches being ineffective in most
cases in the long term, particularly at advanced stages.

apse within six months following
the completion of primary therapy (Du Bois et al., 2009). This demonstrates how
treatment remains challenging with standard approaches being ineffective in most
cases in the long term, particularly at advanced stages. Consequently, there is an
urgent need for investigating novel, improved therapy strategies for HGSC.

In recent years, alternative treatment approaches such as targeted therapy and im-
munotherapy have been and continue to be increasingly investigated (Wilson et al.,
2024).
One promising group of drugs are so called PARP inhibitors, small-molecular inhib-
itors that target poly(ADP-Ribose)polymerase (PARP). PARP is especially vital for
cancer cells with defective DNA repair mechanisms, for example HR deficiencies in
HGSC patients due to mutations in BRCA1/2 or other genes associated with HR
deficiency like several RAD or FA genes. Those cells heavily rely on PARP medi-
ated base excision repair of single-strand breaks to deal with DNA damage. Hence,
inhibition of PARP inactivates DNA repair, resulting in synthetic lethality and anti-
tumor activity (Ashworth et al., 2011; Cancer Genome Atlas Research Network and
others, 2011; Eustermann et al., 2015; Farmer et al., 2005). Based on this mechanism
the first PARP inhibitor finding application in clinics was olaparib. It is used as a
treatment for patients with recurrent cancer who have initially responded to prior
platinum-based chemotherapy and for newly diagnosed patients with advanced-stage
HR-deficient tumors who have experienced tumor reduction or clearance following
platinum-based chemotherapy (European Medicines Agency, 2025). The effect of
olaparib differs in patients as demonstrated in a phase II clinical trial with a diverse
patient cohort (Gelmon et al., 2011).

rienced tumor reduction or clearance following
platinum-based chemotherapy (European Medicines Agency, 2025). The effect of
olaparib differs in patients as demonstrated in a phase II clinical trial with a diverse
patient cohort (Gelmon et al., 2011). Of the 63 enrolled ovarian cancer patients espe-
cially patients with BRCA mutations exhibited impressive partial responses, but also
a proportion of patients with BRCA wildtype displayed significant responses to the


 4
treatment with olaparib. A second factor that influences the response to olaparib is
the platinum resistance status of patients. The results of the trial demonstrated that
while the response rate in platinum sensitive patients was around 50 % for BRCA
wildtype patients and 60 % for BRCA mutation patients, it was significantly lower
in platinum resistant patients with around 5 % respectively 30 %. Other examples of
PARP inhibitors that are used for treating HGSC are rucaparib and niraparib (Mirza
et al., 2016; Syed, 2017).
Another routinely used targeted therapy uses anti-angiogenic agents to interfere with
the development of new blood vessels within the tumor. This process, called an-
giogenesis, is essential for tumor growth, prevents hypoxia and necrosis, and pro-
motes invasion, metastasis, and ascites. By inhibition of angiogenesis tumor growth
is restrained (Hanahan & Weinberg, 2000). One example for such an agent is the
humanized monoclonal antibody bevacizumab. It targets the cytokine VEGF-A, an
important growth factor responsible for the recruitment of blood vessels during an-
giogenesis (Burger, 2011). Bevacizumab, as already mentioned above, sometimes is
added to the standard carboplatin-paclitaxel regimen and used as maintenance ther-

n
important growth factor responsible for the recruitment of blood vessels during an-
giogenesis (Burger, 2011). Bevacizumab, as already mentioned above, sometimes is
added to the standard carboplatin-paclitaxel regimen and used as maintenance ther-
apy to increase progression free survival (PFS) as shown in the ICON7 and GOG218
clinical trials (Burger et al., 2011; Oza et al., 2015). Apart from bevacizumab, there
are also other anti-angiogenic agents such as pazopanib, nintedanib, or cediranib (Du
Bois et al., 2013; Ledermann et al., 2013).

Also several immunotherapeutic approaches are actively investigated (Wilson et al.,
2024). HGSC is an immunogenic tumor, that means it can cause an immune response,
and there are implications that patients could be responsive to immunotherapy. The
patients immune system is able to identify the tumor, for example through the re-
cognition of autologous tumor-associated antigens by T cells (L. Zhang et al., 2003).
Despite this, previous clinical trials on various immunotherapeutic agents have largely
demonstrated limited response rates (Lheureux et al., 2019).
One investigated type of immunotherapies are immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs).
ICIs are a class of drugs designed to enhance the immune system’s ability to target
cancer cells by blocking specific inhibitory pathways known as checkpoints. Those
checkpoints regulate immune responses, prevent excessive immune activation, and
protect against autoimmunity. However, tumor cells can exploit these mechanisms
to evade immune detection. By inhibiting immune checkpoints, the immune sys-
tem activity against cancer cells can be restored. Two examples for immune check-
points are the T cell inhibitory receptors cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4

sms
to evade immune detection. By inhibiting immune checkpoints, the immune sys-
tem activity against cancer cells can be restored. Two examples for immune check-
points are the T cell inhibitory receptors cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4
(CTLA-4) and programd cell death protein-1 (PD-1) receptor. CTLA-4 downregu-


 5
lates the activation of T cells, while PD-1, upon binding to its ligands PD-L1/2 on
tumor cells, suppresses immune activity, allowing tumors to evade detection (Pardoll,
2012). ICIs, including ipilimumab (National Cancer Institute (NCI), 2020), which
targets CTLA-4 as well as nivolumab (Hamanishi et al., 2015) and pembrolizumab
(Matulonis et al., 2019), both targeting PD-1, have been evaluated in clinical trials
for advanced recurrent ovarian cancer. The outcomes of these studies indicate modest
response rates. Nivolumab achieved an objective response rate (ORR) of 15 %, while
pembrolizumab demonstrated a lower ORR of 8 %. Similarly, the trial investigating
ipilimumab reported an ORR of approximately 10 %. These results suggest that while
checkpoint inhibitors show some clinical activity, their efficacy as therapy in recurrent
ovarian cancer remains limited.

Overall, the challenges in effectively treating HGSC highlight the critical need for
further research in this field. Advancing our understanding of HGSC’s mechanisms
and molecular characteristics is essential for identifying new biomarkers as potential
therapeutic targets, and developing new, more effective therapies capable of address-
ing advanced disease, therapy resistance, and tumor recurrence.


1.2 Tumor microenvironment

namic ecosystem, consisting of a wide variety of tumor, stroma, and immune cells,

1.2 Tumor microenvironment
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a highly heterogeneous, complex, and dy-
namic ecosystem, consisting of a wide variety of tumor, stroma, and immune cells,
along with various regulatory signaling factors such as chemokines, cytokines, and
growth factors. It evolves together with the tumor, contributing to tumor hetero-
geneity by fostering the development of diverse cancer cell clones. The TME plays
a crucial role in tumor survival, progression, metastasis, and immune evasion, either
promoting or suppressing the immune response through various cellular interactions
(Hanahan & Weinberg, 2011; Jia et al., 2022). Additionally, it can significantly influ-
ence a patient’s response to treatment (Wu & Dai, 2017). Due to its heavy influence
on tumor development, the TME is an interesting and important target in the invest-
igation of novel, effective treatment strategies.

Within the TME, stroma cells such as cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), an-
giogenic vascular cells, endothelial cells, and pericytes are integral for tumor pro-
gression. They do not only provide structural support, but also contribute to the
promotion of several hallmarks of cancer (Hanahan & Weinberg, 2011). This is ac-
complished through their secretion of signaling factors, extracellular matrix (ECM)
components, and growth factors, which influence the TME. In doing so, they help fa-

 6
cilitate immune evasion, disrupt growth suppression, enable invasion and metastasis,
and alter the metabolic pathways of tumor cells (Hanahan & Coussens, 2012).

The immune cells in the TME are composed of cells of the innate immune system,

fa-

 6
cilitate immune evasion, disrupt growth suppression, enable invasion and metastasis,
and alter the metabolic pathways of tumor cells (Hanahan & Coussens, 2012).

The immune cells in the TME are composed of cells of the innate immune system,
like dendritic cells (DCs), natural killer cells (NK cells), macrophages, and myeloid-
derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) as well as the adaptive immune system, including
CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, regulatory T cells (Tregs), and B cells. By displaying
their different cellular functions and interacting with each other and other parts of
the TME they either contribute to a pro-tumor activity by supporting tumor growth
and immune evasion, or an anti-tumor immune response (Giraldo et al., 2019).
DCs capture and present tumor-associated antigens to T cells, triggering an immune
response. NK cells are essential for identifying and directly killing tumor cells through
their cytotoxic activity, recognizing cells that display stress markers or altered self-
antigens. CD4+ T helper cells activate other immune cells, including CD8+ cytotoxic
T cells, which directly target and kill tumor cells, or B cells, which contribute to the
immune defense by producing antibodies that bind to tumor antigens, aiding in the
recognition and elimination of cancer cells.
In contrast, Tregs play a pivotal role in maintaining immune tolerance by suppress-
ing the activity of other immune cells, thus allowing tumors to evade detection and
destruction. MDSCs inhibit T cell activity and promote an immunosuppressive en-
vironment beneficial to tumor growth. Lastly, M2 macrophages also promote tumor
progression by supporting tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, and immune suppression
through the secretion of anti-inflammatory cytokines (Giraldo et al., 2019;

pressive en-
vironment beneficial to tumor growth. Lastly, M2 macrophages also promote tumor
progression by supporting tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, and immune suppression
through the secretion of anti-inflammatory cytokines (Giraldo et al., 2019; Hanahan
& Weinberg, 2011).

Given the central role these diverse cell populations play in shaping the TME, it is
essential to understand their individual functions and interactions to develop more
targeted and effective therapies for tumor treatment.

1.2.1 Key cell populations within the TME

Cancer-associated fibroblasts

CAFs are fibroblasts within the stroma region of the TME. They primarily differen-
tiate from mesenchymal-derived cells, but can also transform from other cell types.
They produce various proteins, including most components of the extracellular mat-
rix (ECM) like collagens, fibronectin, and laminin as well as ECM regulating proteins
(Kalluri & Zeisberg, 2006). CAFs support tumor progression via several mechanisms,

 7
for example by expressing chemokine CXCL14, which plays an important role in pro-
moting tumor growth, and the expression of the migration and invasion enhancing
fibroblast activation protein α (Sjöberg et al., 2016; W. Yang et al., 2013). Moreover,
they support the infiltration of Tregs into the TME, which leads to immune inhibition,
and release angiogenesis promoting molecules (Givel et al., 2018).
Various studies suggest that CAFs in OC can serve as biomarkers for poor prognosis
(Givel et al., 2018; W. Yang et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2017). One study conducted
in HGSC showed that a high accumulation of a specific CAF subtype, CAF-S1, is a
hallmark of a dismal prognosis (Givel et al., 2018). Furthermore, CAFs contribute

osis
(Givel et al., 2018; W. Yang et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2017). One study conducted
in HGSC showed that a high accumulation of a specific CAF subtype, CAF-S1, is a
hallmark of a dismal prognosis (Givel et al., 2018). Furthermore, CAFs contribute
to increased resistance to platinum-based treatments and expedite tumor recurrence
by expressing fibroblast activation protein α. The overexpression of this protein in
the tumor stroma is one of the hallmarks of platinum resistance and predicts shorter
recurrence in patients, as shown in a study which analyzed over 300 epithelial OC
sample (Mhawech-Fauceglia et al., 2015). All in all, CAFs play a critical role in OC
progression, immune evasion, and treatment resistance with their presence and sub-
types serving as key prognostic indicators of poor outcomes.

Tumor-associated macrophages

Macrophages are innate immune cells involved in inflammation and tumorigenesis. In
the TME, they are referred to as tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), which can
either be tissue-resident macrophages or infiltrating macrophages derived from mono-
cytes originating in the bone marrow. In general, there are two main phenotypes of
TAMs: M1 and M2 macrophages. However, these phenotypes represent the extremes
of a continuous spectrum, with TAM subpopulations displaying varying degrees of M1
or M2 characteristics, or even sharing features of both. M1-like macrophages develop
from monocytes stimulated with interferon-gamma IFN-γ, granulocyte-macrophage-
colony-stimulating factor, or bacterial lipopolysaccharide. They possess anti-tumor
functions, including cytotoxicity, tumor-suppression, and immune-stimulation. In
contrast, M2-like macrophages differentiate from monocytes stimulated with tumor
expressed cytokines, like IL-4, IL-10, or IL-13.

polysaccharide. They possess anti-tumor
functions, including cytotoxicity, tumor-suppression, and immune-stimulation. In
contrast, M2-like macrophages differentiate from monocytes stimulated with tumor
expressed cytokines, like IL-4, IL-10, or IL-13. They are pro-tumor macrophages (Gal-
diero et al., 2013; Grivennikov et al., 2010; Sica et al., 2008). Through remodeling
and regulation of the ECM by degrading ECM components, M2 macrophages facilit-
ate tumor migration, invasion, and metastasis (Finkernagel et al., 2016). Moreover,
they contribute to immune evasion and angiogenesis via the expression of various
immunosuppressive and pro-angiogenic cytokines as well as VEGF-A, an important
angiogenic factor (Sica et al., 2008).


 8
In OC, TAMs of mixed-polarization are present (Reinartz et al., 2014). As tumor
and TME evolve, changes in TAM infiltration and polarization occur. Studies have
shown that higher TAM and M2 macrophage density correlates with more advanced
cancer stages and poorer prognosis. Additionally, M1/M2 ratio has been identified
as a prognostic factor for PFS and overall survival(OS) in epithelial OC, with higher
ratios associated with extended survival (M. Zhang et al., 2014). In conclusion, TAMs
and particularly their polarization states play a crucial role in shaping the TME of
OC, influencing tumor progression, immune evasion, and patient prognosis.

Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes

Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) are a major component of the TME and com-
prise of B, T, and innate lymphoid cells. Among these, T lymphocytes are the main
cell type acting in adaptive cancer immunity.
T cells develop in the thymus to naive T cells. Only upon encountering antigens,
the naive cells are activated and start differentiating, proliferating, and maturing.

ng these, T lymphocytes are the main
cell type acting in adaptive cancer immunity.
T cells develop in the thymus to naive T cells. Only upon encountering antigens,
the naive cells are activated and start differentiating, proliferating, and maturing.
Depending on the cytokine environment, they differentiate into various subtypes with
different functions within the TME. Mature T lymphocytes can be categorized into
three main types: CD4+ T helper cells (Th cells), CD8+ T cytotoxic cells (also
cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs)), and Tregs.
Th cells regulate immune responses by releasing cytokines that can either promote or
inhibit inflammation. Based on this, they are classified into pro-inflammatory Th1
cells, which help CTLs in their anti-tumor activity, or anti-inflammatory Th2 cells,
which promote tumor progression. CTLs exhibit tumor-suppressive activity. Like
Th1 cells, they secrete inflammatory cytokines, but in addition they express cell lytic
molecules like perforin and granzymes to destroy infected or malignant cells after
recognizing them. Tregs are cells whose primary function is to prevent autoimmune
diseases by inhibiting self-reactive and auto-reactive cells. But within the TME they
act as immunosuppressors, promoting tumorigenesis through mechanisms such as
the secretion of immunosuppressive molecules, cytolysis of effector cells, metabolic
disruption, and modulation of dendritic cell maturation (Chen & Mellman, 2013; Y.
Yang et al., 2020).
In OC, the distribution and localization of TILs play a crucial role in determining
clinical outcomes. A study analyzing over 180 tissue samples from advanced OC
stages found that patients with T cell-infiltrated tumors had a higher 5-year OS rate
compared to those with no T cell infiltration.

TILs play a crucial role in determining
clinical outcomes. A study analyzing over 180 tissue samples from advanced OC
stages found that patients with T cell-infiltrated tumors had a higher 5-year OS rate
compared to those with no T cell infiltration. This study also suggested a significant
association between intratumoral T cells and delayed relapse (L. Zhang et al., 2003).


 9
Several studies support these findings, showing that patients with CTL-infiltrated
tumors experienced extended PFS and OS (Clarke et al., 2009; Hamanishi et al.,
2007; Sato et al., 2005). In contrast, high Treg infiltration was associated with poor
clinical outcomes, as demonstrated by Curiel et al. (Curiel et al., 2004). Cancer
immunity phenotypes are typically categorized into three major types based on the
extent of TIL presence: immune-deserted, immune-excluded, and inflamed tumors
(Chen & Mellman, 2013). While immune-deserted tumors lack TILs, and immune-
excluded tumors only feature immune cells at the stroma-epithelial interface but not
within the tumor, inflamed tumors display significant TIL infiltration. These immune
profiles have also been observed in HGSC, where distinct immune compositions have
been identified (Kandalaft et al., 2022).
All in all, TILs, particularly their distribution and polarization within the TME,
play a crucial role in shaping ovarian cancer progression and patient prognosis, with
immune profiles offering valuable insights into disease outcomes.

1.2.2 TME in ovarian cancer

In line with the unique heterogeneity of HGSC, the TME of this cancer type is
also highly diverse and intrinsically heterogeneous (Jiménez-Sánchez et al., 2020).
Moreover, it can be dynamically modulated by patient treatment such as chemo-
therapy (Khairallah et al., 2018).

terogeneity of HGSC, the TME of this cancer type is
also highly diverse and intrinsically heterogeneous (Jiménez-Sánchez et al., 2020).
Moreover, it can be dynamically modulated by patient treatment such as chemo-
therapy (Khairallah et al., 2018). While commonly used chemotherapy strategies
induce response-specific changes within the TME, the precise mechanisms driving
these alterations are still not completely understood. Although chemotherapies do
not induce immunogenic cell death, they still promote inflammation and anti-tumor
responses through various pro-inflammatory processes. These include an increase
in MHC I expression on antigen-presenting cells, depletion of Tregs, polarization of
TAMs toward the M1 phenotype, and enhanced tumor permeability, which facilitates
the infiltration of TILs (Böhm et al., 2016).
A study conducted by Jiménez-Sánchez et al. investigated the TME in metastatic
HGSC before and after NACT. Their findings revealed the coexistence of immune-
suppressive, immune cell-excluded regions alongside immuno-activated, inflammatory
regions within the same tumor site of treatment-naive HGSC. Furthermore, the study
demonstrated that NACT led to increased immune cell infiltration, enhancing the tu-
mor’s immunogenicity, and indicating that chemotherapy locally activates the immune
system (Jiménez-Sánchez et al., 2020).
Interestingly, the post-NACT immune landscape and its balance between pro-tumor
and anti-tumor immune cell populations have been shown to correlate with patient
outcomes. Specifically, the presence of stromal TILs offers the most predictive insights

 10
(Leary et al., 2021; Mesnage et al., 2017). These findings underscore the critical role
that immune cell composition plays in predicting treatment response and survival.

While significant progress has been made, our understanding of the interplay between
treatment and immune cell populations remains far from complete. Continued re-
search is essential to fully unravel the complex dynamics of the TME and its impact
on tumor behavior and treatment response.


1.3 Cell cycle
The cell cycle is a complex and tightly regulated series of events that result in cell
growth, the duplication of genetic information, and the subsequent distribution of
the duplicated genome between two daughter cells. This process is crucial for prolif-
eration, maintaining genomic integrity, and ensuring accurate cell division (Israels &
Israels, 2000; Williams & Stoeber, 2012).
Generally, it can be divided into four distinct phases: gap 1 phase (G1), synthesis
phase (S phase), gap 2 phase (G2), and mitosis phase (M phase). During G1, the
cell grows in size and prepares for the following phase by expressing genes that are
necessary for DNA replication. In S phase, the cell duplicates its DNA. In G2, the
cell continues to grow and synthesize components essential for cell division. At this
stage, the cell also performs a final check to ensure that DNA replication is complete
and error-free. Finally, M phase involves the separation of the replicated genome into
two equal parts, followed by cytokinesis, which divides the cytoplasm and completes
the formation of two daughter cells (Israels & Israels, 2000; Ligasová et al., 2023).

The progression through these phases must be tightly regulated to ensure proper
cell division.

followed by cytokinesis, which divides the cytoplasm and completes
the formation of two daughter cells (Israels & Israels, 2000; Ligasová et al., 2023).

The progression through these phases must be tightly regulated to ensure proper
cell division. Key regulators of the cell cycle are cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs),
cyclins, and CDK inhibitors. These molecules work together to control cell cycle pro-
gression (Ligasová et al., 2023; Williams & Stoeber, 2012). CDKs and cyclins form
complexes, in which CDKs serve as the catalytic unit, while cyclins act as the reg-
ulatory unit. Upon activation through phosphorylation, these complexes themselves
phosphorylate specific target proteins, driving the cell cycle forward. Different cyclin-
CDK pairs are active at distinct stages of the cell cycle, as the expression levels of
cyclins vary during different phases - cyclin levels increase or decrease to regulate the
timing of cell cycle transitions (Israels & Israels, 2000). Additionally, CDK activity is
controlled by CDK inhibitors, which can bind to and inhibit CDK-cyclin complexes,
ensuring the cell cycle progresses only under appropriate conditions (Besson et al.,
2008).

 11
Alongside the regulation provided by cyclin-CDK complexes, the cell cycle is further
controlled by cell cycle checkpoints. These checkpoints serve as regulatory mechan-
isms that ensure the cycle halts if DNA is damaged or if cells fail to meet necessary
conditions for division. Through this, it is guaranteed that only healthy, fully pre-
pared cells proceed through each phase, preventing the transmission of mutations to
daughter cells (Ligasová et al., 2023; Williams & Stoeber, 2012).
One of the most important checkpoints in the cell cycle is the restriction point in G1,

fully pre-
pared cells proceed through each phase, preventing the transmission of mutations to
daughter cells (Ligasová et al., 2023; Williams & Stoeber, 2012).
One of the most important checkpoints in the cell cycle is the restriction point in G1,
which is regulated by the retinoblastoma (Rb) protein. Rb functions as a gatekeeper,
controlling the progression from G1 to S phase. In its unphosphorylated form, Rb
tightly binds to the E2F transcription factor, inhibiting its activity and preventing the
transcription of genes required for DNA replication. If conditions are favorable, such
as in the absence of genomic damage, Rb is phosphorylated by cyclin D-CDK4/6
complexes, causing it to dissociate from E2F. This release allows E2F to activate
the transcription of genes necessary for DNA synthesis, facilitating progression past
the restriction point into S phase. In the presence of genomic damage, however,
Rb remains unphosphorylated, preventing the activation of E2F and thereby leading
to a delay or abandonment of the cell cycle to allow for DNA repair or apoptosis.
Notably, Rb remains phosphorylated throughout S, G2, and M phase, and is only
dephosphorylated upon completion of mitosis, ensuring that the cell cycle does not
proceed prematurely (Henley & Dick, 2012; Israels & Israels, 2000).
Another critical checkpoint in the cell cycle is regulated by the tumor suppressor
protein p53. It is a transcription factor that continuously monitors the cellular genome
integrity. In response to DNA damage, it can initiate cell cycle arrest, facilitate DNA
repair, or in cases of severe damage promote apoptosis. Thereby, it helps to maintain
genomic stability. When DNA damage is detected, p53 binds to sequence-specific
DNA sites.

response to DNA damage, it can initiate cell cycle arrest, facilitate DNA
repair, or in cases of severe damage promote apoptosis. Thereby, it helps to maintain
genomic stability. When DNA damage is detected, p53 binds to sequence-specific
DNA sites. This induces an increased p53 protein synthesis and a subsequent activa-
tion of p53 via phosphorylation. Activated p53 controls the cell cycle by upregulating
the expression of p21, a CDK inhibitor that specifically targets CDK 2, 4, and 6. In-
hibition of these prevents the phosphorylation of Rb and subsequently halts the cell
cycle progression. The cell remains in G1, giving the cell time to repair the damaged
DNA. If the damage is irreparable, p53 directs the cell towards cell death by upreg-
ulating pro-apoptotic proteins (Engeland, 2022; Israels & Israels, 2000).

As outlined, cyclin-CDK complexes and cell cycle checkpoints are essential in regulat-
ing the cell cycle, maintaining genomic integrity, and ensuring accurate cell division.
Disruptions in these regulatory mechanisms can lead to uncontrolled cell prolifera-


 12
tion, which is a hallmark of cancer (Williams & Stoeber, 2012). This underlines the
importance of understanding these processes, as they not only play a central role in
normal cell development but also in tumorigenesis.

1.3.1 Out-of-cycle states

In addition to active cell cycle phases, there are also so-called out-of-cycle states.
Those are distinct non-dividing, non-proliferative stages that play important roles
for the cell in both physiology and disease. There are quiescence, senescence, and
dormancy, which represent different modes of cell cycle arrest characterized by vary-
ing triggers, reversibility, and biological functions. While all involve stopping prolif-

ell in both physiology and disease. There are quiescence, senescence, and
dormancy, which represent different modes of cell cycle arrest characterized by vary-
ing triggers, reversibility, and biological functions. While all involve stopping prolif-
eration, the molecular mechanisms and implications of each state differ.

Quiescence (G0 phase) is a non-proliferative state, in which cells reversibly exit the
cell cycle in response to specific environmental conditions such as nutrient depletion,
growth factor deprivation, or increased cell density during early G1. This revers-
ible arrest allows cells to conserve resources and maintain tissue homeostasis under
unfavorable conditions. Upon cellular exposure to mitogenic and growth signaling,
G0 can be easily reversed and cells enter the cell cycle at G1, resuming proliferation
(Coller et al., 2006; Israels & Israels, 2000). Quiescent cells typically exhibit low
metabolic activity and suppressed expression of proliferation markers but retain the
capacity for rapid activation. In the context of cancer, quiescence may enable tumor
cells to survive therapies targeting dividing cells, contributing to disease persistence
and relapse (Valcourt et al., 2012).
In contrast, cellular senescence is a distinct, non-proliferative, out-of-cycle state that is
characterized by the irreversible arrest of the cell cycle. Unlike quiescence, senescence
represents a permanent interruption of cell division. Senescent cells cannot re-enter
the cell cycle, even in the presence of mitogenic stimuli. It occurs in diploid cells
as an end of proliferation and acts as a protective mechanism in response to various
cellular stresses such as DNA damage, telomere shortening, oncogene activation, or
oxidative stress.

in the presence of mitogenic stimuli. It occurs in diploid cells
as an end of proliferation and acts as a protective mechanism in response to various
cellular stresses such as DNA damage, telomere shortening, oncogene activation, or
oxidative stress. Senescence ensures that cells with significant damage or potential
to become harmful do not continue to divide, thus safeguarding the organism from
compromised cell function (Calcinotto et al., 2019; Williams & Stoeber, 2012).
Lastly, dormancy describes a prolonged, reversible, non-proliferative state of cancer
cells that allows them to survive undetected for years after removal of the primary
tumor (Endo & Inoue, 2019). In general, dormancy can be classified into two forms:
tumor mass dormancy, where proliferation and cell death are balanced by factors like
limited blood supply and immune surveillance, and cellular dormancy, characterized

 13
by individual cancer cells entering a quiescent-like state with cell cycle arrest and
stress tolerance. Dormant cells show resistance to therapy and reduced metabolism,
enabling them to evade treatment and later cause relapse. Although dormancy shares
some features with senescence, it is distinct in being reversible and metabolically sub-
dued, whereas senescence is typically irreversible and metabolically active (Min & Lee,
2023).

Understanding these distinct out-of-cycle states is critical for comprehending how
cells regulate proliferation under normal and pathological conditions. While quies-
cence allows cells to temporarily pause proliferation in response to environmental
cues, senescence represents a permanent arrest triggered by stress signals to prevent
propagation of damaged DNA. Dormancy occupies an intermediate role, particularly

cence allows cells to temporarily pause proliferation in response to environmental
cues, senescence represents a permanent arrest triggered by stress signals to prevent
propagation of damaged DNA. Dormancy occupies an intermediate role, particularly
relevant in cancer, where reversible arrest facilitates long-term survival of dissemin-
ated tumor cells. Together, these states reveal complex cellular strategies to balance
growth, repair, and survival, and underline their importance in research.

1.3.2 Cell cycle and tumorigenesis

As discussed, the cell cycle is a tightly regulated process, facilitating cell division and
growth while ensuring genomic stability. However, in cancer this controlled system
becomes disrupted, leading to dysregulated proliferation, genomic instability, and
tumorigenesis (Glaviano et al., 2025).
Cell cycle dysregulation is a multilayered process involving genetic mutations, aberrant
cyclin-CDK complexes, and loss of critical checkpoints. Oncogene mutations typically
drive the cell cycle forward and therefore increase cell division rates either by directly
enhancing the activity of cyclin-CDK complexes or activating downstream pathways
that subsequently stimulate cyclin and CDK expression. For instance, mutations
in MYC or CCND1, the gene that encodes cyclin D1, lead to the overexpression
of cyclins, especially cyclin D1, and CDKs. Similarly, mutations in various RAS
genes activate cyclin and CDK activating signaling pathways such as MAPK/ERK
and PI3K/AKT, which in turn stimulate cyclins and CDKs. The overexpression and
increased activity of cyclin-CDK complexes accelerate progression through the cell
cycle, allowing cells to bypass critical checkpoints, ultimately driving uncontrolled
proliferation.

, which in turn stimulate cyclins and CDKs. The overexpression and
increased activity of cyclin-CDK complexes accelerate progression through the cell
cycle, allowing cells to bypass critical checkpoints, ultimately driving uncontrolled
proliferation. Mutations in tumor suppressor genes further compromise these check-
points, removing the critical brakes on cell division, and impairing processes like
cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Two examples of key tumor suppressors involved in
cell cycle regulation that are often mutated in various cancers are p53 and Rb. As
discussed earlier, p53 and Rb are essential checkpoints that halt cell cycle progres-

 14
sion when DNA damage occurs, making them both essential for maintaining genomic
integrity. Mutations in RB1, the encoding gene of Rb, can lead to uncontrolled S-
phase entry. Mutations in p53’s encoding gene TP53, a mutation often encountered
in HGSC, can result in the loss of cell cycle arrest and apoptotic response. Both al-
low cells with damaged DNA to proliferate unchecked, contributing to tumorigenesis
(Glaviano et al., 2025; Madaminov et al., 2021).

While mutations in oncogenes typically drive the cell cycle forward, leading to un-
controlled proliferation, in some cases, these same mutations can instead trigger a
protective mechanism known as oncogene-induced senescence (OIS). This process is a
natural protection against tumorigenesis. Instead of promoting cell cycle progression,
it induces a permanent cell cycle arrest. Thus, senescence is a physiological tumor-
suppressing mechanism, inhibiting the transformation from benign tumor to malig-
nancy. For example, RAS mutations without further mutations in oncogenes or tumor
suppressor genes can trigger OIS. Alone this mutation is not sufficient to drive the

-
suppressing mechanism, inhibiting the transformation from benign tumor to malig-
nancy. For example, RAS mutations without further mutations in oncogenes or tumor
suppressor genes can trigger OIS. Alone this mutation is not sufficient to drive the
progression into cancer, but instead leads to permanent cell cycle arrest.
Next to senescence, quiescence and dormancy also play significant roles in tumor
development. Quiescence allows cancer cells to reversibly exit the cell cycle, enabling
survival under adverse conditions and resistance to therapies targeting dividing cells.
Dormancy, often seen in disseminated tumor cells, permits long-term survival in a
non-proliferative state, contributing to tumor relapse years after initial treatment.
Both states enable cancer cells to evade treatment and may serve as reservoirs for
tumor recurrence (Endo & Inoue, 2019; Valcourt et al., 2012).
In recent years, cell cycle-targeting therapies have emerged as promising treatments
for cancers driven by cell cycle dysregulation. These therapies typically aim to re-
store normal cell cycle control by inhibiting key regulators such CDKs, which are often
overactive in cancer cells. Additionally, some therapies aim to induce senescence in
tumor cells, effectively halting their proliferation. These therapies exploit the natural
tumor-suppressive role of senescence, triggering a permanent cell cycle arrest in can-
cer cells, thus reducing tumor growth. However, effectively managing this as well as
other out-of-cycle states remains a challenge (Glaviano et al., 2025).

Overall, the cell cycle and its control represent a critical aspect of tumor heterogeneity
and cancer development, influencing both cell proliferation and cell death. Under-

as
other out-of-cycle states remains a challenge (Glaviano et al., 2025).

Overall, the cell cycle and its control represent a critical aspect of tumor heterogeneity
and cancer development, influencing both cell proliferation and cell death. Under-
standing the mechanisms underlying cell cycle dysregulation and out-of-cycle states
is key to improving cancer treatments.


 15
1.3.3 Cell cycle staging

The kinetics of the tumor cell cycle impact prognosis and influence how a tumor
responds to treatments targeting specific cell cycle phases. There are several methods
to determine the cell cycle stage a cell is in, each with its advantages depending on
the research question (Williams & Stoeber, 2012).
One frequently used method measures the amount of DNA within the cells. While
cells in the earlier cycle stages, G0 and G1, contain only one genome set, in stages after
the S phase, G2 and M, the amount of DNA in cells doubles. The DNA content during
S phase is somewhere in between. To measure the DNA content, DNA is stained by
fluorescent dyes that stoichiometrically bind to the DNA, for example Hoechst 33258,
Hoechst 33342, propidium iodide, or 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole. The fluorescent
signal is analyzed and the percentages of cells in G0/G1, S, and G2/M phase can be
calculated using several mathematical models (Ligasová et al., 2023).
Another widely used approach, many cell cycle analysis methods are based on, is
immunofluorescence (IF) staining. It relies on detecting specific cell cycle proteins
with antibodies conjugated with fluorescent dyes and thus enables the visualization
and qualitative and quantitative analysis of cell cycle progression and cell proliferation
(Bruhn et al., 2014; Williams & Stoeber, 2012). An example for a method based on

ntibodies conjugated with fluorescent dyes and thus enables the visualization
and qualitative and quantitative analysis of cell cycle progression and cell proliferation
(Bruhn et al., 2014; Williams & Stoeber, 2012). An example for a method based on
IF staining is Ki67 staining. Ki67 is a nuclear protein that is expressed during all
active phases of the cell cycle (G1, S, G2, and M) but is absent in non-dividing
cells (G0, dormant, and senescent cells). Through this characteristic, it is widely
used to assess cell proliferation and the fraction of proliferating cells in a population
(Ligasová et al., 2023). Another method using this approach aims to identify cells
in S phase. It is based on culturing cells in media containing nucleoside analoga like
bromodeoxyuridine. Cells in S phase incorporate the artificial building block into the
newly synthesized DNA and can be detected by staining cells with antibodies that
specifically bind to the added modified nucleoside (Bruhn et al., 2014; Ligasová et al.,
2023).
Beyond those two examples, many other markers can be used to study and determine
the different cell cycle stages. Several examples are listed in Table 1.

1.4 Spatial resolution methodologies
Spatial resolution refers to the ability to distinguish and map features within a tissue
based on their location and structure. It is essential in biological research for un-
derstanding the organization of cells, tissues, and molecular interactions. In tumor
research, spatial resolution is crucial for examining how cancer cells interact with the
TME. These interactions are key to understanding cancer progression and therapy
resistance, making spatial resolution critical for developing targeted treatments.

For spatial analysis, tissue samples must be prepared to preserve their biological
structure on a molecular and cellular level. Formalin fixation followed by paraffin
embedding is the most common method used in clinical settings, producing stable
formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks that can be stored long-term and
used for various staining techniques. Another method, cryosectioning, rapidly freezes
tissues, preserving molecular structures. Resulting blocks from both methods are
sliced with a microtome into thin tissue sections, before they can be analyzed using
various techniques (Bodenmiller, 2016).
Traditional staining methods such as hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), immunohisto-
chemistry (IHC), and IF are widely used to visualize tissue structures. H&E staining
is a standard practice in pathology for diagnosis and prognostic evaluation. It provides
a general overview of tissue architecture, but lacks molecular specificity. IHC uses

(IHC), and IF are widely used to visualize tissue structures. H&E staining
is a standard practice in pathology for diagnosis and prognostic evaluation. It provides
a general overview of tissue architecture, but lacks molecular specificity. IHC uses
antibodies to visualize specific proteins with a chromogenic signal, typically detecting


 17
one or two markers at a time. IF, using fluorescence-labeled antibodies, allows detec-
tion of up to five markers with high resolution, but is limited by photo bleaching and
fluorophore overlap, restricting the number of markers visible at once.
While valuable, these methods have limitations when studying the complex TME.
H&E offers minimal molecular information, and both IHC and IF are constrained by
their low multiplex capacity, making it difficult to analyze multiple tissue components
simultaneously (Fischer et al., 2008; Tan et al., 2020).
In contrast to the limited amount of molecular information H&E, IHC, and IF can
provide, omics approaches such as genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics offer
the ability to generate vast amounts of molecular data, even at a single-cell level.
Techniques like whole genome sequencing, RNA-seq, and mass spectrometry (MS)-
based proteomics enable comprehensive profiling, thereby providing deep insights into
molecular landscapes. However, these methods lack spatial context, which is critical
for understanding how cells interact within their environment (Hasin et al., 2017).

The low multiplex capacity of traditional staining methods as well as the lack of
spatial resolution in omics technology highlight the need for novel methods that in-
tegrate molecular data with tissue architecture, offering a more complete picture of
the structural and functional landscape of the heterogeneous TME.

as the lack of
spatial resolution in omics technology highlight the need for novel methods that in-
tegrate molecular data with tissue architecture, offering a more complete picture of
the structural and functional landscape of the heterogeneous TME. This has led to
the development of advanced imaging technologies, that enable highly multiplexed
spatial analysis, and novel spatially resolved omics methods. While these emerging
approaches, which are discussed in the following chapters, offer exciting potential
for simultaneously analyzing multiple biomarkers and providing spatial context, they
still have some limitations (Merritt et al., 2020). These challenges present an on-
going opportunity for the development of new technologies and methodologies that
could overcome the limitations of existing approaches and enable a more complete
and scalable integration of molecular and spatial data.

1.4.1 Highly multiplexed imaging techniques

Highly multiplexed imaging techniques enable the simultaneous detection of multiple
biomarkers within a single tissue sample, allowing for a comprehensive spatial analysis
of molecular composition and cellular interactions. Unlike traditional methods such
as IHC and IF, these advanced techniques are not limited to a few markers and
can measure a large number of proteins, nucleic acids, or metabolites in the same
sample, making them essential for studying complex heterogeneous environments like
the TME.
The existing techniques can be divided into two categories: mass cytometry/ spectro-

 18
metry-based and fluorescence-based imaging. Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based
methods provide higher sensitivity in comparison to fluorescence-based methods, but
are limited in their smaller coverage area and therefore low throughput, and more

18
metry-based and fluorescence-based imaging. Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based
methods provide higher sensitivity in comparison to fluorescence-based methods, but
are limited in their smaller coverage area and therefore low throughput, and more
complex, time-intensive analysis. In contrast, fluorescence-based methods are not as
detailed, but are less dependent on specialized reagents, expensive equipment, and
allow a higher throughput, thereby making the analysis of larger tissue areas possible
(Giesen et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2018).

Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging

Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging combines mass cytometry or MS with
spatial tissue analysis, allowing for simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers in
tissue sections with high precision at subcellular resolution. The general principle
involves using antibodies conjugated to heavy metal isotopes or other molecules to
label the tissue, which is then ablated. The released ions are analyzed to map their
abundance across the tissue, revealing detailed molecular patterns.

One widely used method is imaging mass cytometry (IMC). It uses metal-tagged
antibodies and a laser that ablates specific tissue regions. Resulting ions are analyzed
by time-of-flight mass cytometry, allowing the detection of over 40 protein markers
simultaneously with high-resolution, high-plex imaging (up to 1 µm). However, it is
limited by the size of the region of interest (ROI) and low throughput due to long
analysis time per sample (Giesen et al., 2014; Milosevic, 2023).
Building on similar principles, multiplex ion beam imaging (MIBI) uses secondary ion
mass cytometry. Here, an ion beam hits the tissue to release secondary ions, which
are analyzed to map the distribution of metal-labeled antibodies.

ilosevic, 2023).
Building on similar principles, multiplex ion beam imaging (MIBI) uses secondary ion
mass cytometry. Here, an ion beam hits the tissue to release secondary ions, which
are analyzed to map the distribution of metal-labeled antibodies. It provides excellent
spatial resolution (down to 200 nm) for single-cell analysis and can detect up to seven
markers simultaneously, with the potential to expand detection by iterating up to 10
times over tissue layers. MIBI offers better single-cell resolution than IMC but is also
limited by small ROIs and the requirement for specialized equipment (Angelo et al.,
2014; Baharlou et al., 2019).
In contrast, matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI)-imaging MS takes
a label-free approach. Using laser ablation, it ionizes molecules like proteins, lipids,
and metabolites for analysis via MS. It offers spatial resolution between 10-50 µm and
can detect up to 10-20 markers simultaneously. While it provides detailed molecular
information over a wide variety of molecules, MALDI-imaging MS is limited in mul-
tiplex capacity and requires careful sample preparation (Zaima et al., 2010).


 19
Taken together, mass cytometry/MS-based imaging methods provide high-plex and
quantitative analysis, making them ideal for studying complex tissues such as the
TME. These techniques can analyze a broad range of biomarkers in parallel with sub-
cellular resolution. Nonetheless, they are often time-consuming, require specialized
instrumentation with complex data analysis, and have relatively low throughput. Ad-
ditionally, while MALDI-imaging MS offers broad coverage, its multiplex capacity is
lower than that of IMC or MIBI, and the methods are generally restricted to smaller
tissue sections.

ditionally, while MALDI-imaging MS offers broad coverage, its multiplex capacity is
lower than that of IMC or MIBI, and the methods are generally restricted to smaller
