Fluorescence-based imaging

Fluorescence-based highly multiplexed imaging techniques use antibodies conjugated
to fluorescent molecules to detect extra- and intracellular proteins in tissue sections.
These methods allow the detection of multiple markers within a single tissue sample
by applying multiple cycles of fluorescence staining and visualization, followed by
fluorophore bleaching. This enables the simultaneous identification of numerous bio-
markers, offering detailed phenotypic and neighborhood information.

Tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) is one such technique, enabling
the detection of up to 60 markers within a single tissue section. With t-CycIF, even
large tissue areas can be explored with single-cell resolution. It uses sequential cycles
of staining with DNA dye and 3-5 fluorescence-labeled antibodies (depending on the
number of available fluorescence channels of the imaging device), imaging, and fluoro-
phore inactivation through oxidation in hydrogen peroxide under light exposure. The
resulting low-plex images are then overlaid to visualize the tissue in a high-plexed
way (up to 60-plex images). t-CycIF stands out for not requiring specialized reagents,
its compatibility with commercially available antibodies, and its customizable anti-
body panels, making it adaptable for various projects. It also works well with FFPE
samples and allows for H&E staining afterwards. While the resolution depends on
the imaging system, super-resolution imaging (ă200 nm (Schermelleh et al., 2019))
is possible.

els, making it adaptable for various projects. It also works well with FFPE
samples and allows for H&E staining afterwards. While the resolution depends on
the imaging system, super-resolution imaging (ă200 nm (Schermelleh et al., 2019))
is possible. Albeit a key limitation is tissue degradation after several cycles. Con-
sequently, the truly possible number of biomarkers is restricted by the number of
cycles the tissue integrity allows for (Lin et al., 2018).
Another method, co-detection by indexing (CODEX), is based on DNA-barcoded
antibodies that are conjugated to target proteins. Fluorescence-labeled reporter se-
quences bind to the DNA barcodes, enabling the visualization of multiple markers in


 20
a tissue section. After imaging, the reporter sequences are chemically stripped, allow-
ing for the application of new markers and fluorescence labels in subsequent cycles.
This iterative process enables the detection of up to 60 markers at high resolution
(up to 200 nm), with three markers per cycle, while minimizing batch effects typical
for other techniques. However, it is limited by costly reagents, the availability of
non-cross-reacting and barcoded antibodies, and small tissue areas (4 mm ˆ 4 mm).
The system is also sensitive to photo bleaching (Black et al., 2021).
A further approach, Opal multiplex IHC assay, uses tyramine signal amplification
to visualize proteins. In this method, target-specific primary antibodies bind to the
proteins, and secondary antibodies conjugated with horseradish peroxidase bind to
the primary antibodies. The secondary antibodies react with tyrosine linked to a
fluorophore and lead to the formation of covalent bonds between fluorophore and
tissue in the vicinity of the target antigen. Afterwards, heat-induced antigen re-

nd to
the primary antibodies. The secondary antibodies react with tyrosine linked to a
fluorophore and lead to the formation of covalent bonds between fluorophore and
tissue in the vicinity of the target antigen. Afterwards, heat-induced antigen re-
trieval removes the antibodies to enable the next round of antibody staining. After
all marker signal amplification rounds, the tissue is stained with a nuclear dye, be-
fore the staining is visualized. Through the use of secondary antibodies and signal
amplification, Opal enables the detection of low-abundant proteins. Moreover, there
is no antibody cross reactivity, achievable resolution is relatively high, up to 250 nm,
and it provides a higher signal-to-noise ratio and photostability compared to other
fluorescence techniques. Disadvantages of this method include the limitation to 8-10
markers per slide due to fluorophore overlap and tissue decay after multiple rounds
of staining (Harms et al., 2023; Opal Multiplex, 2022).

All in all, fluorescence-based imaging methods offer a powerful way to visualize mul-
tiple markers in a single tissue section with single-cell resolution. They are becoming
more widely used with a growing popularity due to the abundant availability of re-
agents and antibodies as well as the ability to examine larger tissue areas, in contrast
to the smaller ROIs typical in mass cytometry. However, they also encounter chal-
lenges such as background fluorescence (particularly in FFPE tissue blocks), signal
overlap between markers, and the potential for signal leakage caused by overlapping
emission and absorption spectra.

1.4.2 Proteomics

Proteomics is the investigation of structure, function, composition, activity, and in-
teractions of proteins (Al-Amrani et al., 2021). Since protein expression changes
throughout the cell cycle and adjusts to environmental conditions, proteomics en-
ables an actual insight into current cellular events and is an important foundation
for the characterization of cellular systems. Because of this, it is widely used in vari-
ous research areas, especially medical fields like oncology for finding new diagnostic
biomarkers and therapy targets (Aslam et al., 2016; Vitzthum et al., 2005).
In general, there are three major forms of proteomics:
Expression proteomics focuses on the qualitative and quantitative analysis of pro-
teins differently expressed within two cellular states, such as diseased and healthy
cells. With it disease specific biomarkers and proteins involved into the disease mech-
anism can be identified. Usually, techniques like 2-dimensional gel electrophoresis and
MS are used.

ssed within two cellular states, such as diseased and healthy
cells. With it disease specific biomarkers and proteins involved into the disease mech-
anism can be identified. Usually, techniques like 2-dimensional gel electrophoresis and
MS are used. A second approach concentrates on the protein structure. Structural
proteomics utilizes methods like X-ray crystallography and nuclear magnetic reson-
ance spectroscopy to investigate 3-dimensional complex structures. Lastly, there is
the field of functional proteomics. It aims to unravel the biological role of proteins
within the cell, especially by focusing on protein-protein interactions (Al-Amrani et
al., 2021).
For investigating the cellular proteome, a series of different methodologies can be
utilized. Purification of protein samples is often done using various chromatographic
methods. Complex protein mixtures can be separated using gel based approaches

 22
such as 2-dimensional gel electrophoresis. While for the analysis of individual proteins
methods like western blotting, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, or protein mi-
croarrays/chips are commonly applied, the latter one also as high-throughput method,
whole proteome characterizations are usually performed with high-sensitivity, high-
throughput methods like MS. And, as already mentioned, X-ray crystallography and
nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, also high-throughput, are used for determ-
ining the 3-dimensional structure of proteins. Next to these listed techniques, there
are many more that are employed in proteomics analysis, but listing all would exceed
the scope of this work (Aslam et al., 2016).

MS-based proteomics analysis

Over the last decade, MS has become the main method for qualitative and quantitat-
ive proteomics analysis.

are employed in proteomics analysis, but listing all would exceed
the scope of this work (Aslam et al., 2016).

MS-based proteomics analysis

Over the last decade, MS has become the main method for qualitative and quantitat-
ive proteomics analysis. With its high sensitivity, resolution, accuracy, and possibility
to perform it on a broad range of scales, from identification of single proteins to many
thousand proteins, it is ideal for studying a wide variety of protein samples in great de-
tail (Rozanova et al., 2021; Shuken, 2023). The method is based on ionizing peptides
and proteins, separating them based on their mass-to-charge ratio m{z, and ultimately
detecting the ions via measuring time, m{z, and signal intensity. Commonly, electro-
spray ionization, MALDI, or surface enhanced laser desorption/ionization are used
for the ionization of the analytes (Aslam et al., 2016; Rozanova et al., 2021).
The used MS approach usually depends on the research question. There are two main
strategies: untargeted and targeted quantification. For global proteomics, untargeted
quantification is ideal for analyzing large numbers of proteins across samples, provid-
ing a broad proteomics overview. Targeted quantification, however, focuses on specific
proteins or modifications, offering higher sensitivity for applications like biomarker
validation.
MS-based quantification can be label-based, where stable isotopes enable precise
protein comparisons across samples, or label-free, which relies on natural peptide
intensities but demands high experimental reproducibility. Furthermore, quantifica-
tion can be relative, comparing protein levels between samples, or absolute, measuring
the exact concentration of a protein within a sample (Rozanova et al., 2021).

intensities but demands high experimental reproducibility. Furthermore, quantifica-
tion can be relative, comparing protein levels between samples, or absolute, measuring
the exact concentration of a protein within a sample (Rozanova et al., 2021).
Moreover, there are two different concepts of sample preparation - the experimental
strategy can be either bottom-up or top-down. The bottom-up method involves
digesting proteins into smaller peptides for high-throughput analysis, which is effective
for complex samples but loses the relationship between peptides and their parent
proteins, complicating interpretation. In contrast, the top-down approach analyzes


 23
whole proteins, preserving their structural context and enabling the identification
of protein modifications, but it is less widely used due to its complexity and lower
throughput (Al-Amrani et al., 2021; Shuken, 2023).
From these broad MS approaches, a variety of specific techniques have emerged, en-
abling a very focused, problem-specific exploration of protein functions, modifications,
and interactions in various biological contexts. One prominent example is shotgun
proteomics, which is commonly used for untargeted analysis. In shotgun proteomics,
proteins are first digested into peptides, which are then analyzed using data-dependent
or data-independent acquisition. In the former, a subset of the most abundant pre-
cursor ions is selected for fragmentation and analysis, whereas in the latter, all pre-
cursor ions within a predefined mass window are fragmented simultaneously, offering
more reproducibility and less bias in comparison (Rozanova et al., 2021).

Spatially resolved proteomics

Spatially resolved proteomics is an advancing field that integrates proteomics ana-

defined mass window are fragmented simultaneously, offering
more reproducibility and less bias in comparison (Rozanova et al., 2021).

Spatially resolved proteomics

Spatially resolved proteomics is an advancing field that integrates proteomics ana-
lysis with spatial resolution tissue analysis, providing insights into functional roles
of proteins in situ (Lundberg & Borner, 2019). Understanding complex cellular
reactions, like gene expression and signaling cascades, within their spatial context
is essential, as tissue usually has a high level of cellular heterogeneity and cellular be-
havior is influenced by its microenvironment. While bulk-proteomics offers valuable
insights into tissue-wide mechanisms, it averages out the molecular heterogeneity
present in complex tissues, such as tumors and their TME, thereby obscuring im-
portant intracellular variation. In contrast, highly multiplexed imaging methods, like
those discussed before, provide this needed spatial context while enabling the simul-
taneous detection of multiple protein markers. They can therefore also be seen as a
type of spatial proteomics. However, these imaging techniques often face challenges
related to low throughput and limited multiplexing capacity, restricting their abil-
ity to provide truly comprehensive spatial proteomics information. To address these
limitations of traditional proteomics approaches and imaging technologies, several
methods have been developed to achieve spatially resolved proteomics (Taylor et al.,
2021).
Firstly, there is laser capture microdissection. It is a method combining targeted
tissue isolation by cutting tissue with a laser with MS-based proteomics analysis of
the isolated tissue region. As a result, it provides spatial and proteomics information

y, there is laser capture microdissection. It is a method combining targeted
tissue isolation by cutting tissue with a laser with MS-based proteomics analysis of
the isolated tissue region. As a result, it provides spatial and proteomics information
with high precision and high resolution around 100-200 µm (Zhu et al., 2018). On the
downside, laser capture microdissection is limited by a low protein yield due to small


 24
sample sizes, a high amount of manual steps, making it work-intensive and prone to
mistakes, and compromised sensitivity due to small tissue sizes (Gutstein & Morris,
2007; Taylor et al., 2021).
Another method, nano-droplet processing in one pot for trace samples, is especially
useful for small samples. It enables highly sensitive analysis on a single-cell level by
isolating proteins in nano-droplets that are then processed and analyzed typically by
liquid chromatography-tandem MS (LC-MS/MS). Through the droplets sample loss
is minimized, the method’s accuracy is increased, and also low-abundant proteins can
be detected. However, it also has limitations: only small sample sizes are feasible,
and the approach requires specialized equipment, which makes it less accessible (Kelly
et al., 2019).
A third approach is called ProteomEx. Its general principle is based on the integra-
tion of tissue expansion with MS-based proteomics to achieve high-resolution spatially
resolved proteomics. Expansion is achieved by embedding the tissue in a hydrogel.
Through this growth in size, protein distribution within the tissue can be investig-
ated more easily and in more detail. The expanded tissue is then microdissected and
analyzed by MS, typically LC-MS/MS, preserving protein localization while enabling
the identification of thousands of proteins.

ion within the tissue can be investig-
ated more easily and in more detail. The expanded tissue is then microdissected and
analyzed by MS, typically LC-MS/MS, preserving protein localization while enabling
the identification of thousands of proteins. With this method, a resolution up to
160 µm can be achieved, and it is also feasible for small tissue regions without losing
spatial information. Additionally, necessary reagents are commercially available and
the method is cost effectively, robust, and easy to implement. Key drawbacks of
ProteomEx are a possible tissue distortion due to the expansion, lower protein yields,
and, as a consequence, weaker protein signals that may lead to less detected proteins
(Li et al., 2022).

All in all, spatially resolved proteomics approaches provide the possibility to analyze
proteins within their cellular and tissue context. Thereby, they enable investigation
of complex tissues, such as the TME, with high spatial precision and sensitivity, cre-
ating functional protein landscapes. However, despite their promising nature, current
spatial proteomics methods face limitations in scalability, sensitivity, and workflow
complexity. While some achieve relatively high spatial resolution, none meet the
precision of spatially resolved imaging techniques. Additionally, they often require
complex protocols, costly equipment, and specialized reagents, limiting accessibility.
Therefore, there remains a clear need for a spatial proteomics method with high
spatial precision, simple protocols, and affordable materials. Addressing this gap will
improve our ability to map the proteomics architecture of tumors, paving the way for
better-targeted therapies.

2 Aim of the Project
This project aims to establish the basis for a new spatially resolved proteomics method
called ProteoPicking. It will integrate t-CycIF staining with MS-based proteomics for
the targeted analysis of specific ROIs. By interlinking multiplexed imaging analysis,
which enables detailed compositional and phenotypic characterization of ROIs, with
proteomics profiling of the isolated cells, ProteoPicking aims to provide a holistic view
of the structural, functional, and molecular landscape of tissue samples.
The project is guided by the following objectives:

 1. Evaluation of the feasibility of ProteoPicking and compatibility of t-CycIF stain-
 ing with MS-based proteomics analysis.

 2. Establishment and optimization of an experimental workflow comprising t-CycIF
 staining, tissue sample collection of specific ROIs, and subsequent MS analysis.

 3. Exploration of key factors influencing the number of detected proteins within
 samples, like the amount of tissue per sample, the targeted tissue region, or
 used sample lysis buffer.

 4. Development of a data analysis pipeline that integrates image analysis data with
 proteomics analysis data, enabling comparative and spatially informed analysis
 of the structural, functional, and molecular landscape of different tissue samples.

By systematically addressing these objectives, this project aims to overcome key tech-
nical and analytical challenges in developing the method, laying the groundwork for
its effective application. With continued refinement, the successful implementation of

matically addressing these objectives, this project aims to overcome key tech-
nical and analytical challenges in developing the method, laying the groundwork for
its effective application. With continued refinement, the successful implementation of
ProteoPicking has the potential to provide an enhanced spatially resolved proteomics
platform for detailed molecular profiling of complex heterogeneous tissues.

3.1.2 Patients & specimens

All tissue samples were obtained from the ONCOSYS-OVA prospective study collec-
tion or the Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). The ethics board of the Women’s

3.2 Methods
For this Master’s thesis, two experiments - a pilot experiment and an optimization
experiment - were performed. Below, the experimental procedure of both and any
differences between pilot and optimization experiment are described. The changes in
the protocol were necessary since the analysis of the proteomics results of the pilot
experiment showed that the samples were contaminated with keratin and a not further
identified type of polymer, which interfered to a certain degree with the analysis.
Figure 1 depicts the workflow of the method.


 29

3.2.1 Sample sectioning

Tissue samples were processed into FFPE blocks by the tissue preparation and histo-
chemistry unit of the Anatomy Department of the University of Helsinki. Of those

teps of ProteoPicking. Figure created in

3.2.1 Sample sectioning

Tissue samples were processed into FFPE blocks by the tissue preparation and histo-
chemistry unit of the Anatomy Department of the University of Helsinki. Of those
FFPE blocks, tissue sections were cut with a Microm HM 355 motorized rotary micro-
tome. For the pilot experiment, 8 µm thick slides were sectioned, while the sections
for the optimization experiment were 10 µm thick. The sliced sections were trans-
ferred into a waterbath set to 40 ˝ C for approximately 1 min to completely unfold.
Only then the sections were scooped up with SuperFrost Plus Adhesion Microscope
Slides, dried as thoroughly as possible, placed onto a tray, baked at 56 ˝ C for 2 h, and
dried overnight at room temperature. Afterwards the samples were stored at 4 ˝ C
until further usage.

Throughout the optimization experiment, several practices were implemented to avoid
contamination. Gloves were used throughout the entire experiment. The microtome,
the tools for transferring the sections, the work surface, and the storage box of the
microscope slides were cleaned thoroughly beforehand with ethanol. The water of the
waterbath was replaced with fresh water directly from the Milli-Q filtering system,
instead of using pre-filled Milli-Q water from reservoir containers, and the waterbath
was cleaned before refilling. A new package of microscope slides was opened to prevent
contamination from earlier experiments, and the slides were stored vertically in a
plastic box with a lid, which was only removed during the baking step to allow
the remaining water to evaporate from the slides. In comparison, during the pilot
experiments the slides were transported and stored on wooden trays without proper
cover.

the remaining water to evaporate from the slides. In comparison, during the pilot

3.2.2 t-CycIF staining

The t-CycIF protocol (Lin et al., 2018) was adapted to be compatible with proteomics
MS assays. Within the standard protocol, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) is used
as a buffer to maintain pH and osmolarity to preserve tissue morphology and protein
integrity (Visikol, 2024). But since we perform MS-based proteomics analysis follow-
ing the t-CycIF process and the phosphate content of PBS would interfere with this
analysis, it was replaced with tris-buffered saline (TBS), which provides comparable
buffering properties (Cappiello et al., 1997).

Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval, and pre-staining

First, slides were baked at 56 ˝ C for 30 min. Then, they were deparaffinized and
rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each followed by
immersing them for 3 min each in a series of ethanol solution with gradually decreas-
ing ethanol concentration (100 %, 90 %, 70 %, 50 % and 30 % in Milli-Q). Finally,
the slides were washed twice for 1 min in 1x TBS (50 mM tris-HCl, pH 7.6; 150 mM
NaCl). In a next step, samples were submerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid buffer
(10 mM citric acid monohydrate, pH 6.0) and heated up to 95 ˝ C for 15 min in an
Antigen retrieval Chamber High Voltage. This is done to fully retrieve the antigen
molecules, which can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the formalin fixation.
Afterwards, the slide holder with slides and filled with the hot citric acid buffer was

etrieval Chamber High Voltage. This is done to fully retrieve the antigen
molecules, which can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the formalin fixation.
Afterwards, the slide holder with slides and filled with the hot citric acid buffer was
taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room temperature and then slides
were washed three times for 3 min each in 1x TBS. To minimize unspecific antibody
binding of the tissue, 200-300 µL of filtered Odyssey blocking buffer were pipetted
onto the tissue samples, which were then incubated at room temperature for 30 min
in a moist Kartell Polystyrene Microscope Slide Box incubation chamber to prevent
evaporation and dehydration of the tissue. After incubation, the Odyssey blocking
buffer was removed by vertically tapping the slides on tissue paper. The samples were
then washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS.

For the optimization experiment, again protocol changes were implemented to avoid
any kind of contamination. All used equipment, like slide holders, slide chambers,
antigen retrieval chamber, incubation chamber, and tweezers, as well as work sur-
faces were cleaned thoroughly before the experiment. Fresh xylene was used and the
ethanol chain was freshly prepared, in comparison to the pilot experiment where pre-
prepared solutions were reused. Reagents and buffers were prepared in glass Schott
flasks and the water was acquired directly from the Milli-Q filtering system, instead of

 31
using pre-filled Milli-Q water from reservoir containers. Moreover, the use of plastic
containers was avoided as much as possible. Plastic slide holders and chambers were
replaced with glass slide holders and chambers, except for the antigen retrieval cham-
ber, which was only available in a plastic version.

Antibody staining

Before the actual staining was performed, antibody panels for both experiments were
planned with three cycles for the pilot project (Table 6) respectively two cycles for
the optimization experiment (Table 7). The antibody panels used in this project
had already been validated and established in previous experiments, making further
validation not necessary. The antibodies were selected to detect tumor, stroma, and
immune cells as well as to identify different phases of the cell cycle.
For each cycle of staining, an antibody mastermix was prepared containing all anti-
bodies for one cycle and a nuclear dye, diluted accordingly in filtered Odyssey blocking
buffer. Hoechst 33342 was used for nuclear staining in each cycle. Adding it to each
staining mastermix is especially important for subsequent image processing and ana-
lysis steps (see chapter Image acquisition, processing, analysis, and visualization).
100 µL of the antibody mixture were pipetted per sample. The slides were then in-
cubated in a moist incubation chamber at 4 ˝ C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies were necessary
on the next day and the slides were directly washed three times in 1x TBS for 3 min
each. Afterwards, 80-100 µL of 70 % glycerol in Milli-Q were pipetted onto the tissue
samples and the slides were covered with Epredia Menzel Microscope Coverslips for
the following imaging. After the imaging, coverslips were removed again by placing
the slides vertically in 1x TBS.

% glycerol in Milli-Q were pipetted onto the tissue
samples and the slides were covered with Epredia Menzel Microscope Coverslips for
the following imaging. After the imaging, coverslips were removed again by placing
the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short while the coverslips detached on their
own and the coverless slides were either prepared for the next staining cycle (fluoro-
phore inactivation) or stored in 1x TBS at 4 ˝ C for further steps.

For the pilot project, two of the three used tissue samples were only stained with one
cycle of antibodies (cycle 3) to have two samples that did not undergo the aggress-
ive bleaching step, which is performed in between each staining cycle (see chapter
Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching)). This approach allows the comparison of pro-
teomics results between sections with and without fluorophore inactivation, assessing
its potential impact on MS-based proteomics analysis outcomes.
Also during this phase of the optimization experiment, special precautions were taken.
Materials and work surfaces were again cleaned before usage. A new package of cov-


 32
erslips was opened to prevent contamination from earlier experiments. And for the
washing and decoverslipping steps, glass slide holders and chambers were used instead
of plastic ones.

Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching)

The slides need to undergo fluorophore inactivation between each staining cycle to
guarantee that no fluorescent signal from the previous cycle remains. For this, the
slides were placed in a transparent plastic container with 50 mL freshly prepared
bleaching solution (40 mL 1x TBS, 7.5 mL 30 % H2 O2 , 2.5 mL 480 mM NaOH). 50 mL
are sufficient for 7 to 8 slides. The completely submerged slides were exposed to LED
lights from an upper and lower light panel for 90 min at room temperature. After
the first staining cycle, the bleaching solution was replaced two times, once after
30 min and again after 60 min. For every following bleaching step, the solution was
exchanged only once after 45 min. Afterwards, the slides were washed four times in
1x TBS for 3 min each.

During this step of the protocol, for both experiments the slides were submerged in
bleaching solution in plastic dishes because there was no glass replacement that could

bleaching solution in plastic dishes because there was no glass replacement that could
have been used for the optimization experiment. The slide holders and chambers for
the washing step were again replaced with glass versions for the second experiment.

3.2.3 Image acquisition

The images of the tissue samples were acquired using a CyteFinder II imaging instru-
ment. It is a high-throughput automated microscope device for digital imaging that
enables multiplexed, high-resolution tissue imaging of whole slide sections. Within
the scanner, there is a slide holder with space for two slides and multiple microscope
lenses to scan the slides at different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, and 40x). With its
different channels, up to four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously in addition
to a nuclear dye. The scanner is connected to various software applications to control
and perform the imaging. The RareCyte Instrument Controller helps to operate the
microscope. The RareCyte CyteHub application is a control center for scanning as-
says and image analysis. With the RareCyte CyteFinder program, the scan process
can be set up and monitored, and the RareCyte Imager software allows real-time
imaging and picking control (see chapter Cell picking).

Before the scanning, covered slides were placed upside down in the scanner since
the microscope lenses are inverted and positioned beneath the slide holder. Then,
a scanning assay was created within the RareCyte CyteHub application. In it, all
necessary parameters for the scanning process were defined, see Table 8.

After the assay was created, the slide scanning was started using the RareCyte
CyteFinder program. The first time a slide was scanned, a pre-scan was performed
to identify the tissue area. This enables manual adjustment of the scanning region
and selection of focus points necessary for accurate overlay of images from multiple
cycles of one section. The chosen scanning region and focus points were stored in the
RareCyte CyteHub program and were applied to every future scan of the slide.
The actual scanning process was performed automatically. For this, the scanning area
was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so-called “tiles”, by the system. The tiles had
overlapping regions with their adjacent neighbor tiles to enable correct assembling of
the tiles into a complete image during the image processing (see chapter Image pro-
cessing). The size and number of tiles depended on the chosen magnification and
were automatically calculated by the system. Tiles were scanned sequentially, with


 35
the next tile being scanned only after all channels of the prior tile were completed.
After the whole selected area was scanned, the raw data of each tile was stored in
a rcpnl file for further processing. Moreover, for each scanning cycle an ome.tiff
file was created from the RareCyte CyteFinder II program, containing the completely
assembled image with all channels of the respective cycle. These files were later used
for the ROI selection in the cell picking process (see chapter Cell picking).

3.2.4 Cell picking

The cell picking step is a critical connecting step in this workflow that interlinks
t-CycIF-based spatial analysis with MS-based proteomics analysis. The protocol for
the targeted microdissection of selected ROIs was adapted from Pick-seq, a spatially
resolved transcriptomics approach established by RareCyte (Ericson et al., 2019; Po-
dyminogin et al., 2019).

ROIs selection

For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the ome.tiff files of the individual cycles were
opened in the tissue result section of the RareCyte CyteHub software. The desired
cells were identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter, which corres-
ponds to the diameter of the micro needle that was used for the picking. The selected
ROIs were stored as a rcpickmap file.


 36
Picking preparations

The necessary needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL
PCR tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of the cells
from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the collection
of the picked cells. Depending on the experiment, they either contained 20 µL of urea
buffer (50 mM tris-HCl, 6 M urea, pH 7.5 to 8, protease and phosphatase inhibitor

m the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the collection
of the picked cells. Depending on the experiment, they either contained 20 µL of urea
buffer (50 mM tris-HCl, 6 M urea, pH 7.5 to 8, protease and phosphatase inhibitor
tablets) or 20 µL TFE lysis buffer (50 % (v{v) 2,2,2-trifuoroethanol, 300 mM tris-HCl,
pH 8.0, protease and phosphatase inhibitor tablets). When everything was prepared,
the retrieval module was placed into the right slide holder position in the CyteFinder II
device.
Next, the RareCyte Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle was
performed. This step ensures precise control over the needle by aligning the actual
position (x, y) of it with the position displayed in the RareCyte Imager and by assur-
ing that the tip of the needle actually touches the slide (z) during the picking process.
For the calibration, an empty slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide
holder position. The fingerprints helped with focusing the image. Then a needle was
selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing solution. Afterwards, the
needle was positioned above the slide for either an automatic calibration or a manual
one, where the parameters of the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were
adjusted.
In a last step, the created rcpickmap file, which contained the selected ROIs, was
imported into the RareCyte Imager software with the Imager Main Control together
with the metadata file of the slide.

Picking & collection

For controlling the picking and collection process, the RareCyte Imager software was
used. First, the picking parameters were set, see Table 9. Then the slide the cells were
to be picked from was put into the left slide holder position facing upwards, and its

lling the picking and collection process, the RareCyte Imager software was
used. First, the picking parameters were set, see Table 9. Then the slide the cells were
to be picked from was put into the left slide holder position facing upwards, and its
image was focused using the Hoechst channel. Subsequently, the picking process was
performed. It comprised of a series of steps, see Figure 3. The needle was flushed two
to three times in the rinsing solution, then it was filled with retrieval solution before

As soon as the cells were collected, the next cell picking cycle was started until all

tubes were exchanged, or the picking process was finished by rinsing the needle three

After all ROIs from one slide were picked, the remaining tissue was imaged again.
For this, as described in chapter Antibody staining, a coverslip was positioned above

3.2.5 Image processing, phenotyping, analysis and visualization

In the pilot experiment, rcpnl files from imaging were simply combined into whole
images through stitching and registration (see below) to enable tissue visualization.
The optimization experiment, however, involved comprehensive processing of the gen-
erated rcpnl files, following the entire workflow described below - including all pro-
cessing steps, phenotyping, and image analysis.
An overview of image processing, phenotyping, and visualization is shown in Figure 4.


Figure 4: Overview of the image processing (including illumination correction, stitching
and registration, segmentation, and quantification), phenotyping, and visualization. Figure
created in BioRender, 2025.


Image processing

The first step of the image processing workflow is illumination correction. Uneven

tion correction, stitching
and registration, segmentation, and quantification), phenotyping, and visualization. Figure

Image processing

The first step of the image processing workflow is illumination correction. Uneven
illumination, particularly darker edges in tiles, can compromise quantitative analysis
and impact subsequent processing. To address this, the BaSiC program (background
and shading correction, Peng et al., 2017) is used to correct rcpnl files generated

 39
by the RareCyte CyteFinder application. BaSiC models and compensates for illu-
mination bias (the non-uniform brightness from the light source) and shading bias
caused by optical effects like vignetting. This correction results in uniform image
illumination, ensuring consistent fluorescence intensity measurements and reliable
downstream analysis.
Next, a complete multiplex image is created by assembling all tiles, channels, and ima-
ging cycles, including the tiles from the post-picking image, from a single slide. For
this, the open-source Python package ASHLAR (alignment by simultaneous harmon-
ization of layer/adjacency registration, Muhlich et al., 2022) was used to process the
illumination-corrected rcpnl files. ASHLAR’s workflow consists of two main steps:
stitching and registration. In those steps, it relies on the nuclear staining through
Hoechst, as nuclei provide high-contrast, and well-defined features with clear bound-
aries. During stitching, adjacent tiles within the same imaging cycle are aligned.
During registration, images of different channels from different cycles covering the
same tissue regions are aligned and overlaid. The output is an ome.tiff file - a
multichannel, pyramidal stacked image containing multiple resolution layers. This

.
During registration, images of different channels from different cycles covering the
same tissue regions are aligned and overlaid. The output is an ome.tiff file - a
multichannel, pyramidal stacked image containing multiple resolution layers. This
format enables faster visualization and efficient processing by loading only the neces-
sary portions and resolutions of the image into memory as needed.
Following stitching and registration, rectangular regions containing all selected ROIs
of one sample were extracted from the whole slide images. The coordinates defining
these regions in the original whole slide images were determined using post-picking
image channels, showing the remaining tissue holes, and the ‘new shape layer’ fea-
ture of the open-source multidimensional image viewer Napari (Napari Contributors,
2019). Cropped images were then generated by cutting out the areas corresponding
to these coordinates. All subsequent processing steps, phenotyping, and analysis were
performed on these cropped images rather than the entire slides.
After extracting the ROI areas from the whole slide images, gamma correction and
unsharp masking were applied to enhance nuclear visibility by increasing background
intensity and sharpening cell edges. The used parameters for this step, γ “ 2{3,
σ “ 1.0, and strength “ 2.5, were determined beforehand through empirical testing.
Using the two contrast and sharpness enhancement methods was necessary to improve
the performance of the subsequent processing step - image segmentation.
Segmentation is the process of detecting and outlining individual cells in an image
by assigning each pixel to either a specific cell or the background. For this step, the
nuclear segmentation model Mesmer (mesoscopic segmentation of cellular and nuclear

ntation is the process of detecting and outlining individual cells in an image
by assigning each pixel to either a specific cell or the background. For this step, the
nuclear segmentation model Mesmer (mesoscopic segmentation of cellular and nuclear
regions, Greenwald et al., 2022) was used. It is a set of pre-trained deep learning
models designed for high-quality biological image analysis. Using a convolutional


 40
neural network Mesmer generates per-pixel probability values for nuclear regions, cell
boundaries, and background. A distance transform and post-processing algorithm,
similar to the watershed method, refines these probability maps to accurately segment
individual nuclei, even in densely packed regions. Mesmer operates on two input
channels - either one nuclear and one cytoplasmic, or two nuclear channels. In this
project, the Hoechst channels from cycles one and two were used. The resulting cell
masks were saved as ome.tiff files.
As the final processing step, single-cell information was extracted by quantifying the
segmented images using the scikit-image Python package (Van der Walt et al., 2014).
It uses the assembled images and generated mask files to produce a data frame with
multiple parameters for spatial analysis for each image. Each data frame includes key
features such as CellID, x and y coordinates, eccentricity, area, and median marker
intensity values for each object across all channels.

Phenotyping

Following image processing, the next step is cell phenotyping, in which each cell is
classified based on the expression levels of specific biomarkers. This is carried out
using Scimap, a scalable toolkit for analyzing spatial molecular data (Nirmal & Sorger,
2024). The package operates on the quantified single-cell data frames generated in

ied based on the expression levels of specific biomarkers. This is carried out
using Scimap, a scalable toolkit for analyzing spatial molecular data (Nirmal & Sorger,
2024). The package operates on the quantified single-cell data frames generated in
the last image processing step, using marker intensity values along with the x and y
coordinates of each cell.
A key step in this workflow is gating, which involves defining intensity thresholds for
key phenotype markers to separate expressing from non-expressing cells. For the im-
ages of the optimization experiment, nearly all markers from both cycles were gated,
except for the two Hoechst channels. Gating thresholds were manually defined using
the Scimap gate finder function, which overlays marker expression data on the ori-
ginal tissue image in the image viewer Napari. This approach allowed for the visual
exploration of intensity ranges and the determination of cutoff values to classify cells
as positive or negative for each relevant marker. Based on this binary classification,
each cell is then assigned a phenotype using a logic table (see Table 10). This table
defines which combinations of positive and negative markers correspond to specific
cell types. The resulting phenotype annotations were added to the single-cell data
frame for downstream analysis.

Image analysis

For image analysis, a combined data frame was created containing single-cell data
only from the cells retrieved during the picking process, along with metadata spe-
cifying the patient sample and the ROI from which the cells originated. This was
achieved by cropping the single-cell data frames generated during image processing
and phenotyping to retain only cells within the selected ROIs, adding the relevant
metadata, and then merging the resulting data frames. The cropping was based on
the x- and y-coordinates of the cells and the coordinates of the ROIs. The coordin-
ates of the ROIs within the cropped images were determined using post-picking image
channels, showing the remaining tissue holes, and the ‘new shape layer’ feature of the
open-source multidimensional image viewer Napari.
The combined data frame served as the basis for quantitative analysis. Compositional
analysis of the picked samples was performed to determine the total number of cells
in each sample, the proportions of tumor, immune, and stroma cells within samples
from tumor, tumor-stroma interface (TSI), and stroma regions as well as the relat-
ive abundances of defined tumor phenotypes within tumor cells in tumor and TSI
region samples and stroma phenotypes within stroma cells in TSI and stroma region
samples. Altogether, this provided an actual insight into sample composition.


 42
Image visualization

The open-source multi-dimensional image viewer Napari (Napari Contributors, 2019)

stroma phenotypes within stroma cells in TSI and stroma region
samples. Altogether, this provided an actual insight into sample composition.


 42
Image visualization

The open-source multi-dimensional image viewer Napari (Napari Contributors, 2019)
was used for visualization throughout the workflow described above. It facilitated
the visualization and combination of different channels while allowing adjustments to
contrast, intensity, opacity, and color to enhance image interpretation. Additionally,
Napari enabled the overlay of segmentation masks onto images, providing a clear
depiction of cell boundaries and aiding in quality control of the cell segmentation
results. Its interactive capabilities were particularly useful for visually inspecting and
validating each processing step, the phenotyping results, and any analysis outputs,
thereby ensuring the accuracy of the whole workflow.

3.2.6 Proteomics analysis

Mass spectrometry analysis

MS analysis of the picked cell samples and subsequent proteomics data processing were
performed by the Turku Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre,
University of Turku. The samples were processed using a bottom-up approach,
following the facility’s established protocols. The used method was data-independent
acquisition shotgun proteomics via LC-MS/MS. The resulting proteomics data was
processed using the Spectronaut software (Biognosys, version 19.2.240905.62635) em-
ploying the direct data-independent acquisition workflow for protein identification.
Label-free quantification was performed using the MaxLFQ algorithm, with normal-
ization applied according to Callister et al., 2006.
The output included a report containing the number of identified proteins of each

kflow for protein identification.
Label-free quantification was performed using the MaxLFQ algorithm, with normal-
ization applied according to Callister et al., 2006.
The output included a report containing the number of identified proteins of each
sample and metadata specifying the analyzed samples as well as a data frame con-
taining all identified proteins, the number of distinct peptides detected per protein in
each sample, and the normalized signal intensities for each protein across all samples.
Both were generated with the Spectronaut software.

Proteomics data analysis

For assessing the proteomics data of both experiments, the software Freestyle was
used. With it the chromatograms of the MS runs were visualized and their quality
assessed. This step allowed for manual inspection and ensured the reliability of the MS
data prior to further analysis. Following this, Spectronaut reports of both experiments
were used to evaluate and compare the number of identified proteins in between

 43
samples with different cell types and picking conditions.
While the analysis of the pilot experiment was limited to those two steps, for the
optimization experiment a comprehensive analysis of the proteomics data was per-
formed. Initially, samples with fewer than 500 identified proteins were excluded due to
low protein yield. The remaining data was then explored using clustering heatmaps,
which grouped samples based on similar protein detection patterns. Subsequently,
differential expression analysis (DEA) was conducted to identify proteins with stat-
istically significant expression. For this analysis, samples were grouped by tissue
region of origin (tumor, TSI, or stroma) and by cell type (proliferating or dormant).

rential expression analysis (DEA) was conducted to identify proteins with stat-
istically significant expression. For this analysis, samples were grouped by tissue
region of origin (tumor, TSI, or stroma) and by cell type (proliferating or dormant).
Differential expression was assessed by comparing proliferation and dormancy pairs
within each tissue region. Proteins with a p -value ă 0.05 and a log2 fold change ą ˘2
were considered differentially expressed.
Finally, DEA results were integrated with image analysis results by correlating dif-
ferentially expressed proteins with the actual proportions of cells exhibiting a pro-
liferative/dormant phenotype within samples. This integrated approach enabled a
comprehensive understanding of the relationship between protein expression profiles
and cellular phenotypes of samples.


3.3 Code availability
Code and scripts used in this project are available at the ProteoPicking repository
on the Färkkilä lab GitHub, University of Helsinki (Färkkilä lab, 2025). Parts of
the image processing pipeline, in particular illumination correction, stitching and
registration, and quantification as well as the script for image visualization in Napari
were conducted using parts of the already established image processing workflow
from the ImageProcessing repository on the Färkkilä lab GitHub (Färkkiä lab, 2024).
The code for the segmentation with Mesmer is adapted from scripts available at
DeepCellApplications repository on the Van Valen lab GitHub (Van Valen Lab, 2023).

4 Results
4.1 Pilot experiment
4.1.1 Experimental design

The pilot experiment was designed to assess the feasibility of ProteoPicking and
explore key factors influencing the number of detected proteins in tissue samples.
In total, 25 samples from three sections and two patients were analyzed. One section
was cut from the FFPE-block of patient C129 and two sections were cut from the
FFPE-block of patient C331. To determine whether t-CycIF staining, particularly
the aggressive fluorophore bleaching step, affects protein abundance within the tissue,
one section from each patient was subjected to standard IF staining (equivalent to
one cycle of t-CycIF staining, but without fluorophore inactivation), while the second
C331 section underwent three full cycles of t-CycIF staining. To investigate a possible
difference in the protein abundance between tumor and stroma regions, ROIs of both
tissue regions were selected from the two C331 tissue sections. Tumor regions were
defined as PAX8+ and further classified based on cell cycle markers, pRB+ for S
phase or pH3+ for M phase. This distinction between tumor cells in different cell
cycle phases was made to make the ROI selection more targeted and assess how
complicated the determination of suitable regions is. Stroma regions were defined
as Vimentin+ and PAX8−. Of the section from patient C129, only tumor region
samples were analyzed. Since the exact protein content per pick was unknown, it was
tested how many picks were necessary for a reliable protein detection. Samples were
collected with 1, 5, 10, and 20 picks from tumor and stroma regions. The prepared
tissue sections were 8 µm thick, whereas standard t-CycIF is typically performed on
4 µm to 5 µm sections.

were necessary for a reliable protein detection. Samples were
collected with 1, 5, 10, and 20 picks from tumor and stroma regions. The prepared
tissue sections were 8 µm thick, whereas standard t-CycIF is typically performed on
4 µm to 5 µm sections. The increased thickness meant that each pick contained more
tissue, reducing the number of picks required to obtain sufficient material for MS
analysis. To optimize sample preparation, two different lysis buffers were compared:
urea and TFE. While all initial samples were prepared in urea, additional 10-pick
tumor samples from both C331 sections were prepared in TFE to compare protein
detection efficiency between the two buffers. To establish a reference for the number of
detectable proteins and to ensure that the IF- and t-CycIF-stained sections contained
any detectable proteins, whole-section lysates were prepared of each tissue section as
positive controls.

4.1.2 IF and t-CycIF staining enables the characterization of cell types
 and tissue regions

One of the three sample sections underwent three cycles of t-CycIF staining, while
the other two sections were stained using standard IF staining, which corresponds
to one cycle of t-CycIF staining without a fluorophore inactivation step. For IF as
well as t-CycIF the protocol was followed as described in the method section. The
antibodies for each cycle and the respective used concentrations are shown in Table 6.
The IF samples were stained with antibodies of the third cycle only. For the staining,
an antibody panel was chosen that was already validated in earlier projects of the
lab. It consisted of the following biomarkers: the tumor marker PAX, the stroma
marker Vimentin, the immune markers CD45 and Iba1, and cell cycle markers Ki67,
Cyclin D1, pRb, MPM2, pH3, and p21.

that was already validated in earlier projects of the

As described earlier, the stained tissues were thicker than those usually used in
t-CycIF experiments. Nevertheless, nearly all used markers resulted in clear stain-
ing patterns with sufficient signal-to-noise ratios, as shown in Figure 5B. Only the
staining of CD45 of the t-CycIF sample displays a noisier background than the other
markers. However, it was still possible to distinguish positive from negative cells,
especially in combination with Iba1, the second immune cell marker used in this pro-
ject. Both t-CycIF and IF staining results provided clear characterization of tumor
and stroma areas and enabled a distinction between mitotic tumor cells and tumor
cells in S phase.
Based on nuclear staining with Hoechst, images of cycles one to three of the t-CycIF
sample section in addition to the image acquired after the picking process were
accurately stitched with no overlapping or missing areas. This additional fourth cycle
enabled the exact tracking of the picked tissue regions. There were no tissue shifts,
detachments, or deformations in the images that would interfere with ROI selection
or other following steps.

4.1.4 Proteomics results demonstrate compatibility of t-CycIF with MS-
 based proteomics analysis

Initial evaluation of the MS-derived proteomics data was performed using Freestyle,
a software tool for visualizing chromatograms and corresponding spectra. Inspec-
tion of the chromatograms confirmed successful detection of peptide-derived signals
across all runs, indicating that protein digestion and MS acquisition were success-
ful. Peptide identification was further supported by spectral peaks with charges of
+2, which are characteristic for tryptic peptides generated during sample prepara-
tion (Steen & Mann, 2004). However, in addition to expected peptide signals, several
non-peptide impurities were consistently detected in all samples. These included
prominent keratin and polymer contaminants, both of which can interfere with pro-
tein detection and quantification. These impurities were identified in collaboration
with the Turku Proteomics Facility and were primarily characterized by a charge
state of +1 in the mass spectra. Keratin-related peaks appeared at earlier retention
times, while polymer-associated signals were detected at later stages of the chromato-
graphic runs. Representative chromatograms and mass spectra from one tumor and
one stroma sample are shown in Figure 7A, with highlighted regions indicating areas
of contamination.

r-associated signals were detected at later stages of the chromato-
graphic runs. Representative chromatograms and mass spectra from one tumor and
one stroma sample are shown in Figure 7A, with highlighted regions indicating areas
of contamination.

Following chromatographic assessment, the number of identified proteins per sample
was extracted from the generated Spectronaut analysis reports. Figure 7B, C, and
D depict comparisons of the number of identified proteins within different samples.
Despite variability across all samples, some trends were observed.
Firstly, the number of identified proteins was comparable between samples stained

 50
with IF and those stained with t-CycIF, indicating that the additional staining and
bleaching steps in t-CycIF did not markedly impair protein detection. Moreover, a
general trend was observed that showed that samples with a higher number of picked
regions contained more identifiable proteins. Samples with only one pick generally
yielded low protein counts and showed considerable variability. With 5 or 10 picks per
sample, the number of identified proteins increased but remained variable between
samples. For 20 pick samples, protein detection tended to be more consistent and
frequently achieved a high protein yield relative to the rest of the dataset. This
highlights that larger pick numbers generally improved reliability and proteome cov-
erage. However, several 5 and 10 pick samples also reached protein counts that were
sufficiently high to provide meaningful proteomics profiles, making them suitable for
downstream analysis despite their smaller input size.
Then, tumor and stroma samples with equal numbers of picks per sample were com-
pared. In general, tumor-derived samples tended to yield higher numbers of iden-

profiles, making them suitable for
downstream analysis despite their smaller input size.
Then, tumor and stroma samples with equal numbers of picks per sample were com-
pared. In general, tumor-derived samples tended to yield higher numbers of iden-
tified proteins than stroma-derived ones. The only exception was observed in the
one-pick samples, where stroma-derived samples showed higher protein counts than
their tumor-derived counterparts. However, one-pick samples also displayed consid-
erable variability overall, and this observation may reflect an outlier rather than a
consistent trend.
To further evaluate protocol performance, a comparison was conducted between 10
pick tumor samples processed with urea buffer versus those processed with TFE.
Overall, the number of proteins identified was comparable between the two buffer
types. However, the IF sample processed with TFE resulted in a slightly higher
number of detected proteins than the one processed with urea buffer. In contrast, the
t-CycIF sample processed with urea yielded a substantially higher number of proteins
than its TFE-processed counterpart.
Lastly, the number of identified proteins of the post-picking scrape samples was com-
pared with the protein yield of all small region samples. As expected, these whole-
tissue lysates yielded significantly higher protein counts, ranging between 6,000 and
7,000 identified proteins. This difference reflects the substantially greater amount
of input material, which increases the likelihood of detecting low-abundance proteins
that may fall below the detection threshold in smaller samples. All in all, this compar-
ison illustrates the expected difference in depth of proteome coverage when analyzing
small, spatially restricted regions versus entire tissue sections.

4.2 Optimization experiment
4.2.1 Experimental design

The optimization experiment was designed with the goal to improve the experi-
mental protocol, in particular with regard to minimizing any kind of contamination.
Moreover, it was constructed that based on the generated data, an analysis pipeline
could be developed for analyzing image and proteomics data of various samples in a
comparative way.
To avoid contaminants as far as possible, especially from polymeric materials and
exogenous keratin sources like skin flakes, hair, and dust, several precautionary meas-
ures were implemented in the workflow. Glass laboratory ware was consistently used
wherever possible. Prior to each experimental step, surfaces, instruments, and tools
were thoroughly cleaned. Only newly prepared or unopened reagents and solutions
were utilized, with all solutions and buffers stored in glass bottles to ensure no con-
tamination from plastic particles potentially migrating into the solutions. Finally,

e thoroughly cleaned. Only newly prepared or unopened reagents and solutions
were utilized, with all solutions and buffers stored in glass bottles to ensure no con-
tamination from plastic particles potentially migrating into the solutions. Finally,
consumables such as microscope slides and coverslips were taken from newly opened
packages to further limit contamination risks. To monitor possible contaminants in-
troduced by the microscope slide alone and contamination caused by the t-CycIF
staining, two picking samples were taken from empty microscope slides as negative
controls. One of those slides underwent the whole t-CycIF staining process, sample 0,
while the other was freshly taken out of the package before the picking was performed,
sample 00.
In addition to the two negative controls, 42 samples from six different patients, seven
samples per patient, were analyzed. To improve comparability despite the inherent
heterogeneity of HGSC, patient samples were selected based on uniform treatment,
NACT with IDS, and consistent HR status, HRD. Additionally, tissue composition
of the patient samples was checked with previous image analysis data to ensure that
enough suitable picking regions matching the objective of this project were present.
Based on the results of the pilot project, it was decided to cut 10 µm sections and com-
bine 7 picks per sample in urea buffer, which approximately corresponds to the tissue
amount of 10 picks from 8 µm sections. Before the sample picking, each section under-
went two cycles of t-CycIF staining with the tumor marker PAX8, immune markers
CD45 and Iba1, the stroma marker Vimentin, and the cell cycle markers Ki67, pRb,
and p21. These biomarkers were selected to enable the distinction between tumor,

4.2.2 t-CycIF staining provides tissue and cellular characterization, en-
 abling ROI selection

During this experiment, all tissue sections were stained with two cycles of t-CycIF
staining. For this, 10 µm sections were used and a slightly adjusted t-CycIF protocol
was followed, incorporating the described precautionary steps to minimize contam-
ination. Again, an antibody panel was used that had already been validated during
earlier projects of the lab. It comprised tumor, stroma, immune, and cell cycle mark-
ers.
Even with thicker tissue sections than during the pilot project and minor protocol
changes, all used markers displayed a clear staining pattern with little background


 56
noise, as shown in Figure 8B. Based on the nuclear staining channels, the stitching and
registration of both cycles and the post-picking images were successful. The resulting
multichannel images were accurately overlaid, showing no overlaps or missing areas.
ROIs were identified using the images of the stained tissue. During the picking,
the second staining cycle provided guidance for identifying pre-selected ROIs and
validating expected marker combinations. The positive and negative marker combi-
nations used for identifying cells and regions of interest are shown in Figure 9 together
with examples of selected areas for tumor, TSI, and stroma samples. Based on tissue
structure, revealed by nuclear staining and staining with PAX8 and Vimentin, tumor,
TSI, and stroma areas were determined. ROIs for tumor samples were selected to

er
with examples of selected areas for tumor, TSI, and stroma samples. Based on tissue
structure, revealed by nuclear staining and staining with PAX8 and Vimentin, tumor,
TSI, and stroma areas were determined. ROIs for tumor samples were selected to
be predominantly surrounded by tumor cells. In contrast, TSI ROIs were chosen at
the border of tumor and stroma regions, encompassing a mixture of both cell types.
Finally, stroma ROIs were confined to areas exclusively surrounded by stroma cells,
avoiding proximity to tumor regions. In general, ROIs for one sample were selected
from the same tissue region. The specific cell selection was made based on absence or
presence of proliferation marker Ki67, cell cycle arrest marker p21, and pRb in tumor
and stroma cells. Noteworthy, the selection of tumor and TSI ROIs was based on

guided the stroma ROI selection.
During ROI selection, particular care was taken with two patient samples, C369 and
C758, which showed minor tissue detachment at the borders. Although these areas
were limited and did not impact the overall analysis, ROIs were deliberately placed
away from these detached regions.

4.2.3 Image segmentation was achieved by combining two image enhance-
 ment methods with the Mesmer nuclear segmentation model

Next step in the image processing pipeline, after registration and stitching, is segmen-
tation. The laboratory’s standard segmentation pipeline typically utilizes the Stardist
model (Schmidt et al., 2018) for nuclear segmentation. However, its performance on
this dataset was suboptimal. Stardist failed to segment a substantial portion of cells
accurately. This was likely due to the higher cell density and overlap in the images,

(Schmidt et al., 2018) for nuclear segmentation. However, its performance on
this dataset was suboptimal. Stardist failed to segment a substantial portion of cells
accurately. This was likely due to the higher cell density and overlap in the images,
caused by the use of thicker tissue sections compared to those commonly used in
standard t-CycIF experiments.
To address these challenges, two additional segmentation models, Cellpose (Stringer
et al., 2021) and Mesmer (Greenwald et al., 2022), were evaluated. Both models were
specifically developed for biomedical image segmentation and were selected based on
their performance in a recent benchmarking study comparing state-of-the-art compu-

ranked lower in most categories but showed particular strength in scalability. In con-

ance,
placing near the middle of the ranked methods. Due to considerations of time ef-
ficiency, the comparison was conducted on a representative image crop rather than
whole-slide images. As illustrated in Figure 10A, Mesmer outperformed both Star-
dist and Cellpose, successfully segmenting the majority of cells visible in the sample.
Notably, Cellpose performed the poorest, primarily segmenting well-separated cells
but struggling with densely clustered cell regions - an outcome that contrasts with
its strong performance reported in the benchmarking study. Based on these results,
Mesmer was selected as the primary segmentation tool for subsequent analysis.

However, despite the improved segmentation performance, Mesmer’s segmentation
remained imperfect, in particular for cells exhibiting low signal intensity, indistinct
boundaries, or located in less focused image planes. To further enhance segmentation
accuracy, image processing techniques aimed at improving intensity and contrast were
implemented prior to segmentation. Four methods were evaluated: contrast limited
adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), gamma correction, unsharp masking, and
Richardson-Lucy deconvolution.
CLAHE enhances local contrast in an image by improving the visibility of details
within small regions. It works by dividing the image into non-overlapping tiles, the
size of which is defined by the kernel size parameter, where smaller kernels create
more tiles and allow for more localized contrast adjustments. Within each tile, a
pixel intensity histogram is computed and redistributed into a fixed number of bins,
which determines the resolution of intensity levels used. To prevent excessive contrast
amplification and noise, a clip limit restricts the maximum height of histogram bins -

nsity histogram is computed and redistributed into a fixed number of bins,
which determines the resolution of intensity levels used. To prevent excessive contrast
amplification and noise, a clip limit restricts the maximum height of histogram bins -
any counts above this limit are clipped and then evenly redistributed across all bins
(Zuiderveld et al., 1994).
In contrast, gamma correction is a method that modifies image brightness through a
nonlinear adjustment of pixel values by raising them to the power of γ. For γ ă 1,
darker pixels are brightened, enhancing contrast and revealing details in shadowed
regions. Conversely, for γ ą 1, the image darkens, compressing highlights and in-
creasing contrast in bright areas.
Unsharp masking enhances image sharpness by subtracting a blurred (unsharp) ver-
sion of the image from the original, which increases the contrast of edges and fine
details. The blurring is performed using a Gaussian filter, where the parameter σ


 59
defines the standard deviation of the Gaussian function and thus controls the spread
of the blur. A larger σ results in a smoother, more widely spread blur. How much
of the blurred image is subtracted from the original is determined with the strength
parameter. This parameter acts as a scaling factor that controls the intensity of the
sharpening effect: higher strength values increase the contribution of the blurred im-
age subtraction, leading to more pronounced edge enhancement, while lower values
produce a subtler sharpening effect (Gonzalez & Woods, 2018).
Lastly, Richardson-Lucy deconvolution is a method that aims to reduce image blur
and sharpen details. It relies on a point spread function, which models how the image
was blurred during imaging. Here, a Gaussian function with standard deviation σ

).
Lastly, Richardson-Lucy deconvolution is a method that aims to reduce image blur
and sharpen details. It relies on a point spread function, which models how the image
was blurred during imaging. Here, a Gaussian function with standard deviation σ
was used to represent the blur level. The process is repeated for a set number of
iterations, where more iterations lead to a clearer, sharper image but also require
more processing time (Lucy, 1974; Richardson, 1972).
Parameter values for each image processing method were determined through em-
pirical testing This involved the visual assessment of Mesmer segmentation results
on images processed with the different enhancement methods and various para-
meter combinations to ultimately identify those yielding the most accurate segment-
ation appearance. The optimized parameter settings were as follows: CLAHE with
kernel size “ 8 ˆ 8, bin number “ 200, and clip limit “ 0.03, gamma correction with
γ “ 2{3, unsharp masking with σ “ 1.0 and strength “ 2.5, and Richardson-Lucy
deconvolution with σ “ 1.0 and 30 iterations.
The final Mesmer segmentation results using these optimized parameters are presen-
ted in Figure 10B. The different enhancement approaches showed only marginal dif-
ferences in performance. While CLAHE combined with Mesmer performed similarly
to Mesmer alone, likely because Mesmer’s internal workflow already incorporates
a CLAHE step, gamma correction, unsharp masking, and Richardson-Lucy decon-
volution achieved slightly better segmentation accuracy than Mesmer alone. Ulti-
mately, computational efficiency alongside accuracy guided the choice of enhance-
ment method. Although Richardson-Lucy improved image sharpness with additional

decon-
volution achieved slightly better segmentation accuracy than Mesmer alone. Ulti-
mately, computational efficiency alongside accuracy guided the choice of enhance-
ment method. Although Richardson-Lucy improved image sharpness with additional
iterations, the increased computational time did not proportionally translate into im-
proved segmentation. In contrast, gamma correction and unsharp masking provided
a balance of enhanced segmentation performance and faster processing times. There-
fore, a combination of both was adopted, leveraging their complementary effects -
gamma correction brightens darker regions to improve intensity, while unsharp mask-
ing enhances image sharpness. This combination yielded a satisfactory segmentation
accuracy, as demonstrated in Figure 10C.

to adapt the workflow for whole-slide images. The Mesmer model, adapted from the

f gamma correction and unsharp
masking with the nuclear segmentation model Mesmer.


Following the segmentation optimization performed on image crops, efforts were made
to adapt the workflow for whole-slide images. The Mesmer model, adapted from the
Van Valen Lab’s DeepCell Applications repository GitHub, was unable to process
large tissue areas directly. Therefore, whole-slide images were tiled prior to segment-


 61
ation, with each tile being segmented separately and subsequently being reassembled.
A workflow incorporating these steps was developed and operated as intended; how-
ever, three major issues arose:
First, the computational runtime required for segmenting a single whole-slide image
was very long, exceeding more than three days, rendering this approach impractical
for processing multiple samples within a reasonable time frame. Second, the resulting
cell masks lacked unique identifiers - individual cell IDs, which typically distinguish
cells from one another, were partly assigned to multiple cells. This overlap would
have posed significant challenges for downstream processing and analysis. Lastly,
although segmentation of the tissue and regions surrounding it generally achieved
satisfactory accuracy, the image borders, black background areas completely devoid
of cells, exhibited extensive over-segmentation, generating numerous near pixel-sized
masks, which would have complicated downstream analysis.
Due to these limitations, whole-slide image segmentation was deemed unsuccessful.
As a practical alternative, rectangular regions encompassing all selected ROIs for
each sample were extracted from the whole-slide images. A total of six small crops
were extracted from each tissue section, two each from tumor, TSI, and stroma re-

.
As a practical alternative, rectangular regions encompassing all selected ROIs for
each sample were extracted from the whole-slide images. A total of six small crops
were extracted from each tissue section, two each from tumor, TSI, and stroma re-
gions, with one representing a proliferating/non-dormant area and one a dormant
area, resulting in 36 crops overall. All subsequent processing and analysis steps were
conducted using these small crops of the whole slide images rather than the entire
tissue section images.

4.2.4 Quantification and phenotyping enable single-cell characterization
 of cells within ROIs

To obtain detailed single-cell information, small crops containing selected ROIs were
quantified and phenotyped. Cells located outside of ROIs were excluded from further
analysis to specifically focus on those captured during the tissue picking process. Cell
type annotations were based on seven markers (see Figure 11A) and phenotyping was
performed using the Scimap toolkit (see chapter Image processing, phenotyping, ana-
lysis, and visualization). The accuracy of this classification was thoroughly validated
through visual inspection with a customized Napari application, which enabled over-
laying cell annotation masks onto the t-CycIF image crops. By visualizing multiple
markers simultaneously, different cell types were clearly distinguished. The assigned
phenotypes corresponded well with expected marker profiles, as demonstrated in Fig-
ure 11B.
The tumor marker PAX8 served as the primary marker to distinguish tumor cells
from non-tumor populations. Both stroma and immune cells were identified based on

In total, the 3847 cells located within ROIs were identified and phenotyped in the
described way. Of these, 1,722 (44.8 %) were classified as tumor cells, 727 (18.9 %) as
immune cells, and 1,398 (36.3 %) as stroma cells. Their exact distribution between
the different phenotypes is shown in Figure 11B.
In conclusion, the applied phenotyping approach enabled accurate and spatially re-
solved identification of diverse cell types and functional states within the picked tissue
regions, laying the foundation for detailed downstream analysis.

4.2.5 Compositional analysis facilitates accurate sample categorization

Building on the phenotyping results, a compositional analysis was performed to eval-
uate the cellular makeup of each individual sample. This allowed for an overview of
how successfully different cell types were targeted across picked regions and how their
relative abundances varied by tissue context and cell state.

Samples were categorized based on both the tissue region they were picked from,
tumor, TSI, and stroma as well as the cellular state that was targeted, proliferating
or dormant for tumor and TSI, and non-dormant or dormant for stroma. This yielded
six distinct groups of samples, organized into three main categories based on the region
of origin of the picked cells. Each was comprised of two subgroups based on cellular
state, enabling structured comparison across biological conditions.
As shown in Figure 12A, the distribution of the three major cell types, tumor, stroma,
and immune cells, varied noticeably across sample groups. Tumor samples were gen-
erally enriched in tumor cells, with the majority of samples containing more than
50 %. Nonetheless, all tumor samples also included a notable fraction of stroma and
immune cells. As intended during ROI selection, TSI samples consistently contained
a mixture of tumor, stroma, and immune cells. The relative abundance of these
populations varied substantially across samples: tumor cell content ranged from ap-
proximately 20 % to nearly 70 %, stroma cells from around 15 % to over 50 %, and
immune cells from below 10 % to roughly 50 %. Compared to tumor and stroma
samples the cellular composition of TSI samples exhibited the greatest compositional
heterogeneity. Stroma samples demonstrated the highest specificity. All samples

4.2.6 Generated proteomics data of picked samples enables development
 and application of an integrated analysis pipeline combining protein

Building on the validated sample composition, proteomics analysis was performed to
evaluate the molecular profiles of the enriched cellular states.
First, similar to the pilot experiment, a chromatographic evaluation of the MS runs
was conducted using Freestyle. Compared to the pilot experiment, a markedly lower
number of overall signals was observed in the chromatograms across all runs of all
picked samples, see Figure 13A. While a certain reduction was expected and favorable,
indicating successful implementation of improved sample preparation protocols for
reducing impurities, it also reflects a general decrease in peptide-derived signals across
all sample types. This was consistent in all types of picked samples, proliferating,
non-dormant, and dormant samples from tumor, TSI, and stroma regions.
Subsequent analysis using Spectronaut confirmed the limited protein detection. Across
all picked samples, the number of identified proteins ranged from fewer than 200 up
to 1400-1500 proteins, with a substantial proportion of samples falling below 1000
identified proteins, as shown in Figure 13B. In this experiment, 7 pick samples from
10 µm tissue sections were analyzed, which is comparable to the 10 pick samples from
8 µm sections in the pilot study. Although the pilot dataset was small, with only
seven 10-pick samples in total, and showed considerable variability, most samples in
that study had protein yields between 1000-3000 proteins, with two outliers below
this range.

ons in the pilot study. Although the pilot dataset was small, with only
seven 10-pick samples in total, and showed considerable variability, most samples in
that study had protein yields between 1000-3000 proteins, with two outliers below
this range. Therefore, based on those results, a higher protein yield would have been
anticipated in the current samples.
