Following the clustering analysis, a DEA was conducted to compare protein abund-
ance between sample categories. For this, samples were grouped first by tissue region
(tumor, TSI, and stroma), and within each region further subdivided by cellular state
(proliferating/non-dormant vs. dormant).
This analysis compares protein intensities between two sample categories using un-
paired two-sided t-tests to assess statistical differences in expression levels. Due to
the requirements of this test, only proteins detected in both groups and in more than
one sample per group were included.

between two sample categories using un-
paired two-sided t-tests to assess statistical differences in expression levels. Due to
the requirements of this test, only proteins detected in both groups and in more than
one sample per group were included. This is essential, as statistical comparisons such
as the t-test require numerical input from each group to calculate differences. In
MS data, the absence of detection does not represent a true zero value and therefore
cannot be substituted into the analysis. Additionally, proteins detected in only a
single sample within one group may reflect random or technical variation rather than
a consistent biological signal. Including such proteins would risk introducing noise
rather than capturing meaningful differences. These filtering criteria ensure that the
analysis is based on consistent, quantifiable data rather than isolated or potentially
artifactual detections.
As a result of these criteria, many proteins were excluded from the DEA, especially
those detected exclusively in one group. This is a notable limitation, particularly given
the earlier heatmap results (Figure 14A), which suggested that certain proteins are
detected almost exclusively in proliferating or dormant samples. These group-specific
proteins, although excluded from DEA, were visualized separately in heatmaps in
Figure 14B, where log10 -transformed intensities are shown for proteins detected in
only one group in more than one sample. While they were not formally tested for
differential expression due to statistical constraints, they represent a set of biologically
interesting proteins that demand further investigation beyond this thesis’s scope.

To address the issue of false positives due to multiple hypothesis testing, the false dis-
covery rate correction was applied using the Benjamini-Hochberg method. However,
all adjusted q -values exceeded 0.05, indicating no proteins remained statistically sig-
nificant after correction. However, given the small sample sizes and the exploratory
nature of this study, primarily focused on establishing a functional analysis pipeline,
further analysis steps were based on unadjusted p -values. While this approach does
not satisfy stringent statistical criteria, it can be accepted since the main goal is
method development rather than definitive biomarker discovery.

Overall, the analysis presented here establishes and applies a comprehensive proteo-
mics pipeline that integrates spatially resolved protein expression data with cellular
composition measurements. This pipeline encompasses chromatographic quality as-
sessment, protein identification and quantification using Spectronaut, visualization
of expression patterns, DEA, and integration with spatial cellular composition via
correlation analysis.

5 Discussion
This study demonstrates the successful integration of t-CycIF staining with MS-based
proteomics and establishes a functioning experimental workflow and a robust data
analysis pipeline for spatially resolved tissue analysis. The combined approach en-
ables detailed cellular and tissue characterization while providing proteomics insights
from precisely selected ROIs.

t-CycIF staining & subsequent ROI selection

A key step in the project is the t-CycIF staining. In particular, it was important
to demonstrate that the staining protocol works effectively with thicker tissue sec-
tions, up to 10 µm, than those typically used in t-CycIF experiments. Despite the
increased thickness, nearly all antibodies showed clear staining patterns with good
signal-to-noise ratios, indicating that the protocol is robust under these conditions.
However, re-validating some antibody markers originally optimized for thinner sec-
tions on thicker tissues could further improve staining quality and ensure reliable
interpretation.
Based on the successful t-CycIF staining, the ROI selection and the subsequent pick-
ing were effectively guided. The staining facilitated the identification of key tissue

es could further improve staining quality and ensure reliable
interpretation.
Based on the successful t-CycIF staining, the ROI selection and the subsequent pick-
ing were effectively guided. The staining facilitated the identification of key tissue
regions, including tumor, TSI, and stroma as well as the further characterization of
various cells. A notable advantage of the approach was the inclusion of biomarkers
relevant for ROI selection in the final staining cycle, which permitted real-time val-
idation and adjustment during the picking process of the pre-selected ROIs. This
strategy enhanced the accuracy of targeting specific regions and cell types, improving
the overall reliability of spatially resolved sampling. However, identifying suitable
ROIs was still challenging, especially when the target cell populations were scarce or
unevenly distributed within the tissue. In such cases, locating regions with sufficient
representation of the desired biomarkers required increased effort and careful exam-
ination. To address this limitation, it is recommended to utilize any available prior
staining data or histological information from the tissue samples before starting with
the experiment. Pre-assessing tissue characteristics allows for informed selection of
samples that are more likely to contain regions exhibiting the phenotypes of interest,
thereby increasing the efficiency and success rate of ROI targeting. It is also import-
ant to keep in mind that some cell types or marker-defined populations are naturally
rare or unevenly distributed. This variability should be considered when designing
research questions and planning future experiments using ProteoPicking, as it affects
how easily these cells can be isolated and how the obtained data can be interpreted.

Image processing & phenotyping

Following t-CycIF staining, ROI selection, and sample picking, the next essential step
was the establishment of a robust image processing pipeline.
As mentioned before, illumination correction, stitching and registration of the images
were successful. The pre-established BaSiC and ASHLAR workflows from the Färkkilä
GitHub ImageProcessing repository work not only for the usually used 4 µm to 5 µm
tissue section images, but also for the thicker ones used for this project. Thus, they
were implemented into the image processing pipeline of ProteoPicking without fur-
ther problems.

The next critical component of the processing step was the segmentation of the im-
ages, which needed to accurately delineate individual cells within especially densely
clustered regions due to the higher thickness of the imaged tissue sections. Among the
segmentation models tested, Mesmer demonstrated superior performance in handling
the images. Its effectiveness was further enhanced by applying image pre-processing
steps, in particular gamma correction and unsharp masking, which improved image
intensity and contrast, enabling more accurate nuclear segmentation. Based on these
results, gamma correction, unsharp masking, and Mesmer were combined and success-
fully integrated into the processing pipeline to segment small image crops containing
the selected ROIs.
Despite all advancements, the current segmentation workflow exhibits limitations and
considerable room for refinement.

were combined and success-
fully integrated into the processing pipeline to segment small image crops containing
the selected ROIs.
Despite all advancements, the current segmentation workflow exhibits limitations and
considerable room for refinement. Future efforts should focus on a systematic, quant-
itative evaluation of various image enhancement techniques and combinations of them
to objectively assess their individual and combined impact on segmentation accuracy.
In addition, extensive objective and quantified parameter optimization is needed to
further improve model performance. Based on evaluation and parameter optimiza-
tion, the most effective and computationally efficient techniques should be implemen-
ted to improve the current performance of the method. Moreover, as a measure to
address the current limitation to small image crops, adapting the workflow for whole-
slide image segmentation would be a sensible next step. At present, the developed
workflow for whole-slide image segmentation is computationally intensive, has a long
running time, and poses the problem of over-segmentation of the image background.
Solving these problems and optimizing the workflow, for example by evaluating op-
timal tile size, number, and overlap, would significantly improve the scalability and
facilitate broader application of the pipeline in future projects. Additionally, it would
allow for whole-slide quantification and phenotyping, enabling more comprehensive


 78
analysis of the picked ROIs, especially with regard to their large-scale spatial context.

With individual cells segmented, the next step involved quantification and phen-
otyping to characterize the cellular makeup of the picked tissue regions in detail.

With individual cells segmented, the next step involved quantification and phen-
otyping to characterize the cellular makeup of the picked tissue regions in detail.
Phenotyping, although time-consuming due to the need for manual verification, was
crucial to accurately identify cell types and functional states based on marker ex-
pression. Beyond its role in analysis, phenotyping also served as a form of quality
control, providing an overview of the total cell counts and phenotypic distributions
across all samples. Thereby, it offers early indications of how successfully targeted
phenotypes were captured as well as the extent of non-target cell inclusion. During
the optimization project, the phenotyping results already served as a first represent-
ation of the picking outcome. Phenotyping results showed that nearly 20 % of all
isolated cells were immune cells, even though in the picking process they were not
targeted and rather tried to avoid being picked. Moreover, it revealed that while the
majority of picked dormant stroma cells belonged to the targeted stricter dormant
phenotype, Ki67−pRb−p21+, two thirds of the dormant tumor cells were part of the
less strict phenotype. These results could be due to various reasons. The total pheno-
type distribution could be influenced by individual samples, whose ROI selection and
picking were not sufficiently targeted, despite prior control based on the captured
tissue images and real-time monitoring during the picking process. Another reason
for the displayed distribution could be the inherent composition and structure of the
used HGSC samples with their given immune infiltration and possible differences in
dormant tumor and stroma cells.

toring during the picking process. Another reason
for the displayed distribution could be the inherent composition and structure of the
used HGSC samples with their given immune infiltration and possible differences in
dormant tumor and stroma cells. Lastly, it is important to consider that during the
picking process not only the targeted cells alone are isolated, but also all cells within
the 40 µm needle diameter. If ROIs are not evaluated thoroughly beforehand, this can
lead to the isolation of non-targeted cells in addition to the targeted ones, contribut-
ing to sample heterogeneity. However, to make more accurate assumptions about the
distribution of the various phenotypes, the cell composition of each individual sample
needs to be considered, as was done during the compositional analysis.

Image analysis & sample composition

While phenotyping provides a global overview, detailed, sample-wise compositional
analysis was necessary to determine whether the composition of individual ROIs truly
reflected the intended targets and to break down the global cellular distribution in
greater depth.
The analysis showed that tumor and stroma cells were distributed as expected based


 79
on ROI locations. That means that tumor samples mainly consisted of tumor cells,
stroma samples mainly of stroma cells, and TSI samples were a mix of tumor and
stroma cells, This confirms the overall success of the picking strategy. However,
despite efforts to avoid immune cell-rich areas, immune cells were detected in all
samples. This supports the assumption that the significant amount of immune cells in
the global sample composition could be due to the inherent character of the analyzed
tissues. In general, the TME is often highly infiltrated by immune cells, which could

s. This supports the assumption that the significant amount of immune cells in
the global sample composition could be due to the inherent character of the analyzed
tissues. In general, the TME is often highly infiltrated by immune cells, which could
make their complete exclusion from samples taken from tumor tissues very difficult or
even nearly impossible. Comparing the composition of main cell types in the samples
derived from the different tissue regions, it became evident that stroma samples were
well targeted, with very little tumor contamination. In contrast, tumor samples con-
sistently included, next to the unavoidable immune cells, stroma cells, which may
be attributed to the complex, slit-like architecture characteristic for HGSC, making
clean separation of tumor cells particularly challenging. For TSI samples, a mixed
cellular composition was expected due to their location at the tumor-stroma interface,
and this was consistently reflected in the observed data.

Examining the distribution of phenotypes within tumor and stroma populations re-
vealed further trends. In proliferating, respectively non-dormant samples, the pro-
portion of proliferating or non-dormant cells was generally higher than the proportion
of dormant cells in the corresponding dormant samples. This likely reflects practical
challenges. Regions enriched in proliferating or non-dormant cells were more abund-
ant and spatially clustered, facilitating easier targeted picking. In contrast, dormant
cells appeared less clustered and harder to isolate, requiring less narrow selection
criteria that may have reduced sample purity. Although this observation hints at
possible biological differences in the spatial organization of proliferating/non-dormant

appeared less clustered and harder to isolate, requiring less narrow selection
criteria that may have reduced sample purity. Although this observation hints at
possible biological differences in the spatial organization of proliferating/non-dormant
and dormant cells, further investigation, such as spatial neighborhood analysis, would
be necessary to support such conclusions, especially given the limited sample size.
When comparing the two dormancy phenotypes defined in this study, stroma samples
generally exhibited a higher proportion of the stricter dormancy phenotype compared
to tumor and TSI samples. Whether this difference reflects true biological variation or
sampling constraints remains unclear. Similarly, the co-occurrence of dormant tumor
and stroma cells in several TSI regions raises the possibility of spatial associations
between cell states. However, this observation remains speculative and would require
further targeted investigation.

All in all, this underlines the importance of spatial validation of the sample com-


 80
position in this workflow. Reviewing imaging data from all staining cycles can con-
firm whether picked regions truly reflect their intended content. Thereby, it can be
determined whether samples can retain their original pre-picking labels or require
reassignment or exclusion, as done during the optimization experiment, ultimately
enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the dataset.

Proteomics data exploration

Following spatial validation of sample composition, the quality and reliability of
proteomics data from picked tissue regions were evaluated to identify feasibility as
well as strengths and limitations of the current workflow, and to guide future im-
provements.

tial validation of sample composition, the quality and reliability of
proteomics data from picked tissue regions were evaluated to identify feasibility as
well as strengths and limitations of the current workflow, and to guide future im-
provements.
The pilot proteomics experiment demonstrated that t-CycIF staining is compatible
with MS-based proteomics, yielding meaningful protein identification even from small,
spatially defined samples. Although some contamination from keratin and poly-
mers was detected, slightly impacting protein detection, the overall success of pro-
tein digestion and MS acquisition was confirmed through consistent peptide signals
and charge states characteristic for tryptic peptides. Importantly, t-CycIF-stained
samples showed comparable protein yields to those stained with IF, indicating that
additional staining and bleaching steps did not markedly reduce proteome quality,
which further solidifies the feasibility of ProteoPicking.
A positive correlation between the number of picked regions per sample and the depth
of proteome coverage was observed. Samples with fewer than ten picks displayed high
variability and lower protein counts, whereas those with ten or twenty picks generally
achieved greater consistency and higher protein yields. This suggests that increasing
the number of picks can enhance data robustness, though several lower pick samples
still provided sufficient protein yields. Additionally, tumor-derived samples tended
to yield higher protein counts than stroma samples, potentially reflecting biological
differences such as higher metabolic activity and protein abundance in tumor cells,
or a difference in the extraction efficiency between the tissue types.
Buffer choice also influenced protein yield.

stroma samples, potentially reflecting biological
differences such as higher metabolic activity and protein abundance in tumor cells,
or a difference in the extraction efficiency between the tissue types.
Buffer choice also influenced protein yield. Although limited by sample size, urea
buffer performed slightly better than TFE, especially in t-CycIF-stained samples.
This fact, together with urea’s lower hazard profile, makes urea buffer preferable as a
lysis buffer for future experiments. Finally, whole-tissue lysates yielded substantially
higher protein counts compared to spatially restricted samples, underscoring the in-
trinsic limitation of proteome depth when analyzing small regions. This reflects the
reduced total protein quantity in small picks rather than methodological failure.


 81
In the subsequent optimization experiment, improvements in sample preparation suc-
cessfully reduced contaminating impurities, as indicated by chromatograms show-
ing fewer non-peptide signals. But in addition, an unexpected overall reduction in
peptide-derived signals was observed. Therefore, it is not completely clear if the re-
duction in signals derived from contaminants was caused by implemented protocol
changes or just by the general lower detection rate.
The observed reduction in protein yield compared to the pilot experiment remains in-
completely explained but likely arises from one or multiple of the differences between
the two projects. One difference was the methodological adjustments between the two
experiments. They were generally minor and aimed to improve sample quality and
minimize contamination. Based on the conducted protocol changes, it is unlikely that
they are responsible, at least not solely, for the substantial decrease in protein identi-

iments. They were generally minor and aimed to improve sample quality and
minimize contamination. Based on the conducted protocol changes, it is unlikely that
they are responsible, at least not solely, for the substantial decrease in protein identi-
fication count in the picked samples. Another difference was technical problems with
the CyteFinder II during the picking process of the optimization experiment. Even
though a workaround was found, some issues during the picking or sample depositing
may have persisted and could have caused the lower protein yield. Lastly, a further
critical difference was the extended storage duration of samples prior to MS analysis.
In the pilot study, samples were stored at ´80 ˝ C for approximately two to three
weeks before processing. In contrast, the samples of the optimization experiment
had a storage period exceeding two months. Although frozen storage should theor-
etically preserve protein integrity, the combination of very low sample volumes and
protein concentrations raises concerns about potential degradation, buffer evapora-
tion, or adhesion of proteins to tube walls over prolonged periods. Such effects, while
not definitively demonstrated here, could contribute to diminished protein recovery
and detection sensitivity. Importantly, full-section tissue lysate controls processed
alongside the small picks exhibited robust protein yields, indicating that the general
workflow, t-CycIF staining, extraction, and MS protocols remained effective. This
suggests that the reduced protein yield in picked samples is more likely caused by
factors specific to sample handling, storage, or picking efficiency, rather than funda-
mental limitations of the proteomics workflow. Taken together, these considerations

sts that the reduced protein yield in picked samples is more likely caused by
factors specific to sample handling, storage, or picking efficiency, rather than funda-
mental limitations of the proteomics workflow. Taken together, these considerations
highlight the need for further systematic investigation into sample handling, storage
conditions, and instrument performance to optimize the analysis.
In addition to low protein yields of the picked samples, analysis of negative controls
revealed additional challenges. The negative control picked from the slide taken dir-
ectly from the package showed an unexpectedly high protein count. Notably, this
sample was collected last, after all other picks had been completed, raising suspicion


 82
of potential sample carry-over during the picking process. This carry-over may result
from residual material adhering to the picking needle between samples. To mitigate
this risk in future experiments, implementing additional needle washing steps or in-
creasing the frequency of needle replacements could be effective strategies.

Altogether, these findings confirm the feasibility of integrating t-CycIF-guided spa-
tial sampling with MS-based proteomics, while highlighting several critical areas for
improvement. Variability in protein detection among samples with low pick numbers
reinforces the importance of improving picking consistency and increasing replicate
numbers to ensure statistical robustness and enable exclusion of poor-quality data.
Finally, advances in MS instrumentation sensitivity and optimized sample processing
protocols hold promise for enabling proteomics on smaller tissue regions, thereby im-
proving spatial resolution in the future.

Integrated proteomics analysis pipeline

Next, based on the proteomics data generated during the optimization experiment
and despite the generally low protein yields, a functional analysis pipeline was suc-
cessfully established. This pipeline provides a solid foundation for extracting mean-
ingful information from spatially resolved proteomics datasets. Nevertheless, there is
still room for complementary analysis like pathway enrichment analysis or network
modeling, to deepen biological insights in future studies, which would have exceeded
the scope of this study.
Using this pipeline, the proteomics profiles of picked samples were explored in regard
of their region of origin and their cell state. Due to the limited sample number,
variable protein yields, and conservative quality control criteria applied, the statist-
ical power and significance of findings are limited. Thus, the results presented here
are primarily hypothesis-generating and intended to demonstrate the utility of the
pipeline rather than serve as definitive biological conclusions.

Cluster Heatmap Analysis revealed a core proteome consistently detected across
nearly all samples, suggesting a stable baseline of abundant proteins in all invest-
igated cell types. In contrast, a subset of proteins appeared sporadically across all
samples and groups, indicating they were neither consistently detected nor specific
to any region or cell state, likely reflecting low-abundance targets or technical vari-
ability. Dormant samples generally exhibited fewer identified proteins than prolif-

s and groups, indicating they were neither consistently detected nor specific
to any region or cell state, likely reflecting low-abundance targets or technical vari-
ability. Dormant samples generally exhibited fewer identified proteins than prolif-
erating samples, which may indicate reduced proteome complexity or lower protein


 83
expression associated with dormancy. Clustering patterns also suggested that pro-
tein expression profiles were influenced by both cell state and tissue origin, with
some non-dormant stroma samples clustering alongside tumor/TSI samples and some
dormant tumor/TSI samples clustering alongside dormant stroma samples. This po-
tentially reflects molecular similarities between activated stroma and tumor regions,
and dormant tumor and stroma regions.

The performed DEA faced limitations due to strict filtering and the resulting low
number of samples per comparison group. Moreover, all proteins uniquely detected
in one group needed to be excluded from statistical testing due to the inherent type
of the performed analysis, though these may represent biologically relevant biomark-
ers specific for one cell state. There thorough exploration and analysis would have
exceeded the scope of this thesis, therefore warranting further investigation.
After performing the DEA, results were assessed. No proteins reached statistical
significance after multiple testing correction, a likely consequence of the small sample
size. Nevertheless, determining upregulated proteins by relying on unadjusted p -
values combined with fold-change thresholds remains a widely accepted approach in
exploratory proteomics studies. This strategy provides a practical means to identify
candidate proteins for pipeline validation and future hypothesis generation, while

values combined with fold-change thresholds remains a widely accepted approach in
exploratory proteomics studies. This strategy provides a practical means to identify
candidate proteins for pipeline validation and future hypothesis generation, while
acknowledging the need for cautious interpretation.
For further exploration of differentially expressed proteins, heatmaps normalized via
min-max scaling were generated to highlight relative expression differences. These
visualizations reinforced distinct proteomics signatures associated with proliferating
versus dormant states. The group-specific clusters likely encompass proteins involved
in cell cycle regulation, metabolism, or microenvironment interactions relevant to cel-
lular proliferation and dormancy. An extensive investigation into the function and
background of all these proteins would be interesting, but it is not conducted here
as it exceeds the scope of this study and would be too extensive to address within
the available framework. Given the exploratory nature and limited sample size, these
heatmaps serve as qualitative validation tools rather than definitive evidence of pro-
tein regulation. Follow-up studies using targeted proteomics or complementary func-
tional assays are needed to confirm these observations.

Integration of proteomics data with compositional analysis via correlation assessments
revealed significant positive correlations between protein expression levels and the re-
lative abundance of corresponding cell states. These correlations not only suggest
that certain proteins may serve as markers or functional effectors of specific cellular


 84
phenotypes within tumor and stroma compartments but also provide orthogonal val-
idation of sample characterization.

es. These correlations not only suggest
that certain proteins may serve as markers or functional effectors of specific cellular


 84
phenotypes within tumor and stroma compartments but also provide orthogonal val-
idation of sample characterization. It is important to note that correlation does not
imply causation, and some observed relationships may reflect co-variation with other
factors. And again, the limited sample size further reduces the robustness of these
findings, implying the necessity of a cautious interpretation. Nonetheless, these pro-
teins represent promising candidates for future targeted validation, functional studies,
and biomarker development.

These results demonstrate that ProteoPicking, the developed spatially resolved pro-
teomics approach combining t-CycIF staining with MS-based proteomics analysis, ef-
fectively captures biologically relevant molecular signatures reflective of cellular com-
position and state. This work lays important groundwork for more comprehensive
and statistically powered studies aimed at unraveling functional proteomics hetero-
geneity within complex tissue microenvironments. Nevertheless, while the method is
already applicable and yields valuable insights, it still requires further refinement and
optimization.
In conclusion, the spatially resolved proteomics analysis method developed and estab-
lished in this thesis highlights both the promise of the approach and the continuing
need for methodological advancements in this field.

Multi-omics profiling of cell-cycle spatial niches in
 high-grade serous carcinoma


 Genetics and Molecular Biosciences,
 Molecular and Analytical Health Biosciences,
 Master's thesis

 Author:
 Nika Mikhailava

 Supervisors:
 Anniina Färkkilä
 Ada Junquera Mencía

 05.2026
 Helsinki
Faculty: Faculty of Biological and Environmental Sciences
Degree programme: Genetics and Molecular Biosciences
Study track: Molecular and Analytical Health Biosciences
Author: Nika Mikhailava
Title: Multi-omics profiling of cell-cycle spatial niches in high-grade serous carcinoma
Level: Master’s thesis
Month and year: 05.2026
Number of pages: 60
Keywords: ovarian cancer, high-grade serous carcinoma, tumor microenvironment, t-CyCIF,
molecular profiling, cell cycle, spatial niche
Supervisors: Anniina Färkkilä, Ada Junquera Mencía
Where deposited: HELDA, Helsinki University Open Repository
Additional information:
Abstract:
High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the deadliest gynecological malignancy, typically detected at
advanced stages. The standard of care in more than 50% of cases comprises neoadjuvant chemotherapy
(NACT), followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). HGSC is characterized by initial high
chemotherapy response rates, yet 80% of patients ultimately relapse. While previous research has
focused on “persister" cells - drug-tolerant tumor subpopulations that drive relapse. We suggest that
these do not capture the full spectrum of cells driving disease recurrence. We propose the term
"subsister" cells to describe the broader, more heterogeneous population of tumor cells that survive first-
line chemotherapy, NACT, and retain the potential to fuel relapse with different scales of aggressiveness
and therapeutic targeting.

the term
"subsister" cells to describe the broader, more heterogeneous population of tumor cells that survive first-
line chemotherapy, NACT, and retain the potential to fuel relapse with different scales of aggressiveness
and therapeutic targeting.
We hypothesize that a critical hallmark of “subsister” cells is dysregulation of the cell cycle. Previous
research in our laboratory identified a specific highly proliferative patient archetype characterized by
the prevalence of recurrent cellular neighbourhoods dominated by highly proliferative niches co-
expressing Ki67 and pRb proteins in IDS samples. Because the prevalence of these niches correlates
with a short platinum-free interval (PFI), we hypothesize that these niches represent an aggressive,
chemotherapy-subsisting population.
This study aimed to uncover the molecular features of “subsister” highly proliferative niches in IDS
HGSC samples. To comprehensively evaluate these microenvironments, we employed a stepwise spatial
multi-omics approach. First, we conducted a large-scale spatial proteomics experiment using tissue-
based cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) to precisely characterize the physical location, cellular
composition, and prevalence of those niches across the patient cohort. This extensive protein-level
mapping was subsequently integrated with spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling
(DSP)) to explore the underlying transcriptomic mechanisms within those niches.
Spatial transcriptomics analysis revealed that highly proliferative niches possess a characteristic
transcriptional program. As expected, these niches demonstrated coherent upregulation of pathways
governing active cell proliferation and altered cellular energetics. Beyond these expected findings,

ghly proliferative niches possess a characteristic
transcriptional program. As expected, these niches demonstrated coherent upregulation of pathways
governing active cell proliferation and altered cellular energetics. Beyond these expected findings,
functional enrichment (single-sample gene set enrichment analysis and gene set enrichment analysis)
highlighted distinct survival programs utilized by those niches. Specifically, significant upregulation of
DNA repair mechanisms, DNA synthesis, and sumoylation of DNA replication proteins reveals a
specialized program to manage replicative stress introduced with rapid division. Simultaneously, this
was coupled with a metabolic shift toward the Glycine Metabolic Process, supporting the biosynthetic
demands for nucleotide synthesis and antioxidant defense.
Reflecting these pathway-level adaptations, differential gene expression (DGE) analysis highlighted
specific drivers of this phenotype. FGD5 and SOX13 emerged as the most significantly upregulated
markers in these niches, suggesting critical roles in maintaining "stem-like" plasticity and structural
integrity. Conversely, the prominent downregulation of CCND1 in these active niches aligns with our
previous findings, associating CCND1 enrichment with a better prognosis.
Together, these spatially resolved transcriptomics signatures provide critical insights into the HGSC
"subsister " cell landscape. Characterizing these transcriptomic mechanisms may ultimately assist in the
design of novel treatment strategies that effectively target these aggressive niches and prevent disease
relapse.

1 Introduction
High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most common and lethal subtype of
ovarian cancer. The standard of care includes debulking surgery and platinum-based
chemotherapy. While most patients initially respond to the treatment, more than 75%
of patients experience relapse. At least 50% of the patients receive the neoadjuant
chemotherapy (NACT) as a first treatment, followed by interval debulking surgery
(IDS), to remove the remaining tumor masses. The cells that survive the first line of
chemotherapy are what we have named “subsister” cells. We hypothesize that these
cells are driving the relapse and contributing to treatment resistance formation.

Recent advances in spatially resolved imaging have enabled the characterization of
tumor heterogeneity beyond genomic alterations, highlighting the importance of
phenotypic tumor states and their spatial organization. Because the efficacy of most
cytotoxic chemotherapies is inherently tied to disruption of the cell cycle, the ability of
certain tumor populations to bypass the G1/S, G2/M, and spindle assembly
checkpoints is a primary driver of treatment failure. In this context, multiplexed
imaging approaches such as tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) allow
high-dimensional protein profiling across tissue sections at single-cell resolution. By
mapping the spatial distribution of key cell cycle regulators, this methodology provides
a unique insight into functional tumor states and their spatial organization that

nsional protein profiling across tissue sections at single-cell resolution. By
mapping the spatial distribution of key cell cycle regulators, this methodology provides
a unique insight into functional tumor states and their spatial organization that
governs chemotherapy resistance and facilitates the formation of “subsister” niches.

Using t-CyCIF, our recent exploration identified discrete HGSC patient archetypes
based on common patterns of their “subsister” cell populations (Fig. 1). These included
a highly proliferative, Cyclin D1 (CCND1)-enriched archetype, and a heterogeneous
archetype exhibiting mixed phenotypic features. Notably, the highly proliferative
archetype was associated with poorer platinum-free interval (PFI) and was
independent of genetic alterations. This archetype was enriched in recurrent cellular
neighbourhoods rich in mitotically active tumor pH3+ cells and proliferative Ki67+
pRb+ cells. These findings suggest that “subsister”-based patient archetypes are
clinically relevant, making it relevant to study their survival mechanisms.

2 Background
2.1 Ovarian cancer: epidemiology, risk factors, and subtypes

Ovarian cancer (OC) is recognised as the deadliest gynecological malignancy, affecting
approximately 200,000-300,000 women annually (M. Wang et al., 2024). Globally,
the incidence and mortality rates for ovarian cancer are 6.6 and 4.2 per 100.000 of
women, respectively (Caruso et al., 2025). It is the 18th most common cancer
worldwide and the 8th most common cancer among women. The median diagnosis age
is 63 years, and the 5-year survival is less than 50% (Caruso et al., 2025).

Ovarian cancer has 5 histo-molecular subtypes: HGSC, low-grade serous carcinoma
(LGSC), mucinous carcinoma (MC), endometroid carcinoma (EC), and clear cell
carcinoma (CCC) (Prat, 2012). Geographically, serous and mucinous carcinomas are
more prevalent in high-income countries, while endometroid and clear-cell
carcinomas in low-income countries, particularly in Africa and Eastern Asia (M. Wang
et al., 2024). Serous carcinomas appear as the most prevalent subtype of ovarian
cancer, accounting for approximately 43% of all disease cases (M. Wang et al., 2024).
HGSC is the most aggressive and recurrent subtype of ovarian cancer (Govindarajan et
al., 2020).

Serous carcinomas appear as the most prevalent subtype of ovarian
cancer, accounting for approximately 43% of all disease cases (M. Wang et al., 2024).
HGSC is the most aggressive and recurrent subtype of ovarian cancer (Govindarajan et
al., 2020).

Common risk factors for ovarian cancer include older age, family history of breast or
ovarian cancer, endometriosis, nulliparity, and infertility (Caruso et al., 2025).
Subtype-specific factors include menopausal hormonal therapy, increasing the risk for
serous carcinomas (M. Wang et al., 2024). Hereditary factors are associated with 25%
of ovarian cancer cases (Caruso et al., 2025). Factors protecting against ovarian cancer
are multiparity, breastfeeding, and oral contraceptive use (M. Wang et al., 2024).

2.2 HGSC diagnosis and treatment

Lack of symptoms in the early stages of HGSC poses a critical challenge for its clinical
management. Consequently, the disease is mostly diagnosed at advanced stages. When
present, the symptoms are often non-specific (such as abdominal pain, bloating, and
frequent urination) (Caruso et al., 2025).

Current screening methods for ovarian cancer are limited and include blood-derived
carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125) and transvaginal sonography (M. Wang et al.,
2024). The standard treatment regimen comprises cytoreductive (debulking) surgery
 12

and platinum-based chemotherapy treatment. The specific treatment strategy depends
on the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) stage. For
patients with early-stage disease (FIGO stages I or II), the disease is managed with
surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. In advanced-stage cases of HGSC (FIGO stages
III-IV), patients are treated with NACT followed by IDS. NACT typically consists of 3

atients with early-stage disease (FIGO stages I or II), the disease is managed with
surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. In advanced-stage cases of HGSC (FIGO stages
III-IV), patients are treated with NACT followed by IDS. NACT typically consists of 3
to 4 cycles of combination therapy using a platinum agent and a taxane. After NACT
reduces the tumor burden, patients undergo IDS – a surgery aimed at removing as
much of the remaining macroscopic tumor as possible before completing the last cycles
of chemotherapy.

Following initial treatment, most patients achieve clinical remission. However, the risk
of relapse remains high. Approximately 80% of patients diagnosed with advanced-
stage cancer and 20% of patients with early-stage cancer relapse. Prognosis is heavily
influenced by the molecular characteristics of the tumor. For instance, germline and
somatic homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) mutations in HGSC are
associated with better prognosis (Pennington et al., 2014). Tumors with HRD, often
due to BRCA1/2 mutations, respond better to platinum-based chemotherapy and Poly
(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors. Conversely, tumors that are homologous
recombination proficient (HRP) are correlated with shorter progression-free survival
and overall poorer disease outcomes.

For patients who experience a disease recurrence, the timing of the relapse dictates the
subsequent clinical approach. Those patients who respond to chemotherapy and
relapse more than 6 months after the treatment are considered platinum-sensitive and
are likely to respond to subsequent platinum-based treatments(Miras et al., 2024). In
contrast, patients who relapse within 6 months after chemotherapy are considered
platinum-resistant and have a worse prognosis.

To combat these recurrence rates and improve overall treatment efficacy, targeted
therapies have become increasingly integral. Some of them are widely applied as
maintenance therapies following platinum-based regimens (L. Wang et al., 2025).
Among these, PARP inhibitors are the most impactful of targeted therapies, effectively
exploiting synthetic lethality in HRD tumors. Under normal conditions, PARP
enzymes are responsible for repairing single-strand DNA breaks, which are converted
to double-strand breaks during DNA replication. In HRP cells, these breaks are
normally repaired by the HR pathway, while in HR-deficient tumors, this mechanism
 13

is impaired, leading to DNA damage accumulation and cancer cell death (Caruso et al.,
2025).

Beyond PARP inhibitors, other targeted therapies include antiangiogenic drugs, such
as bevacizumab. By inhibiting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) signaling,
bevacizumab limits tumor vascularization and provides significant clinical benefit
when combined with chemotherapy (Zhang et al., 2025). However, not all targeted
therapies have clinical benefits in HGSC. While immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs),
such as those targeting TIGIT and PD-1/PD-L1 axis, have achieved remarkable success
in solid tumors like melanoma and non-small cell lung cancer, they have shown only
limited benefit in restoring immunity in HGSC tumors. This is largely attributed to the
immunologically “cold” tumor microenvironment of HGSC with low immune
infiltration and high immune suppression.

d non-small cell lung cancer, they have shown only
limited benefit in restoring immunity in HGSC tumors. This is largely attributed to the
immunologically “cold” tumor microenvironment of HGSC with low immune
infiltration and high immune suppression. Current efforts focus on improving targeted
therapies’ potential through therapy combinations and better patient stratification
(Pawłowska et al., 2022) .

Despite these therapeutic advances, the primary obstacle in HGSC treatment remains
the emergence of chemoresistance and subsequent disease recurrence. In addition to
DNA damage repair defects, resistance and recurrence are driven by alterations in the
drug transporters' expression, oxidative stress resistance, and the presence of cancer
stem cells (CSCs).

Crucially, dysregulation of the cell cycle is central to cancer biology. It serves as an
important prognostic factor and driver of treatment failure. Because the loss of normal
cell cycle control allows tumor cells to sustain proliferative signaling and evade growth
suppressors, understanding these mechanisms is essential for addressing
chemoresistance. For instance, in HRP cases of HGSC, 20% of tumors have
amplification or gain of the cyclin E1 (CCNE1) gene, which promotes rapid progression
through the cell cycle and is associated with platinum resistance. Conversely, loss of
the retinoblastoma (RB) protein, a master regulator of the G1-to-S phase transition, is
associated with higher survival rates and platinum sensitivity. Ultimately, these
molecular alterations suggest that the tumor’s ability to survive therapy is linked to
how it manages cell cycle progression and checkpoints.

2.3 Cell cycle

The mitotic cell cycle is a tightly regulated process, ensuring accurate division of
genetic material and formation of two identical daughter cells. It is divided into two
stages: interphase and mitosis (Matthews et al., 2021). Interphase consists of G1, S,
and G2 phases (Fig. 2). During these phases, the cell grows, replicates genetic material,
and prepares for mitosis. DNA replication happens in the S phase, flanked by the G1
and G2 gap phases. These gap phases provide the necessary time for cellular growth
and the synthesis of proteins required for the subsequent division.

Progression through the cell cycle is guided by cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) in
coordination with their regulatory partners, cyclins, which coordinate CDKs and
ensure the transitions between cell cycle phases. The decision to enter the cell cycle
happens in the G1 phase. Here, the cell decides to continue the cell cycle or exit to the
quiescent G0 phase. In the G1 phase, cyclin D binds CDK4/6 and activates it (Song et
al., 2024). Activated CDK4/6 binds and phosphorylates the RB protein, which triggers
the release of the E2F transcription factors. Released E2F transcription factors activate

G0 phase. In the G1 phase, cyclin D binds CDK4/6 and activates it (Song et
al., 2024). Activated CDK4/6 binds and phosphorylates the RB protein, which triggers
the release of the E2F transcription factors. Released E2F transcription factors activate
the expression of genes required for DNA replication and production of cyclin E. Cyclin
E-CDK2 complex phosphorylates RB further and drives the entry to S phase.
 15

Once the decision to enter the cell cycle has been made, cyclin A-CDK2 complexes drive
the initiation and progression of DNA replication. The accumulation of cyclins E and
A, coupled with the inactivation of the APC/CCDH1 complex, enables initiation of
replication. Finally, the transition to mitosis is driven by CDK1. It activates key kinases
and triggers the chromosomal and cellular changes needed for cell division.

While cell cycle checkpoints operate as a DNA integrity tracking mechanism, ensuring
propagation of DNA without errors through multiple generations, cancer cells
frequently bypass these signals. The loss of certain cell cycle checkpoints causes cancer
cells to become heavily dependent on other cell cycle control mechanisms. This
prevents catastrophic damage to genetic material and ensures cell viability.
Consequently, this dysregulation is not just a biological anomaly but a defining feature
of the disease. Specifically, three of the defined Hallmarks of Cancer are directly linked
to the loss of cell cycle control (Hanahan, 2026):

 • Sustaining proliferative signaling: Cancer cells bypass normal growth
 factor requirements to maintain continuous division.
 • Evading growth suppressors: Tumors often disable the proteins, which
 normally stop the cycle at the G1 checkpoint.
 • Enabling replicative immortality: Cells detour programmed senescence to

2.4 Cell cycle dysregulation and therapeutic opportunities in cancer

This continuous progression and failure to exit the cell cycle make malignant cells
specifically vulnerable to therapies targeting the remaining cell cycle machinery
(Matthews et al., 2021). Beyond simple proliferation, cancer cells utilize additional
mechanisms to navigate stressful conditions and evade the normal cell cycle
checkpoints. These mechanisms include checkpoint recovery, where cells override
DNA damage signals, ”resetting” the cell cycle clock to resume division despite the
damage (Min & Lee, 2023; L. Zhu et al., 2015). Furthermore, tumor cells may enter
quiescent or dormant states, shifting into a reversible G0 phase that metabolically
protects them from chemotherapies targeting active DNA synthesis. The evasion of
cell cycle regulation is further achieved by circumventing senescence and apoptosis
through the inactivation of tumor suppressors such as TP53 and RB1. Finally, tumors
 16

often develop mechanisms to bypass replication stress. Cells manage stalled
replication forks by utilizing translesion synthesis or activating dormant replication
origins.

The biological significance of these adaptations is particularly evident when
considering that the most efficient and widely used cancer treatment strategies rely on
the induction of excessive DNA damage. Because these therapeutic strategies aim to
overwhelm the cell's repair capacity, any mechanism that allows a tumor cell to delay,

ing that the most efficient and widely used cancer treatment strategies rely on
the induction of excessive DNA damage. Because these therapeutic strategies aim to
overwhelm the cell's repair capacity, any mechanism that allows a tumor cell to delay,
bypass, or tolerate such damage becomes a primary driver of clinical failure.

In HGSC, platinum-based therapies remain the cornerstone of the treatment. These
agents form DNA crosslinks, disrupting the replication and triggering cell death.
Similarly, in other malignancies such as breast, lung, and hematologic cancers, DNA-
damaging agents, including doxorubicin and topoisomerase inhibitors, are used.
However, the cellular adaptations described directly counteract these interventions.
Consequently, the treatment acts as a selective pressure, facilitating the emergence and
survival of chemoresistant populations.

Traditional chemotherapies can be broadly classified into three groups (Song et al.,
2024):

 • Drugs affecting DNA biosynthesis: They primarily act during the S phase
 by interfering with nucleoside synthesis, either by mimicking nucleosides (e.g.,
 fluorouracil), inhibiting nucleotide formation (e.g., methotrexate), or blocking
 DNA polymerase activity (e.g., cytarabine).

 • Drugs affecting DNA structure and function: They also mainly target the
 S phase by disrupting DNA integrity through alkylation, strand breakage, or
 formation of intra- and interstrand crosslinks (e.g., platinum-based agents,
 cyclophosphamide).

 • Drugs affecting RNA and protein biosynthesis and mitosis: They act
 across the S and M phases. They function by binding DNA and interfering with
 transcription or mitotic spindle dynamics (e.g., taxanes, vincristine,
 adriamycin).
 17

phamide).

 • Drugs affecting RNA and protein biosynthesis and mitosis: They act
 across the S and M phases. They function by binding DNA and interfering with
 transcription or mitotic spindle dynamics (e.g., taxanes, vincristine,
 adriamycin).
 17

To overcome the limitations of these broad-spectrum agents, targeted cell cycle
therapies have emerged as a promising alternative. They offer the advantages of low
toxicity, higher target specificity, and fewer adverse side effects (Song et al., 2024).
Furthermore, unlike traditional DNA-damaging chemotherapies, they don’t introduce
mutations to the DNA, decreasing the chances of mutation-driven drug resistance.

Current targeted strategies focus on disrupting key cell-cycle checkpoints:

 • G1/S checkpoint: Interventions at this checkpoint either interfere with DNA-
 damage sensing Ataxia telangietasia mutation (ATM) related pathways (e.g.,
 ATM-Chk2-p53 signaling) or directly inhibit the cyclin-dependent kinases
 driving cell cycle progression, such as CDK2 and CDK4/6

 • G2/M checkpoint: This checkpoint monitors DNA damage before the mitotic
 entry and inactivates CDK1 in case of damage. Therapies here target kinases
 such as CHK1, Wee1, and CDK1, collectively disrupting the ATR-Chk1-Wee1
 axis. This forces damaged cells into premature mitosis and following mitotic
 catastrophe.

 • Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC): Interventions target core SAC
 components such as BUB1, BUBR1, MAD2, CENP-E, and CDC20 to disrupt
 mitotic fidelity and induce cell death during division.

While the therapeutic strategies detailed above specifically target the machinery of the
cell cycle, their clinical efficacy is rarely uniform across the entire tumor. The ability of

disrupt

While the therapeutic strategies detailed above specifically target the machinery of the
cell cycle, their clinical efficacy is rarely uniform across the entire tumor. The ability of
a cancer cell to utilize survival adaptations is not dictated solely by its internal genetic
makeup. Rather, these cell cycle decisions are heavily influenced by the spatial
architecture and biochemical signaling of the surrounding tissue microenvironment.
To understand why specific populations can evade these potent therapies while others
succumb, the focus must shift from the individual tumor cells to the complex ecosystem
that sustains them.

2.5 Tumor microenvironment

The understanding of cancer has greatly evolved over the last two decades. Initially
being considered simple spheroid assemblies of malignant cells, tumors are now
acknowledged as highly complex ecosystems, comprising tumor cells, immune cells,
extracellular matrix (ECM), cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), vascular system, and
 18

various less abundant constituents. Collectively, this whole cancer ecosystem is known
as the tumor microenvironment (TME).


Figure 3. Cellular heterogeneity and spatial architecture of the TME. Schematic representation of the
diverse cellular populations and ecological interactions within the TME. Diagram created with BioRender

The TME plays an active role in every stage of cancer progression, from tumor
initiation and progression to invasion, intravasation, and eventual metastatic
dissemination (Anderson & Simon, 2020). The critical role of TME becomes evident
from the efficacy of therapies that target its stromal and immune compartments,
rather than relying on direct cytotoxicity against the cancer cells.

eventual metastatic
dissemination (Anderson & Simon, 2020). The critical role of TME becomes evident
from the efficacy of therapies that target its stromal and immune compartments,
rather than relying on direct cytotoxicity against the cancer cells. Malignant cells
actively orchestrate this microenvironment by recruiting and reprogramming non-
cancerous cells, which subsequently adopt designated roles that can heavily influence
patient prognosis.

The immune landscape of the tumor is particularly important. Tumors are broadly
classified into “immune-infiltrated” (often termed “immune-hot”), “immune-
excluded, and “immune-silent” phenotypes (Anderson & Simon, 2020). This
immunological profile is often dictated by the underlying tumor genetic phenotype.
 19

For instance, in HGSC, tumors with BRCA1/2 mutations typically exhibit an
”immune-hot” profile, while BRCA-wildtype tumors are more often ”immune-silent”
or ”immune-excluded”.

Within the TME, the dynamic interactions between tumor and immune cells directly
determine the course of the disease. High infiltration of CD8+ cytotoxic T cells and
CD4+ helper T cells into the TME is generally associated with better prognosis, as
CD8+ cells directly kill tumor cells and inhibit angiogenesis, while CD4+ helper T cells
support cytotoxic responses. In contrast, the presence of regulatory T cells promotes
tumor progression by suppressing anti-tumor immune responses. Similarly, tumor-
associated macrophages, which often represent the most abundant immune
population within the TME, can adopt M1 (pro-inflammatory) and M2 (anti-
inflammatory) states and exhibit a context-dependent role through this polarization.
Usually, macrophages are polarized towards the M2 state, which promotes

population within the TME, can adopt M1 (pro-inflammatory) and M2 (anti-
inflammatory) states and exhibit a context-dependent role through this polarization.
Usually, macrophages are polarized towards the M2 state, which promotes
immunosuppression and is associated with poor clinical outcomes.

Beyond the immune compartment, the TME is supported by non-immune stroma. It
includes endothelial cells, CAFs, adipocytes, and the structural network of ECM. These
components interact with both tumor and immune cells, regulating signaling
molecules and nutrient delivery, and facilitating migration. For instance, CAFs secrete
TGF-β, inducing epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) of the tumor cells and
modifying the ECM density. This dense matrix can physically barrier immune cells
from infiltrating the tumor, thereby directly contributing to therapy resistance and
poor clinical outcomes (Yang et al., 2023).

HGSC is considered a particularly heterogeneous type of cancer. The composition and
state of the TME vary between the patients and greatly influence the prognosis.
Moreover, in HGSC, tumor phenotypes are not solely determined by intrinsic genomic
alterations. Recent studies demonstrate that specific molecular features of HGSC are
consistently associated with specific immune and stromal landscapes. For example,
HGSC tumors with MHCII expression exhibit enhanced antigen presentation capacity
and are associated with spatially organized and immune-infiltrated “hot”
microenvironments, which directly correlate with improved clinical outcomes (Perez-
Villatoro et al., n.d.).
 20

Beyond immune regulation, the TME actively dictates tumor cell behavior through
complex programs. Key biological survival programs such as EMT, cancer stemness,

directly correlate with improved clinical outcomes (Perez-
Villatoro et al., n.d.).
 20

Beyond immune regulation, the TME actively dictates tumor cell behavior through
complex programs. Key biological survival programs such as EMT, cancer stemness,
and tumor dormancy are sustained by specific stromal signals. While EMT is driven by
increased fibroblast activation and extracellular matrix remodeling, promoting
immunosuppressive niches, stem-like and dormant tumor cell populations are
supported by specific, localized stromal signals enabling their persistence. Altogether,
these findings highlight that tumor cell states and TME co-evolve in HGSC, jointly
shaping disease progression and treatment response.

Despite the critical importance of this co-evolution, the intricate signaling and
architecture of the TME cannot be captured by traditional bulk and single-cell
analyses. These conventional methods require tissue dissociation, losing the critical
context of cellular neighborhoods. Investigating how these localized niches drive
therapy resistance necessitates a shift toward spatial technologies. These methods
preserve the native relationship between the malignant and stromal components.

2.6 Spatial resolution methodologies

Spatial resolution methodologies have greatly evolved over the past two decades. They
enable precise mapping of biomolecules within the tissues, linking tissue architecture
with molecular information. Specifically, spatial proteomics provides quantitative,
localized data on protein abundance, while spatial transcriptomics reveals gene
expression patterns across tissues. Integrating these modalities provides a powerful
framework for understanding biological development, disease progression, and
therapeutic response.

2.7 Spatial transcriptomics

Spatial transcriptomics approaches enable the identification of cell types and states,
the determination of spatially variable gene expression, and the detailed
characterization of cell neighborhoods (Jain & Eadon, 2024). Early attempts to create
spatial transcriptomics methods date back to the 1960s, relying on hybridization of
radioactively labelled DNA or RNA to tissue sections, followed by recording the signal
with autoradiography. Currently, there is a wide range of spatial transcriptomics
methodologies available, providing spatial data with varying degrees of resolution,
sequencing depth, and genomic coverage (see Table 1).

Spatial transcriptomics technologies can be broadly divided into two groups:
sequencing-based and imaging-based approaches.

2.7.1 Sequencing-based spatial transcriptomics

Sequencing-based spatial transcriptomics technologies utilize next-generation
sequencing and DNA barcoding to map spatial transcriptomics data across the tissue
sample. They are further subdivided to in situ capture (ISC) methods and region-of-
interest (ROI)-based sequencing.

 • ISC methods

 ISC methods operate by hybridizing the tissue to a slide with the barcoded
 probes attached. Upon tissue permeabilization, RNA is released from the tissue
 22

 and binds to these capture probes. The most prominent technologies
 representing ISC methods include Visium, Slide-seq and Stereo-seq. They
 provide varying resolution, ranging from subcellular to roughly 25 cells per
 sequencing spot. However, these methods generally require fresh-frozen tissue
 for optimal performance.

 • ROI-based sequencing

e Visium, Slide-seq and Stereo-seq. They
 provide varying resolution, ranging from subcellular to roughly 25 cells per
 sequencing spot. However, these methods generally require fresh-frozen tissue
 for optimal performance.

 • ROI-based sequencing

 ROI-based sequencing is an alternative to ISC-based methods. This approach
 involves the supervised, morphologically guided selection of ROIs from stained
 tissues. The ROI selection is followed by the collection of the cells from the
 selected ROIs into a microplate for downstream sequencing. ROI-based
 sequencing methods have the advantages of lower cost and the ability to target
 specific tissue areas based on functional or pathological features.

 GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP) is the premier example of this approach.
 It enables ROI-guided, highly customizable molecular profiling of tissues. The
 coverage of genes is comprehensive and comparable to ISC technologies
 (around 18000 genes). It is important to note, however, that ROI-based
 methods lack single-cell resolution and introduce user bias during the manual
 ROI selection step.

2.7.2 Imaging-based spatial transcriptomics

Imaging-based spatial transcriptomics methods rely on direct visualization of RNA
molecules within the tissue sections using fluorescence. Imaging-based spatial
transcriptomics approaches are primarily divided into situ sequencing (ISS) and in situ
hybridization (ISH) approaches.

 • In Situ Sequencing (ISS)

 ISS relies on reverse transcription of mRNA into cDNA in situ, followed by
 sequencing through rolling circle amplification. The sequences are decoded by
 recording the fluorescence from the latterly added, labeled nucleotides. These
 methods usually involve the preselection of target genes.

into cDNA in situ, followed by
 sequencing through rolling circle amplification. The sequences are decoded by
 recording the fluorescence from the latterly added, labeled nucleotides. These
 methods usually involve the preselection of target genes.

 • In Situ Hybridization (ISH)-based methods
 23

 ISH-based technologies rely on the direct hybridization of fluorescently labeled
 probes to mRNA molecules within the tissue and microscopically recording the
 fluorescence. While these methods enable subcellular recording of the signal,
 they are limited by the need for predetermined target panels. Representative
 platforms include CosMx, Xenium, MERFISH, and seqFISH+.

2.7.3 GeoMx digital spatial profiling

Representing a prominent example of the ROI-based sequencing technologies
introduced in Section 2.7.1, GeoMx DSP is a method for highly multiplex profiling of
proteins and RNA, highly compatible with FFPE samples (Merritt et al., 2020). It
enables profiling of both RNA and proteins within the preselected ROI. To achieve this,
it utilizes a variety of mRNA probes or antibodies conjugated with UV-photocleavable
oligonucleotide barcodes (Fig. 4).

The general workflow proceeds as follows:

 1. Following the antigen retrieval, fluorescent morphology markers are applied to
 the tissue sections to visualize the underlying tissue architecture.

 2. Next, the slides are loaded into the DSP and scanned for immunofluorescence
 markers to guide the selection of ROIs for the experiment. Following the
 imaging and ROI selection, the ROIs are segmented into areas of illumination
 (AOIs) based on morphological features, defining the exact cellular
 compartments from which the sequencing will occur.

 3. Then, the oligonucleotides from the probes get photocleaved with focused UV

2.8 Spatial proteomics

Spatial proteomics is revolutionizing our understanding of biological processes by
exposing how tissue architectures are formed at the protein level. This field provides
critical insights into the tissue microenvironment, driving discoveries in cancer biology
and beyond. Spatial proteomics methods can be broadly categorized into three groups:
liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS)-based, imaging mass
spectrometry (MS)-based, and antibody-based methods (see Table 2) (Hu et al., 2025).

2.8.1 Antibody-based spatial proteomics

Antibody-based methods rely on signal molecules linked to antibodies to determine
precise protein localization within tissues. These methods are limited by the relatively
low number of targets that can be detected in a single experiment. To address this,
multiplexed antibody-based methods have been developed, which can be categorized
into fluorophore-labeled, DNA oligonucleotide-labeled, and metal isotope-labeled
methods.

 • Fluorophore-labeled methods

 Typically involves multiple rounds of staining, image acquisition, and
 fluorophore inactivation. While these techniques offer high spatial resolution
 and excellent subcellular localization of proteins, the scalability of such methods
 is limited. Challenges include altered antigenicity, incomplete fluorescence
 quenching, cross-reactivity of antibodies, and the difficulty of resolving protein
 abundances that vary across several orders of magnitude.

scalability of such methods
 is limited. Challenges include altered antigenicity, incomplete fluorescence
 quenching, cross-reactivity of antibodies, and the difficulty of resolving protein
 abundances that vary across several orders of magnitude.

 • DNA-oligonucleotide-labeled methods

 Significantly improve scalability and throughput. These approaches typically
 involve a single staining step with oligonucleotide-conjugated antibodies,
 followed by cyclic hybridization and removal of fluorescently labeled probes for
 readout.

 • Metal-labeled antibody methods

 Conjugate antibodies with metal isotopes, coupling the process with mass
 spectrometry readout. Following a single round of staining, the metal tags are
 released from the tissue sample and detected by a mass spectrometer. This
 allows the detection of over 40 markers with high resolution and speed.
 However, these techniques are destructive to the sample, preventing
 downstream analysis of the same tissue section.
 26

2.8.2 LC–MS/MS-based spatial proteomics

LC-MS/MS-based spatial proteomics combines liquid chromatography-tandem mass
spectrometry with precision microdissection techniques, such as laser capture
microdissection. Tissues are either divided into small, standardized regions (“voxels”),
or ROIs are specifically excised for profiling, requiring fewer protein samples and
resources.

2.8.3 Imaging MS-based approaches

Finally, imaging MS-based approaches enable untargeted profiling of protein
distribution within the tissue. In these techniques, the mass spectrum of charged
molecules is recorded systematically across the tissue surface, allowing researchers to
generate spatial maps of molecular protein abundance.

2.8.4 Tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence

he tissue. In these techniques, the mass spectrum of charged
molecules is recorded systematically across the tissue surface, allowing researchers to
generate spatial maps of molecular protein abundance.

2.8.4 Tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence

As a prominent example of antibody-based spatial proteomics, t-CyCIF creates a
multiplexed image by collecting low-plex images from the same sample in cycles and
then assembling them into one high-dimensional representation (Lin Jia-Ren et al.,
2018). This fluorophore-labeled technique allows for multiplexing up to 60 protein
markers.

The standard t-CyCIF workflow begins with sections cut from FFPE blocks, followed
by dewaxing and antigen retrieval. The pre-staining with secondary antibodies is
performed to reduce the autofluorescence (Fig. 5). The pre-staining is followed by a
chemical bleaching, including oxidation in a high-pH hydrogen peroxide and light
exposure. All subsequent cycles follow the iterative process: 1) staining with antibodies
against protein antigens, 2) counterstaining with DNA dye, 3) imaging, and 4)
fluorophore bleaching. Notably, the signal-to-noise ratio is increased with successive
cycles due to the reduction of intrinsic tissue autofluorescence. Finally, the
computational image processing steps include illumination correction and stitching to
generate the final multiplexed image.
 27

tissue, iterative cycles of staining and bleaching, and downstream computational image analysis.

Aims and objectives of the study
The goal of this study is to characterize the molecular features and mechanisms
inherent to highly proliferative “susbister” niches in post-NACT samples of HGSC. In
particular, the study focused on identifying biological programs enabling tumor cell
chemotherapy subsistence. To achieve this, the study had the following objectives:

 • To integrate t-CyCIF and GeoMx to map highly proliferative “subsister” tumor
 niches and annotate these hubs in paired GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data.

 • To identify the gene expression programs and signaling pathways operating in
 chemotherapy-subsisting tumor niches

 • To identify key biological processes and pathways that may contribute to
 therapy resistance.

These analyses provided insights into the mechanisms supporting chemotherapy
subsistence and highlighted potential therapeutic vulnerabilities, with relevance for
improving treatment strategies in patients with resistant HGSC.
 29


3 Material and Methods
3.1 Cohort description for t-CyCIF experiment

Cohort data were obtained from participants in the prospective ONCOSYS-OVA
clinical trial. This clinical trial was conducted at the University of Helsinki and Helsinki
University Hospital. This trial facilitated the collection of surgical specimens and
detailed clinicopathological data, including variables such as age, tumor histology, and
FIGO tumor stage. Additional clinical variables, such as progression-free survival
(PFS), PFI, and overall survival (OS), are available for all patients. The study was
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic at Helsinki University

nd
FIGO tumor stage. Additional clinical variables, such as progression-free survival
(PFS), PFI, and overall survival (OS), are available for all patients. The study was
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic at Helsinki University
Hospital and Helsinki Biobank. Patient identifiers and personal information in the
clinical data are securely stored on the HUH server with access restricted to authorized
personnel only.

Interval debulking surgery (IDS) specimens were collected from patients who had
undergone neoadjuvant chemotherapy treatment (NACT) (N=69) and were diagnosed
with the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) stage III-IV
HGSC.

The cohort includes a subset of samples previously profiled with GeoMx and Xenium
spatial transcriptomics technologies (Fig. 6). Samples that have not been previously
profiled were included if derived from the metastatic omentum, exhibited a tumor
purity greater than 10%, and were free of hemorrhage or necrosis. These strict
inclusion criteria were designed to ensure a uniform tissue site and sufficient cancer
cell material. A total of 69 samples were included.

3.2 Sample preparation and marker selection for t-CyCIF experiment

For spatial and molecular profiling, formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
blocks were sectioned at a thickness of 5 m and placed on Superfrost slides. To ensure
spatial concordance across different modalities, consecutive sections separated by 5-
15 m were utilized for GeoMx and t-CyCIF analyses.
 31

The t-CyCIF slides were stained with a validated antibody panel across 7 cycles
targeting 26 protein markers. Most markers were selected to identify cell types
(stromal, immune, cancer) and assess functional cell cycle states. The markers
included are displayed in Figure 7.

3.3 t-CyCIF experiment

3.3.1 Slide baking, deparaffinization, and rehydration

Slides were incubated overnight in the oven at 37°C. On the following day, the slides
were baked at 56°C for 1 hour. Next, they were deparaffinized and dehydrated through
the xylene and alcohol series (xylene 3 × 5 min, followed by sequential rehydration
immersions in 100%, 90%, 70%, 50%, and 30% ethanol 3 min each). Finally, the slides
were washed twice in 1×PBS (2 × 1 min) to complete the rehydration and prepare the
tissue for further staining.
 32

3.3.2 Antigen retrieval

Antigen retrieval was done with a freshly prepared 10 mM citric acid buffer (pH 6.0).
The process was conducted in a pressure cooker containing 550 mL of Milli-Q water in
the main chamber and 225 mL of citric acid buffer in each slide rack holder. The
antigen retrieval was performed using a preset protocol at 95°C for 45 min. Following

e process was conducted in a pressure cooker containing 550 mL of Milli-Q water in
the main chamber and 225 mL of citric acid buffer in each slide rack holder. The
antigen retrieval was performed using a preset protocol at 95°C for 45 min. Following
retrieval, the slides and the citric acid buffer were transferred to a new rack holder and
cooled under the fume hood. Once the slides had cooled, they were washed in 1×PBS
(3 × 3 min).

3.3.3 Pre-staining

Pre-staining was done in a humidified chamber. Sections were incubated with 200 µL
of Odyssey blocking buffer for 30 min at room temperature. Following blocking, the
slides were incubated for 60 minutes at room temperature with 200 µL of a pre-
staining cocktail containing secondary antibodies and Hoechst (see Table 3).
