A bleaching solution was prepared consisting of 120 mL of 1×PBS, 22.5 mL of 30%
H₂O₂, 7.5 mL of 480 mM NaOH. Slides were immersed in this solution within a
transparent rack and placed on an LED light source. Photobleaching was performed
for 90 min at room temperature, changing the buffer solution every 30 min. Following
photobleaching, the slides were washed in 1×PBS (4 × 3 min).

3.3.4 Primary and secondary antibody staining

The slides were incubated with primary unconjugated antibodies overnight at 4°C.
Following the incubation, the slides were washed with 1×PBS (3 × 3 min).

ng, the slides were washed in 1×PBS (4 × 3 min).

3.3.4 Primary and secondary antibody staining

The slides were incubated with primary unconjugated antibodies overnight at 4°C.
Following the incubation, the slides were washed with 1×PBS (3 × 3 min). Secondary
antibodies (1:2000 dilution) and Hoechst (1:1000 dilution) in Odyssey buffer were
 33

applied for 1 hour at RT. Next, they were washed in 1×PBS (3 × 3 min). The area
surrounding the tissue section was dried with fiber-free tissue paper. Slides were then
mounted with 100 µL of 70% glycerol in 1 × PBS, coverslipped and scanned using a
RareCyte CyteFinder scanner.

3.3.5 Subsequent staining cycles

Following the initial scan, coverslips were removed with 1×PBS. Next, slides
underwent photobleaching, changing the solution every 25 min. The slides were
washed in 1×PBS (4 × 3 min). Next, they were stained with pre-conjugated primary
antibodies (recommended dilution) and Hoechst 1:10000 at 4°C. They were washed 3
× 3 min with 1×PBS. Afterwards, the slides were mounted with 100 µL of 70% glycerol
in 1 × PBS, coverslipped, and scanned.

The iterative staining workflow was executed over seven full cycles, each comprising a
sequence of chemical photobleaching, antibody incubation, and digital imaging.

3.4 t-CyCIF image processing

Image preprocessing and analysis were conducted through the sequence of steps,
which included illumination correction, image stitching, segmentation, signal
quantification, phenotyping with TRIBUS, and downstream analyses (Kang et al.,
2025). Illumination correction was performed with BaSic, an image correction method
based on low-rank and sparse decomposition (Peng et al., 2017). Image stitching and
registration were carried out using ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous

ang et al.,
2025). Illumination correction was performed with BaSic, an image correction method
based on low-rank and sparse decomposition (Peng et al., 2017). Image stitching and
registration were carried out using ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous
Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency Registration) (Muhlich et al., n.d.). ASHLAR is a
multi-aimed open-source Python tool for image preprocessing. It performs stitching
and alignment of adjacent tiles within each cycle of immunofluorescence. It also aids
in aligning tiles from different cycles, covering the same region. Following that, cell
segmentation was performed using StarDist (Schmidt et al., 2018). Finally, the
channel-specific signal intensities were quantified at the single-cell level to enable the
phenotyping.
 34


Figure 8. Multiplexed image processing and snalysis workflow. Overview of the computational pipeline,
including illumination correction (BaSiC), image stitching (ASHLAR), cell segmentation (StarDist), signal
quantification, and single-cell phenotyping (TRIBUS), followed by downstream image alignment (Warpy)
and ROI annotation. Diagram created with BioRender (2026).


3.5 t-CyCIF cell phenotyping

Quantified t-CyCIF data were phenotyped using TRIBUS (Kang et al., 2025). TRIBUS
is a knowledge-based classifier for multiplexed images that allows single-cell
phenotyping based on marker combinations in fluorescence images without relying on
manual gating.

For this study, cells were classified into “Tumor” and “Stroma” at the global level
according to the predefined logic tables (Fig. 9). In the second classification level, the
tumor cells were further subdivided into four categories: “Tumor general”, “Tumor
Ki67+pRb+", “Tumor pRb+”, and “Tumor Ki67+”.

tumor cells were further subdivided into four categories: “Tumor general”, “Tumor
Ki67+pRb+", “Tumor pRb+”, and “Tumor Ki67+”.

Next, to refine the tumor classifications, the “Tumor pRb+” and “Tumor-general”
categories were merged. Additionally, the “Tumor pH3+” category was added, with a
gating threshold of 600. Finally, the quality control of phenotyping was performed
using the integrated Napari widget (Chiu Chi-Li et al., 2022).

3.6 GeoMx spatial transcriptomics: ROI selection and data acquisition

GeoMx spatial transcriptomics procedure was performed at the University of Helsinki
(Färkkilä lab) following the standard NanoString Technologies guidelines. The region
of interest (ROI) selection was driven by specific cellular phenotypes identified via t-
CyCIF.

For this study, we utilized the subset of this data, comprising 305 ROIs obtained from
33 samples. This subset included 18 AOIs derived from tumor regions and 287 AOIs
coming from the tumor-stroma interface (TSI).

3.7 GeoMx and t-CyCIF data pairing

t-CyCIF and GeoMx images were aligned using the Warpy workflow (Chiaruttini et al.,
2022). It is a Python-based framework designed for whole slide image (WSI)
registration in Fiji and succeeding analysis in QuPath. This workflow is designed to

ring

t-CyCIF and GeoMx images were aligned using the Warpy workflow (Chiaruttini et al.,
2022). It is a Python-based framework designed for whole slide image (WSI)
registration in Fiji and succeeding analysis in QuPath. This workflow is designed to
register slides using complex spline-based transformations with ImgLib library,
elastix, and BigWarp.
 36

For the alignment process, the GeoMx was selected as a fixed source and t-CyCIF image
as a moving source. The moving and fixed images were initially manually aligned
within Fiji. Then, the rectangular registration region was selected. After choosing the
number of landmarks within the rectangle, the Elastix component in Fiji executed the
registration. The result of the registration was saved to the JSON file.

Next, the ImageCombinerWarpy extension in QuPath was used to combine registered
WSIs into a new QuPath image entry, using the registration information recorded in
JSON file. The combined image allowed the transfer of the regions of interest from
GeoMx to the t-CyCIF image.

The custom Groovy-based scripts were used for the import of the original GeoMx ROI
coordinates and mapping them onto the t-CyCIF image using the Warpy extension.
Following this automated transfer, the manual quality control check of the ROI
transfer was performed to ensure alignment accuracy. Finally, the mapped ROI
coordinates were transferred to the CSV file and the spatial accuracy was verified using
Napari.
 37

3.8 GeoMx label transfer

Marker-quantified data, along with the observations, were compiled into the AnnData
object (Virshup et al., 2024). Where applicable, each cell in the matrix was annotated
with the ROI from the corresponding aligned GeoMx image.

ROIs were selected across three distinct spatial regions: the TSI, the tumor core, and
distant stroma. Based on Pan-Cytokeratin (PanCK) staining, each ROI was segmented
into either one or two Areas of Illumination (AOIs) corresponding to tumor (PanCK+)
and/or stroma (PanCK-) compartments.
 38

Tumor cell phenotypes within each ROI were evaluated, and AOIs were ranked
according to the proportion and counts of highly proliferative cells. Proliferative niches
were then stratified into quartiles, allowing for a direct comparison between the highly
proliferative (highest quartile) and low proliferative (lowest quartile) niches in
posterior analyses.

3.9 Random Forest (RF) for identification of unique transcriptional programs

Selection of pathways for hierarchical clustering was performed using an RF
architecture, implemented via the scikit-learn library in Python. To ensure robust
model performance and address data imbalances, the following configurations were
applied:

 • Ensemble Configuration: The RF model was constructed using the
 RandomForestClassifier from sklearn.ensemble library, using an ensemble of
 2,000 individual decision trees.

 • Feature Importance Ranking: Candidate features were evaluated and
 selected based on the Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI). This metric,

RandomForestClassifier from sklearn.ensemble library, using an ensemble of
 2,000 individual decision trees.

 • Feature Importance Ranking: Candidate features were evaluated and
 selected based on the Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI). This metric,
 accessed via the feature_importances attribute, quantifies a feature's
 contribution to the model by calculating the total reduction in the optimization
 criterion (Gini impurity) across all nodes and trees in the forest.

 • Class Imbalance Mitigation: To account for uneven class distributions
 within the dataset, we employed the class_weight='balanced' parameter. It
 automatically adjusts weights in the input data, ensuring the RF classifier
 remains sensitive to the minority class.

3.10 Differential gene expression analysis and single-sample gene set
 enrichment analysis on GeoMx data

To characterize the transcriptional architecture inherent to highly proliferative tumor
niches, gene expression profiles were analyzed using raw GeoMx digital count
matrices. This pseudo-bulk transcriptomic approach allowed for the assessment of the
total niche environment, including both tumor cells and surrounding
microenvironmental components. Following the rigorous quality control, raw counts
were normalized using the DESeq2 framework and subjected to a variance stabilizing
 39

transformation (VST) to correct the mean-variance relationship. Finally, the Harmony
algorithm was used to reduce technical batch effects.

Differential gene expression (DGE) analysis was performed on the VST-normalized
pseudo-bulk data using a linear-mixed effects model (LMM). The use of LMM was
essential to account for the hierarchical structure of the data, particularly the nesting
of multiple AOIs within individual patient samples.

erformed on the VST-normalized
pseudo-bulk data using a linear-mixed effects model (LMM). The use of LMM was
essential to account for the hierarchical structure of the data, particularly the nesting
of multiple AOIs within individual patient samples. For each gene, a model was fit -
using the lme4 package in R, implemented with the following formula:

 y ~ AOI_category + (1 | Sample)

In this model, y represents the normalized expression values, while AOI_category serves
as the fixed effect. To control patient-specific confounding, the Sample effect was
treated as a random intercept. Model-derived log2 fold changes and p-values were
extracted for each feature, and significantly differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were
prioritized for functional interpretation.

To identify the functional programs active within the bulk niche environment, single-
sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) was utilized to calculate per-AOI
enrichment scores. This analysis utilized the Hallmark, KEGG, BioCarta, and Gene
Ontology Biological Process pathway collections from the Molecular Signatures
Database. To determine which pathways were significantly enriched between the
highly and low proliferative niches, an LMM was applied to these precomputed ssGSEA
scores by utilizing the fixed and random effects parameters described previously.

3.11 Gene set enrichment analysis on GeoMx data

Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was performed using GSEA software v4.4.0
(Broad Institute). Raw gene expression profiles from tumor AOIs were stratified into
two groups based on t-CyCIF spatial phenotyping annotations - highly proliferative
and low proliferative.

Enrichment analysis was implemented using the C2 REACTOME gene set collection
from MSigDB.

ession profiles from tumor AOIs were stratified into
two groups based on t-CyCIF spatial phenotyping annotations - highly proliferative
and low proliferative.

Enrichment analysis was implemented using the C2 REACTOME gene set collection
from MSigDB. Default GSEA parameters were applied, utilizing 1000 permutations,
and permuting by phenotype. For each pathway, the normalized enrichment score
(NES) and false discovery rate (FDR) were calculated. In addition, the percentage of
genes contributing to the enrichment signal (leading edge subset) was computed to
 40

evaluate pathway representation. Pathways with an FDR < 0.05 were considered
significantly enriched.

4 Results
4.1 High-plex spatial proteomics captures cell cycle and phenotypic cell states
 in post-NACT HGSC

To investigate the spatial organization of tumor cell populations following NACT, a t-
CyCIF experiment was performed on a cohort of 69 post-NACT HGSC samples derived
from 64 patients (Fig. 11).

ll cycle and phenotypic cell states
 in post-NACT HGSC

To investigate the spatial organization of tumor cell populations following NACT, a t-
CyCIF experiment was performed on a cohort of 69 post-NACT HGSC samples derived
from 64 patients (Fig. 11). High-quality multiplexed imaging data were successfully
generated across 66 viable samples (three tissues were excluded during the experiment
due to poor tissue quality). This comprehensive dataset captured a wide range of tissue
architectures, enabling the assessment of both intra- and inter-patient heterogeneity
in tumor composition and phenotypic states.


Figure 11. t-CyCIF Cohort Overview. This heatmap confirms successful spatial proteomics profiling for
the final analytical cohort of 69 samples. All specimens were collected at IDS following NACT to
specifically study the "subsister" cell landscape. While samples primarily originate from the metastatic
omentum, the cohort also includes adnexal and ovarian sites.


Building upon our initial exploration of highly proliferative Ki67+pRb+ niches, we
expanded the t-CyCIF antibody panel to comprehensively assess critical biological
programs within the TME (Fig. 12). The spatial expression of these markers assisted in
precise tumor phenotyping. Most importantly, the expanded panel successfully
resolved cell cycle states across the tissue landscape, identifying cell cycle quiescence
(p27) and the G1/S transition (Cyclin D1 (CCND1)), complementing standard Ki67 and
pRb profiling. Furthermore, the newly introduced markers successfully delineated
EMT (captured via E-cadherin and N-cadherin), stress response (c-Jun), stemness
(ZIC2), and DNA damage (gH2AX).

4.2 Spatial cell phenotyping reveals substantial interpatient heterogeneity in
 the distribution of proliferative tumor cell states

TRIBUS classification of quantified t-CyCIF data revealed a clear segregation of tumor
and stromal compartments based on predefined marker logic (Fig. 9). Within the
tumor compartment, we observed additional phenotypic heterogeneity, resolving cells
into proliferative phenotypic states: Tumor Ki67+, Tumor Ki67+pRb+, and Tumor
pH3+.

Firstly, cells were classified into tumor and stromal compartments. Posterior sub-
classification of tumor cells identified multiple phenotypes, including Tumor-general,
Tumor Ki67+, Tumor Ki67+pRb+, and a rare Tumor pH3+ positive proliferative
subset.

Quality control using integrated Napari visualization confirmed the robustness of

ssification of tumor cells identified multiple phenotypes, including Tumor-general,
Tumor Ki67+, Tumor Ki67+pRb+, and a rare Tumor pH3+ positive proliferative
subset.

Quality control using integrated Napari visualization confirmed the robustness of
phenotype assignments and their alignment with protein marker expression. Together,
these results demonstrate that our phenotyping approach resolves proliferative
heterogeneity within tumor compartments and captures proliferative tumor cell states,
providing the basis for downstream spatial transcriptomics analyses.
 43

To assess inter-patient heterogeneity, the proportions of TRIBUS-defined tumor
phenotypes were quantified across individual patients. A stacked barplot of cellular
composition revealed marked variability in tumor subpopulation distributions
between patients (Fig. 13). Individual patients exhibited different compositional
profiles of tumor phenotypic states. While proliferative tumor phenotypes accounted
for up to 58% of all cells in some samples, other samples contained less than 1% of
proliferative tumor cells, demonstrating substantial inter-patient heterogeneity.
Overall, substantial inter-patient variability was observed in the proportions of
TRIBUS-defined tumor cell phenotypes.

4.3 Alignment of spatial transcriptomics and proteomics enables the multi-
 omic spatial mapping of "subsister" niches displaying marked proliferation
 profiles

To ensure downstream analysis focused on the tumor, ROIs transferred from the
GeoMx dataset to t-CyCIF images were restricted to tumor and TSI ROIs. The initial
distribution of tumor cell counts across ROIs varied significantly (0-350 cells per ROI).
To reduce the impact of low-tumor density regions, we excluded the bottom 10th
percentile of (n=34), with a minimum threshold of 12 tumor cells per ROI.

The remaining ROIs were classified into four groups based on the relative proportions
and absolute counts of tumor cells representing Ki67+pRb+ highly proliferative tumor
 44

niches. Specifically, ROIs were stratified into four groups: (i) high proportion/high
counts (highly proliferative) (n=122), (ii) high proportion/low counts (n=31), (iii) low
proportion/high counts (n=35), and (iv) low proportion/low counts (low proliferative)
(n=117). This metric enabled the identification of ROIs enriched in proliferative states
while accounting for variability in ROI size. Notably, the majority of AOIs originated
from TSI regions.

For downstream analyses, we focused on the phenotypic extremes (highly vs. low
proliferative) to maximize biological contrast. Voronoi-based spatial analysis further
supported this classification, revealing unique architectural features between these
niches (Fig. 14). Specifically, highly proliferative niches show dense clusters of actively
proliferating tumor cells, whereas low proliferative niches consisted of non-
proliferative tumor cell populations.

4.4 Spatial transcriptomics confirms that highly proliferative niches are
 governed by coordinated transcriptional programs

Due to the highly proliferative tumor states observed in our proteomics analysis, we
next set out to investigate the underlying transcriptional profiles of these tumor niches.
Integration of spatial transcriptomics data confirmed that these niches are driven by
coordinated gene expression programs.

4.4.1 Proliferative niches effectively display transcriptional upregulation of canonical
 proliferation genes

Tumor niches classification with proteomics was validated through the expression
analysis of established proliferative marker genes (Fig. 16A). Mann-Whitney U tests
demonstrated that AOIs classified as highly proliferative showed significantly higher
expression of core proliferative marker genes compared to low. Specifically, MKI67,
TOP2A, CDK1, CENPF, UBE2C, CDC20, PCNA, and STMN1 showed significant up-
regulation within highly proliferative niches. These findings prove a consistency
between the protein-level selection criteria and the underlying RNA expression profiles
across the cohort.
 46

regulation within highly proliferative niches. These findings prove a consistency
between the protein-level selection criteria and the underlying RNA expression profiles
across the cohort.
 46

4.4.2 Global transcriptional partitioning with machine learning shows disparate
 biological states

To identify the most robust transcriptional drivers of these environments, we utilized
ssGSEA scores. Unlike standard GSEA, which evaluates pathway enrichment between
predefined population cohorts, ssGSEA computes an independent pathway
enrichment score for each AOI. We then applied an RF classifier to rank ssGSEA
pathway importance. The most influential features (n=3100) were selected for
unsupervised hierarchical clustering (Fig. 16B). This high-dimensional feature
selection with RF classifier ensured that the clustering was based on the most
discriminative biological signals.

The resulting dendrogram successfully separated the dataset into three dominant
transcriptional branches, effectively capturing high- and low- proliferation signatures.
Interestingly, while the functional state of the niche acts as a primary driver of
transcriptional similarity, inter-patient heterogeneity remains a significant factor in
global architecture. Specifically, the proliferative sub-clusters were composed
predominantly of AOIs from the same patient. This indicates that core proliferative
programs are conserved across the cohort. Importantly, the global transcriptome is
simultaneously shaped by the unique genetic and environmental background of each
patient.

 and carbohydrate metabolism alongside mitotic machinery in highly

We next sought to identify the specific differentially expressed genes and pathways
driving these functional differences. Differential analysis of transcriptomic profiles
between high- and low-proliferation niches revealed significant divergence at both the
gene and pathway levels. Statistical significance was assessed using a LMM to account
for multiple AOIs per sample.

To determine the functional landscape of these regions, single-sample Gene Set
Enrichment Analysis (ssGSEA) scores were compared across spatial niches
(Differential Enrichment Score > 0.02, P < 0.01) (Fig. 17). Highly proliferative niches
were characterized by a characteristic transcriptional signature displaying metabolic
reprogramming, cell cycle processes, and neuro-specific signaling. As expected for
rapidly dividing cell populations, there was enrichment in structural and mitotic
pathways, including MEIOTIC CHROMOSOME SEPARATION, MICROTUBULE
DEPOLYMERIZATION, and PROTEIN LOCALIZATION TO CONDENSED
CHROMOSOMES, alongside mechanisms of DNA replication like DNA STRAND
ELONGATION. To meet the energetic and biosynthetic demands of this rapid division,
these niches exhibited significant metabolic shifts; GLYCINE METABOLIC PROCESS
emerged as the most significantly enriched pathway, supported by the upregulation of
ASPARTATE and GALACTOSE metabolic processes. Interestingly, the analysis also
revealed a strong signature of neuro-associated pathways, including REGULATION

SPECIALIZATION MEMBRANE and CHOLINE TRANSPORT.

Gene-level analysis identified a set of DEGs distinguishing the spatial niches (|log2 fold
change| > 0.3, P < 0.001) (Fig. 18). Within the high-proliferation niches, FGD5, a gene
involved in cytoskeleton remodeling and stem-like signaling, exhibited the most
profound upregulation, representing the highest level of statistical significance in the
dataset (-log10 P > 5.5). Additional top-ranked genes significantly upregulated in these
highly proliferative regions included SOX13, a transcription factor known to drive
tumor plasticity and, notably, CKAP5, which plays a crucial role in microtubule
dynamics and mitotic spindle organization.

4.6 Highly proliferative niches employ coordinated mechanisms for replicative

To identify the biological processes driving the specific phenotypes of the identified
niches, GSEA was performed on the VST-transformed RNA expression data with the

Analysis of the high-proliferative niches revealed significant enrichment in the core

immense synthetic requirements of these niches, gene sets involved in DNA
metabolism, specifically DNA Replication and Synthesis of DNA, were enriched.

5 Discussion
5.1 Defining the highly proliferative “subsister” niches via spatial multi-omics

The clinical management of HGSC is characterized by a high initial response rate
followed by a common recurrence, a phenomenon often attributed to the survival of
chemotherapy-resistant cell populations. While the presence of residual disease post-
NACT is a known prognostic factor, the molecular characteristics of the specific cells
that survive first-line chemotherapy selective pressure remain poorly defined. In this
study, we have explored a subset of aggressive tumor cells that survive first-line
chemotherapy and have the potential to fuel relapse. Utilizing a spatial multi-omics
framework, we mapped and characterized these survival hubs, highly proliferative
niches, which, as we hypothesize, represent the aggressive population of
“subsister“cells. Characterizing the underlying transcriptomics mechanisms of these
niches is crucial for understanding the biological programs enabling chemotherapy
subsistence. Ultimately, understanding these adaptations provides the knowledge
necessary to design novel targeted treatment strategies.

5.2 Conserved architecture of proliferation and stress survival

the biological programs enabling chemotherapy
subsistence. Ultimately, understanding these adaptations provides the knowledge
necessary to design novel targeted treatment strategies.

5.2 Conserved architecture of proliferation and stress survival

Unsupervised hierarchical clustering of over 3,000 transcriptional ssGSEA pathways,
preselected by RF classifier, revealed that niches group primarily by their functional
state (highly vs. low proliferative) rather than by patient identity. This is a relevant
finding, since HGSC is characterized by great patient heterogeneity and genomic
diversity. Our results suggest that, even though the evolutionary paths of HGSC tumors
differ, biological adaptations to chemotherapy may be shared across individuals.
Therefore, the fact that highly proliferative niches in HGSC exhibit a common
transcriptional program is promising for the development of novel targeted treatments
and patient stratification approaches.

To bettter understand these patterns of adaptation to chemotherapy, we first examined
the functional landscape of highly proliferative niches using ssGSEA and GSEA.
Importantly, pathway enrichment confirmed that these cells are locked in a highly
proliferative state, characterized by the upregulation of gene sets driving active
progression through the cell cycle and DNA replication.

Our analysis further characterized the basis of a coordinated adaptation program,
which may assist tumor cells in surviving chemotherapy. First, we observed a specific
 54

metabolic shift characterized by the enrichment of the Glycine Metabolic Process.
Established literature identifies glycine as a vital precursor of purine synthesis and
glutathione production, driving rapid cancer cell proliferation and antioxidant defense

ic shift characterized by the enrichment of the Glycine Metabolic Process.
Established literature identifies glycine as a vital precursor of purine synthesis and
glutathione production, driving rapid cancer cell proliferation and antioxidant defense
(Amelio et al., 2014). Therefore, its upregulation indicates that “subsister” cells
metabolically adapt to support their high biosynthetic demands, while managing
oxidative stress. Crucially, our study adds to the current literature by demonstrating
that this metabolic reprogramming is not a uniform tumor-wide adaptation, but rather
a spatially restricted vulnerability within specific tumor niches.

Furthermore, according to our findings, highly proliferative niches exhibit an
increased capacity to withstand chemotherapy-induced genomic damage. While
established literature demonstrates that platinum resistance in HGSC relies heavily
on a cell’s ability to tolerate and repair DNA lesions and stabilize replication forks
(Damia & Broggini, 2019), our findings provide a novel spatial context for this. The
enrichment of pathways related to DNA repair, G2/M cell cycle checkpoints, and the
sumoylation of DNA replication proteins in highly proliferative niches points to a
specialized spatially restricted response to severe replicative stress. The upregulation
of these mechanisms suggests that highly proliferative “subsister” cells avoid mitotic
catastrophe by stabilizing stalled forks and managing DNA damage, allowing them to
pass through mitosis under conditions that would be lethal to sensitive cells. These
localized mechanisms present novel therapeutic vulnerabilities. Disrupting
replicative fork stabilization, abrogating G2/M checkpoint, or targeting the

wing them to
pass through mitosis under conditions that would be lethal to sensitive cells. These
localized mechanisms present novel therapeutic vulnerabilities. Disrupting
replicative fork stabilization, abrogating G2/M checkpoint, or targeting the
sumoylation machinery may drive these surviving cells into the cell death they
otherwise avoid.

5.3 Immune evasion and stem-like plasticity

Finally, our analysis further revealed that “subsister” cells actively engineer the tumor
microenvironment. The targeted enrichment of Wnt/-catenin signaling and PD-L1
transcription pathways suggests these niches are actively evading the immune system.
Wnt signaling is a known mediator of immune exclusion, while PD-L1 is a primary
driver of T-cell inhibition (Nguyen et al., 2019). Together, the localized activation of
these pathways likely prevents immune cell infiltration and blocks the immune
responses.
 55

Reflecting these broad pathway-level adaptations, our DGE analysis highlighted
specific transcriptomics markers associated with aggressive “subsister” phenotypes.
Most notably, we observed the upregulation of FGD5 and SOX13, the drivers of stem-
like features in cancer cells. This observation suggests that highly proliferative niches
maintain “stem-like” plasticity required for continuous proliferation within a stressful,
post-treatment microenvironment. Furthermore, the downregulation of CCND1,
essential for G1/S transition, strongly aligns with our previous findings linking the
upregulation of CCND1 to better prognosis. This downregulation may indicate a “cyclin
switch”, potentially reflecting the reliance of CCNE1 instead of ensuring rapid
transition through the G1 phase.

5.4 Strengths and limitations


A central achievement of this study was successfully linking our prior protein-level
explorations to deep RNA-level characterization. By integrating high-plex
immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) with spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx DSP), we
effectively bridged these modalities. The primary strengths of this study lie in its cohort
size (n=66), which is notably large for high-plex spatial profiling, and the successful
multi-omics integration. By utilizing the Warpy alignment pipeline, we were able to
bridge the 5-15 mm gap between serial sections with high fidelity, effectively mapping
spatial transcriptomics signatures (GeoMx) onto established protein architecture (t-
CyCIF). The high concordance between the protein-based phenotypes and the
underlying transcriptomics signatures, specifically the significant upregulation of core
proliferative marker genes such as MKI67, TOP2A, CDK1, CENPF, UBE2C, CDC20,
PCNA, and STMN1 in highly proliferative niches, serves as a robust validation of our
methodology. This approach allowed us to evaluate the post-NACT TME with a level of
spatial precision that traditional bulk and single-cell sequencing cannot achieve.
Furthermore, our rigorous computational pipeline combining RF classification with
unsupervised hierarchical clustering, DGE profiling, GSEA, and ssGSEA-based
pathway enrichment ensures the reliability of our functional state characterizations.
Finally, this study possesses high clinical translation potential, establishing the

ith
unsupervised hierarchical clustering, DGE profiling, GSEA, and ssGSEA-based
pathway enrichment ensures the reliability of our functional state characterizations.
Finally, this study possesses high clinical translation potential, establishing the
framework for discovering actionable molecular targets within highly proliferative
“subsister” niches.
 56

Despite these strengths, certain limitations must be acknowledged. The inherent "z-
axis" variation between adjacent slides means that while we are analyzing the same
niche, we are not analyzing the same cells. This may account for some of the noise in
our hierarchical clustering, despite the strong clustering of highly proliferative AOIs.
Furthermore, while GeoMx DSP provides deep transcriptomic data, it remains a "bulk"
measurement within a defined area. Additionally, a limitation of this study is the
absence of an independent validation cohort to confirm the identified transcriptomics
signatures. Finally, although our analysis suggests active immune evasion, direct cell-
to-cell immune interactions within the TME were not explicitly assessed. Future
studies using single-cell spatial transcriptomics (e.g., Xenium) will be essential to
confirm if genes like FGD5 are expressed in highly proliferative niches and to
accurately map tumor-immune interactions.

Overall, this study successfully fulfilled its aims by defining the molecular landscape of
residual disease in HGSC. We have demonstrated that despite patient-specific genomic
differences, these niches utilize a conserved transcriptomic program centered on cell
cycle checkpoints, DNA replication, replicative stress management, and glycine
metabolism.

5.5 Future perspectives

Based on these results, we think that evaluating drug resistance profiles of these niches

ed transcriptomic program centered on cell
cycle checkpoints, DNA replication, replicative stress management, and glycine
metabolism.

5.5 Future perspectives

Based on these results, we think that evaluating drug resistance profiles of these niches
via computational transcriptomics (e.g., BeyondCell Scoring) will help guide the
development of targeted therapies. An important next step will be to validate our
findings in an independent cohort by utilizing publicly available spatial
transcriptomics datasets (e.g., those acquired via CosMx or Visium) to establish the
broader applicability of the highly proliferative “subsister” niche signature.

Future research should prioritize functional validation in patient-derived organoids to
determine if disrupting specific DNA repair mechanisms, cell cycle checkpoints or
stem-like signaling can successfully collapse the “subsister” niches and prevent tumor
regrowth. Ultimately, this study provides the spatial and molecular roadmap required
to understand HGSC residual disease and transition HGSC maintenance therapy to a
highly proliferative niche-targeted precision strategy.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_1 - pilot experiment/Cell_cycle results own thoughts - 13.10.23.docx
vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
Obtained results I presented in AACR: 
Longer PFS was associated with higher % of arrested cells within each of the compartments
The differences were the most striking in cancer and t cells (CD4,CD8), this was less noticeable in myeloid and stromal cells
Proliferation state proportions were diverse when comparing short and long pfs
Lymphocytes post-NACT showed similar patterns to cancer cells. Long PFS showed 
Spatial distances
This part is very interesting
Resting and arrested CD8 T cells were closer to myeloids in postNACT Short-PFS, while proliferative were far away. Similar pattern in Long-PFS but CD8 T cells seemed in general terms more distant to myeloids
Spatial co-ocurrence in between cell-types change in after chemotherapy
Long PFS seemed to be maintaining many neighbourhoods present in the pre, and showed increased interactions within proliferative cells of all types. Cancer proliferative cells seem to be interacting with many immune cell types that are also proliferating.
Short PFS lost many of those initial interactions seen in Pre, especially within the proliferative compartment (CD4-CD8, CD4-Cancer, CD8-myeloid,CD8-Cancer, CD4-CD8). Also, proliferative myeloid cells lose many of their interactions with Cancer and CD8.
Observations/questions:
p21 is the majoritary arrest-driver for some samples.p21 review: 
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471491417300205?via%3Dihub
I
 have seen both cytoplasmic and nuclear staining patterns
 In some studies, the oncogenic function of p21 was credited to unconventional cytoplasmic localization of p21 that inactivates pro-apoptotic proteins.
p21 and Ki67 are co-expressed in a subset of atypical cells of high-grade/poorly differentiated, advanced human carcinomas
.

PAX8 is a positive regulator of TP53 expression in HGSC. However, the oncogenic properties of mutant p53 in HGSC render PAX8 a positive regulator of transformation rather than a growth suppressor. Furthermore, unexpectedly [28], the mutant p53 upregulates p21 in HGSC, which in this type of cancer plays a non-canonical 
cytoplasmic pro-proliferative
 role, further enhancing the cancer-promoting effect of PAX8.

ubiquitous p21 cytoplasmic staining was not restricted only to chemotherapy resistant cells.

vector_chunks.text:
not observe any difference in p21 staining and localization between previously treated and non-treated tumors, implying that the 
ubiquitous p21 cytoplasmic staining was not restricted only to chemotherapy resistant cells.
Non-canonic D1 expression
The nuclear accumulation of cyclin D1 is indicative of a high mitotic index in cancer cells, and is considered to be a prognostic marker.Cyclin D1 overexpression, as observed in cancer cells, antagonizes the checkpoint-induced cell cycle arrest, disturbs DNA replication and allows damaged DNA to be replicated
Cytoplasmic and membrane-associated cyclin D1 regulates tumor cells invasion and dissemination
.Cytoplasmic cyclin D1 associates with structural proteins of the cytoskeleton, affecting cell shape
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6961668/
HOW DOES IT WORK: Cyclin D1 expression requires the activation of RAS-mediated signaling cascades, which is accompanied by phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)/AKT-dependent CCND1 translation and a decrease in cyclin D1 degradation. The active cyclin D1/CDK4/6 complexes move to the nucleus, and cyclin D1 levels increase from early to late G1 phase, subsequently decreasing during S phase, when cyclin D1 is exported into the cytoplasm for degradation by the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS). This requires the phosphorylation of the threonine 286 (Figure 1(b)), which is catalyzed by, at least, three kinases: glycogen synthase-3β (GSK3β), p38 MAP kinase (MAPK) and extracellular signal-regulated kinases (ERK1/2) [28,29]. In the cytoplasm, the ubiquitylation of phosphorylated cyclin D1 is dependent on a SCF complex consisting of the SKP1 adaptor protein, the CUL1 scaffold protein and a F-box proteins.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_1 - pilot experiment/Cell_cycle results own thoughts - 13.10.23.docx
vector_chunks.text:
(MAPK) and extracellular signal-regulated kinases (ERK1/2) [28,29]. In the cytoplasm, the ubiquitylation of phosphorylated cyclin D1 is dependent on a SCF complex consisting of the SKP1 adaptor protein, the CUL1 scaffold protein and a F-box proteins. Mutations or alterations of cyclin D1-specific E3 ubiquitin ligases contribute to cyclin D1 accumulation in cancers [33,34]. In turn, 
the promotion of cyclin D1 degradation suppresses cancer cell growth
. This can be achieved by the knockdown of USP2 coding a cyclin D1-specific deubiquitylase or by the use of inhibitors.
Cyclin D1 has a nuclear export signal and is a cargo of exportin 1 (XPO1). However, it has no nuclear localization signal and cannot enter the nucleus on its own. Instead, its nuclear entry is favored both by the CIP/KIP (CDK-interacting protein, kinase inhibitory protein) family members and CDK4, and cyclin D1/CDK4/p21CIP1/p27KIP1 complexes are imported into the nucleus upon activation
Different cell-cycle signals might be ! in the microenvironment: 
TME: 
M2 macrophages secrete miR-15a and miR-92a to glioma cells through exosomes, and then miR-15a and miR-92a separately bind to CCND1 and RAP1B, thereby blocking the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway to inhibit glioma invasion and migration
The Two-Faced Nature of p21 May Depend on its Subcellular Localization:
When in the nucleus, p21 can act as a tumor suppressor: it may inhibit CKIs in G1 or G2/M cell cycle arrest initiated by p53; it may downregulate many, but not all, DNA repair pathways; it can limit the proliferation of stem cells; and it can also induce senescence 
[10]
.

Objectives:
Following steps:
Re-evaluate with more calm the phenotype and state division, still worried about the “comb” effect, maybe arrested phenotypes should be more complex
Which non-intuitive double positives are there? 
Are there “escaped cells”, I think it would be interesting to identify them and review their neighbors
Ki67-p21
PCNA-p21?
CyclinA-p21
CyclinA-p27
Which are the staining patterns of functional markers?
 Re-evaluate alone and in combination, distinct subcellular localization accounts for different biological functions-> specifically interesting in cancer cells, but I have also seen eg p21 in stromal-like cells. It would be good to have a tool allowing me to do it, but I guess I need to do it eye-based at the moment.
Idea for pixel-based/combination with nuclear masks:
If the center pixels of the nuclei have lower value than the pixels on the most external part the staining is most likely cytoplasmic
If the center pixels of the nuclei are uniform or higher than the external the staining pattern is most likely nuclear
Interesting to see whether these patterns change pre-post NACT
Distances/distributions:
I would like to explore in depth the kind of distribution in space of arrested-labeled patterns.

4.10 Expansion — Stitching Quality Control Notes
Stitching and registration with Ashlar - Guidelines for reporting 
Go through
 each sample one by one 
in Napari and visually check and 
Report
Stitching verification: That all tiles are registered in all cycles
2) Marker order verification: The cycles are registered in the right order - correct looking marker expression in each cycle 
3) Registration verification: tiles are aligned correctly: check 1st and last cycle 
In practice - can be done in your laptop using Napari 
open one sample, check the channels one by one and check that i) signal matches the marker ii) no tiles are missing in any marker
Overlay DAPI signal from 1st to last cycle - 1st cycle in red, last in green and confirm that you have only yellow nuclei -> repeat steps 1 and 2 for all sample one by one

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.docx::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.docx
vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
Main structures of interest
:
 Tumor cells and tumor-stromal interface
Key markers
: 
Functional, cellcycle (cyclins, pRb, geminin, pH3, PCNA, Ki67) and stress (cJUN)
DNA repair (pRPA, gH2Ax), EMT markers (NCadherin ECadherin, vimenin, asma)
Scanning instructions
Background for 488, 555 and 647 needs to be scanned (cycle0)
Staining instructions for cycle 0-1
Make prestaining with the corresponding secondary, bleach, scan backgrounds 488,555,647, 750. Exposure times here should be similar to those in first cycle pre-Xenium cycif. You can even use the same.
Then add first cycle unconjugated, add secondaries, wash, then incubate with the primary conjugated and scan all these as cycle1
How to adjust the exposure times
Use XXX and XX slides as reference every day
In the functional cellcycle markers we want the best possible RANGE.
 It is important to be able to differentiate the background and the signal
Make sure signal 
does not exceed 40-50k
, intensities may vary a lot across different spots of the slide, and even across slides.
Recommendation: When adjusting the exposures, use the slides with more than one tissue, and check across different areas of the four sections to confirm no overexposure is present.
Pre-Experimental Practices
Order antibodies and reagents in advance
Check availability of reagents ahead of time
Check in advance which antibodies can be diluted more
Mark those that can be diluted with a star.
Ensure adequate storage space for data
Experimental Practices
Communication
Active slack use, avoid talking to others while they focus-work
Refer to experiments by their specific name (e.g., Expansion CycIF).
Always leave notes when working with blocks/slides.
Share daily update in SLACK for Expansion

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ractices
Communication
Active slack use, avoid talking to others while they focus-work
Refer to experiments by their specific name (e.g., Expansion CycIF).
Always leave notes when working with blocks/slides.
Share daily update in SLACK for Expansion
Key-slides Handling (we just have one section adjacent to GeoMx)
Handle these carefully, especially when placing over bleaching solution.
Check the up/down slides and relabel stickers.
Use gray boxes for bleaching
Resection blocks if the current setting doesn’t work (antibodies show strange pattern)
Tissue Detachment
If >5 tissues begin detaching, notify Ada and make a plan.
If >15 tissues detach, place spacers immediately.
Otherwise:
No spacers in cycles 1-3 unless detachment occurs.
‘If tissues are detaching place spacers in 
ALL
 cycles.
Slide Management
Ada will provide a guide on when to re-scan and when not to.
Plan and divide work into batches.
Follow standard naming conventions and ordered scanning practices.
Post-Experimental Practices
Daily Backups
Data must be backed up in at least two locations.
Book Workstations in Advance
Ensure sufficient computational resources are available for analysis.
Update Code in GitHub
Keep all experimental and analysis code updated to prevent loss.
Review and Analyze Results
Ensure proper discussion and documentation of experiment outcomes for future planning.
Scanning quality
You may look for the following:
Antibodies showing the expected pattern ->
Some antibodies like CCNE, pRPA or p21 are not expected to be seen in all samples, if they work in some of them and do not show signal in others it is normal and expectable
 If not, we should internally check if we continue scanning or if we repeat the staining
Key antibodies affected -> repeating staining

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.docx
vector_chunks.text:
be seen in all samples, if they work in some of them and do not show signal in others it is normal and expectable
 If not, we should internally check if we continue scanning or if we repeat the staining
Key antibodies affected -> repeating staining
5th/6th cycles antibodies affected -> continue scanning, replanning experiment after that round of staining
If p27 does not work -> we continue
Intense antibody aggregates
If they are tiny and all around, this counts as the antibody not working.
If there big but constrained to a small area (<5-10% of the tissue), we continue
The aggregate is outside the tissue, or above fat areas, we continue
Blurry antibody aggregates
They are round and have lower or similar signal than 
positives. If
 we have a CD4, CD45, CD3D, CD8a, CD20, FOXP3 antibody, we do not care. If it is functional we can correct but it is not ideal. If it covers <10% of the tissue, continue, otherwise wash, re-mount and scan again.
Computer-generated artifacts (we will ALWAYS ignore these)
If you see tiles missing, squared tiles suddenly appearing etc, just ignore and continue
Hairs, fibers, etc. If they do not cover tumor area, continue. If they cover tumor area, remount and re-scan 
Blurry tissue - redo the focus points and re-scan. Changing slightly the DAPI intensity might help.DAPI is not inside the analysis and therefore exposures can be modified to help improve focusing, but please DOCUMENT.

Big aggregates (follow)

Is antibody showing expected pattern?
YES
: Continue scanning.
NO
: Is the antibody critical (tumor, cellcycle functional, DNA repair)?
YES

: Continue scanning.

vector_chunks.text:
Staining Protocol - Summary for Cyclical Scanning Quality Control
Is antibody showing expected pattern?
YES
: Continue scanning.
NO
: Is the antibody critical (tumor, cellcycle functional, DNA repair)?
YES
: Repeat staining.
NO
: Continue scanning.
After 5th/6th cycle, is antibody showing expected pattern?
YES
: Continue scanning & Replan after 6th cycle.
NO
:Co ntinue scanning & Replan after 6th cycle
Are there intense antibody aggregates?
YES
:
Aggregates < 5-10% of tissue or outside tissue/fat
: 
Continue scanning.
Small and dispersed aggregates >5-10% tissue
Go to 1)NO and pick your solution.
Large aggregates
: Remount and re-scan.
NO
: Continue scanning.
Are there blurry antibody aggregates?
YES
:
< 10% of tissue and filterable antibodies 
(e.g., CD4, CD45, CD8,CD20,etc - NON-FUNCTIONAL)
: 
Continue scanning.
> 10% or non-functional aggregates
: Wash, re-mount, and re-scan.
NO
: Continue scanning.
Are there computer-generated artifacts (missing tiles, unexpected Appearances)?
YES/NO
: Ignore and continue scanning.
.
Are there hairs, fibers, etc.?
YES
:
Covering tumor area
: Remount and re-scan.
Not covering tumor area
: Continue scanning.
NO
: Continue scanning.
Is tissue blurry?
YES
: Re-do focus points and re-scan (adjust DAPI intensity if necessary).
NO
: Continue scanning.

### word/document.xml
# Base paths
TARGET_BASE="/Volumes/h30492/farkkilab2/Backup/4_CellCycle/2025_4_10_Expansion/raw"
SOURCE_BASE="/Volumes/h30492/farkkilab2/Backup/4_10_Expansion/cycle0_main"
# Loop through all cycle0 folders
for src_folder in "$SOURCE_BASE"/*; do
 if [ -d "$src_folder" ]; then
 # Extract sample name (like S005_iOme)
 folder_name=$(basename "$src_folder")
 sample=$(echo "$folder_name" | sed -E 's/^4_([^_]+_[^_]+)_.*$/\1/')
 # Corresponding target folder
 target_folder="$TARGET_BASE/$sample"
 if [ -d "$target_folder" ]; then
 # Copy all .rcpnl files
 cp -v "$src_folder"/*.rcpnl "$target_folder"/
 else
 echo "Warning: target folder $target_folder not found, skipping."
 fi
 fi
done

Technical

Scientific

Integrating findings from very distinct platforms

## Slide 11
Scientific questions
How can we better classify these “arrested” phenotypes?
The preliminary analysis shows how the number of cells in these phases varies across samples, I would like to subcategorize patients based on those (eg S-phase enriched, M-enriched). CCNE1 rich-patterned patients seemed to have increased S-phase cells, which correlates with their biology. Maybe this strategy could also help stratifying the patients.
This data might be useful to create a replication stress score, and to also address checkpoint recovery

Selecting neighborhoods for spatial transcriptomics
Functional tests and validations? ->
TBD with María

vector_chunks.text:
Cell-cycle resolved
tumor metaclusters
 27-02-2025 Ada - Anniina
Big-picture Ideas
Considerations for metaclustering


•Fluorescence Variability: Differences between samples complicate normalization and cross-sample comparisons.

•Unknown Cluster Count: The number of clusters is not predefined, and some metaclusters may be unique to specific samples.

•Cell cycle aberrancies: Tumors’ cell cycle is aberrant in a unique way, making the co-expression of certain markers possible

•Validation Requirement: Image-based validation is essential to confirm accuracy and biological relevance.
Current clustering strategies
Current clustering strategies (I) – Sample level
Our main goal is to define how to integrate the clusters


 Quite manual How do we classify the
 Has not really worked
 Hard to automate sample-specific clusters?
Current clustering strategies (II) – PRIMUS
PRIMUS is an expectation maximization-based tool


 Expectation Maximization
 Expectation (E-step) - Guess the hidden info
 Based on the current best guess, estimate the
 probability that each person belongs to each
 group.

 Maximization (M-step) - Improve the guess.
 Update your guesses using the probabilities from
 the E-step to make them more accurate.
 Current clustering strategies (II) – PRIMUS

 tCycIF
 matrix
 imageIDs

Vector of 1s


 Expectation (E-Step) – Estimate Hidden Variables
 •For each marker in each cell, the model estimates how much of the observed count comes from nuisance effects and how much
 from biological clusters.
 Maximization (M-Step) – Update Estimates
 •Using the probabilities from the E-step, refine the estimates of:
 •The nuisance expression centroids
 •The latent phenotypic expression centroids
 •The cluster memberships
 Repeat Until Convergence

 Which are our controls?
 The last proteopicking experiment failed


•Results are disappointing, with weak signals barely distinguishable from background noise. Scrapes worked

•Unexpected Findings: An empty slide had more protein IDs than some actual samples, raising concerns about
sample mix-up or excessive background measurement.
•Experiment Setup: Used an LC column and method for single-cell proteomics, expected to slightly improve
performance.

empty slide had more protein IDs than some actual samples, raising concerns about
sample mix-up or excessive background measurement.
•Experiment Setup: Used an LC column and method for single-cell proteomics, expected to slightly improve
performance. Instrument performance was verified and deemed not responsible for low IDs.
•Potential Issues: Sample loss (e.g., retention in tubes) or degradation, possibly worsened by prolonged storage.
•Sample Collection Concerns: Otto asks if there were significant differences in sample collection that could have
affected results.Lina says she was just more careful with the process.

•Less Polymer/Keratin Interference: A positive aspect, but possibly at the cost of losing signals from important
proteins.

•Next Steps: Otto suggests discussing the findings over a Zoom call.

### word/document.xml
Research Progress Report Template
Project Title: 
Profiling of Spatial Tumor Microenvironment Architecture in Highly Multiplexed Images of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma
Student Name(s): 
Alva Grönholm
Supervisor: 
Ada Junquera
Start Date: 
August 2024
Expected Completion Date: 
March 2025
GitHub Repository (if applicable):
Github repository
Shared Folder Link:
3.Alvas_MSc
Project Overview
Brief Description of Research Goals:
Refine image-derived quantified data through data normalization and assignment of precise phenotype labels to the cells of the tumor microenvironment (TME). Explore the composition of the TME by analyzing the abundance of phenotypes across the cohort. Compare two different spatial analysis methods—spatial count and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA)—using different parameters (radius: 13, 25, 35 microns) and assessing their suitability for various types of analysis. Determine which approach is better suited for specific analytical goals and compare the results between distinct clinical groups to explore potential differences and trends.
Finished figures for thesis stored in memory stick: 
PENDR-ADA:\Alva\Finished_figures
Step 1: Composition analysis
Description:
 Analyze the composition of (1) global cell types and (2) immune cell types across the cohort.

Notes:
Patients are categorized into Long, Medium, and Short PFS groups based on the 33rd percentile. 
Step 2: Spatial analysis - computing spatial count and LDA
Description:
 Compute spatial count and LDA and cluster the results into RCNs. Spatial count and LDA are computed using the following radii: 13 microns (40 px), 25 microns (77 px), and 35 microns (108 px). The results are clustered using KMeans clustering, with the optimal number of clusters (k) determined using elbow curves and the 
KneeLocator
 method from the 
kneelocator
 package. Annotation of RCNs based on barplots and image verification. 
Date Started:
November 2024
Expected Completion Date:
February 2025

vector_chunks.text:
Profiling of Spatial Tumor Microenvironment
Architecture in Highly Multiplexed Images of High-
 Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma


 Master’s Programme in Translational Medicine,
 Translational Research Study Track
 Master's thesis


 Author:
 Alva Grönholm


 Supervisor(s):
 MD PhD Anniina Färkkilä
 MSc Ada Junquera Mencía


 7.5.2025
 Helsinki
Faculty: Faculty of Medicine
Degree programme: Translational Medicine
Study track: Translational Research
Author: Alva Grönholm
Title: Profiling of Spatial Tumor Microenvironment Architecture in Highly Multiplexed
Images of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma
Level: Master’s thesis
Month and year: 05.2025
Number of pages: 62
Keywords: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, tumor microenvironment, tCycIF, spatial
analysis, spatial count, LDA, recurrent cellular neighborhood
Supervisor or supervisors: Anniina Färkkilä, Ada Junquera Mencía
Where deposited: E-thesis, University of Helsinki
Abstract:
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is the most lethal subtype of ovarian cancer,
characterized by genomic instability, late diagnosis and high rates of recurrence. The tumor
microenvironment (TME) plays a critical role in disease progression and treatment resistance,
with its spatial organization offering potential insights into therapeutic outcomes. Advances in
highly multiplexed imaging and computational analysis have enabled deeper characterization
of the TME, yet optimal strategies for analyzing the TME architecture remain to be explored.

This thesis investigates the spatial organization of the TME in chemotherapy-treated HGSC
patients by applying two spatial analysis methods, spatial count and Latent Dirichlet Allocation
(LDA), to tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) imaging data. By analyzing recurrent

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
he spatial organization of the TME in chemotherapy-treated HGSC
patients by applying two spatial analysis methods, spatial count and Latent Dirichlet Allocation
(LDA), to tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) imaging data. By analyzing recurrent
cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) across multiple spatial scales, the ability of each method to
detect spatial patterns and their clinical relevance is assessed.

The analysis reveals interpatient heterogeneity in the HGSC TME, with distinct immune,
stromal, and tumor composition. Spatial count effectively captured fine-grained, localized
structures but showed sensitivity to spatial scale. In contrast, LDA identified subtle spatial
patterns and remained more stable across radii. Notably, stromal-enriched RCNs were more
abundant in patients with shorter progression-free survival (PFS), suggesting a link between
spatial architecture and clinical outcome.

In conclusion, spatial profiling provides valuable insights into the spatial organization of the
TME and its prognostic significance. Spatial count and LDA offer complementary strengths,
and the choice of method and spatial resolution should be guided by the specific biological
question. These results emphasize the importance of integrating spatial analysis into HGSC
research and highlight its potential for improving patient stratification and tailoring treatment
strategies.

1 Literature review

1.1 High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma

Ovarian cancer is the third most prevalent gynecological cancer, known for its poor
prognosis and high mortality rate. The high mortality rate is affected by the delayed
diagnosis influenced by the late onset of symptoms, the tumor's asymptomatic growth,
insufficient screening methods as well as the absence of reliable biomarkers for early
detection (Momenimovahed et al., 2019). Globally, ovarian cancer is the 7th most
common cancer and the 8th leading cause of cancer-related deaths in women (Lisio et
al., 2019). In Finland, around 500 women are diagnosed with ovarian cancer annually,
with a five-year survival rate of 48% (Munasarjasyöpä, n.d.).

Ovarian cancer is a highly heterogeneous disease, classified into two main types: type
I and type II tumors.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
019). In Finland, around 500 women are diagnosed with ovarian cancer annually,
with a five-year survival rate of 48% (Munasarjasyöpä, n.d.).

Ovarian cancer is a highly heterogeneous disease, classified into two main types: type
I and type II tumors. Type I tumors are typically low-grade, genetically stable and
associated with favorable clinical outcomes. In contrast, type II tumors are high-grade,
genetically unstable and progress rapidly accounting for 90% of ovarian cancer deaths
(Kurman & Shih, 2016).

Among type II tumors, high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and
lethal subtype of all ovarian cancer (Momenimovahed et al., 2019). HGSC is thought
to originate in the secretory cells of the fallopian tube, enabling it to spread easily into
the peritoneum due to the lack of anatomical barriers (Bowtell et al., 2015). HGSC is
often diagnosed at advanced stages, such as FIGO stage III, when the disease has
spread beyond the pelvis into the peritoneal cavity, or stage IV, when the disease has
spread beyond the peritoneal cavity (Matulonis et al., 2016; Momenimovahed et al.,
2019).

The incidence of HGSC increases in women over the age of 65, with the median age at
diagnosis ranging from 50 to 79 years (Momenimovahed et al., 2019). Symptoms of
HGSC are often vague and non-specific, including abdominal pain, nausea, bloating,
and fatigue (Lisio et al., 2019). Risk factors for HGSC include lifetime ovulation cycles
and the presence of germline mutations in the BRCA gene (Kurman & Shih, 2016).

HGSC is characterized by genomic instability, with most tumors harboring TP53
mutations. Approximately 50% of HGSC cases exhibit defects in the homologous
recombination (HR) repair pathway, including BRCA1/2 mutations, which are found
 9

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0005
