vector_chunks.text:
ular interactions.

Most changes in RCN composition occur between 13 µm and 25 µm

In the spatial count method, most observed shifts occur between the 13 µm and 25 µm
radii. The largest splits are observed in the “Tumor core”, “TSI-tumor” and stroma
RCNs, reflecting the increasing diversity of neighboring cells at larger scales (Fig. 7a).
Additionally, at 25 µm, a distinct “immune compartment” RCN emerges, composed of
various immune cells from previously separate immune RCNs, including CD4+ T cells
and CD163- macrophages, as well as cells from the stroma RCNs with immune
infiltration (“Stroma 1” and “Stroma 2”). Increasing the radius from 25 µm to 35 µm
results in fewer changes, with immune cell RCNs remaining consistent.

In LDA, a more pronounced splitting pattern is observed between radii, with most
divergence seen between 13 and 25 µm (Fig. 7b). The majority of splits are concentrated
within the tumor core and the RCNs near the tumor, with cells at the tumor border
becoming more distinct and forming separate RCNs. In contrast, immune cell RCNs
remain relatively consistent across radii, with the CD4+ T cell RCN staying distinct and
uniform across all scales. However, as noted previously, B cells exhibit variability in
LDA, clustering with macrophages at 13 µm and with CD163+ DCs at 35 µm,
highlighting shifts in spatial associations among some immune cells across scales.
 40


Figure 7. Clustree diagrams showing the hierarchical relationships between RCNs across spatial scales
(13 µm → 25 µm → 35 µm) using a. spatial count and b. LDA. The diagrams illustrate how clusters
evolve as the spatial radius increases, with node size representing the number of cells within each RCN.

relationships between RCNs across spatial scales
(13 µm → 25 µm → 35 µm) using a. spatial count and b. LDA. The diagrams illustrate how clusters
evolve as the spatial radius increases, with node size representing the number of cells within each RCN.
Edge color shows the cell count that has transitioned into a particular RCN, and edge color intensity
indicates the percentage of cells that have moved into a specific RCN. Edges representing fewer than
10% of the total population have been excluded.
 41


Spatial count shows RCN convergence with increasing radius, while LDA
tends to maintain distinct RCNs

A key distinction between the two methods is how they diverge across spatial scales.
LDA, particularly at the 13 µm radius, demonstrates a greater tendency to split some
RCNs into finer subclusters, while preserving certain cell type-specific RCNs,
highlighting its ability to better distinguish between cell types. In contrast, the spatial
count method tends to converge, with RCNs merging into broader categories across
radii. For instance, immune RCNs such as CD4+ T cells, CD163+ macrophages, and
CD163- macrophages merge into a broader immune compartment as the radius
increases. In LDA, these immune cell types remain more distinct across all radii,
reflecting its greater tendency for divergence and finer differentiation of cell types.

The tumor core RCN is the largest RCN across all radii and both methods. As the radius
increases to 25 µm, the tumor core RCN diverges into several distinct RCNs, including
TSI-tumor and TSI-stroma. At 13 µm, the tumor core splits into six RCNs in LDA and
four in spatial count, whereas at 25 µm, it splits into three in LDA and two in spatial
count. TSI RCNs are more diffuse at smaller radii, encompassing a wider variety of

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0046
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
tumor and TSI-stroma. At 13 µm, the tumor core splits into six RCNs in LDA and
four in spatial count, whereas at 25 µm, it splits into three in LDA and two in spatial
count. TSI RCNs are more diffuse at smaller radii, encompassing a wider variety of
neighboring cell types. As the radius increases, TSI RCNs become more structured and
distinct, with the clustree analysis showing a clear reorganization into new subtypes
between 13 and 25 µm. By 35 µm, the TSI-tumor RCN splits further into two separate
RCNs, contributing to a more organized architecture around the tumor. Interestingly,
the TSI RCNs appear more distinct in spatial count than in LDA (Fig. 8).
 42


Figure 8. Representative RCN masks computed using spatial count (top) and LDA (bottom) at 13 µm,
25 µm, and 35 µm radii. As the radius increases, the tumor core RCN splits into more distinct TSI RCNs.
Spatial count shows more spatially defined TSI RCNs, especially at larger radii, while LDA identifies a
broader variety of TSI RCNs overall.

As shown previously, endothelial RCNs increase in heterogeneity as the spatial scale
expands, primarily due to the broader neighborhood of cells captured around the blood
vessels. In both methods, the clustree analysis reveals that the endothelial RCN
identified at smaller radii is preserved as the radius increases, but it becomes
increasingly integrated with surrounding cells from stroma RCNs that contain immune
infiltration. This transition reflects the growing complexity of the cellular environment
as larger regions of the tissue are captured, with the endothelial RCNs blending into
more heterogeneous structures that incorporate immune cells and stromal
components.
 43


4.2.4 Spatial distribution and enrichment of RCNs across clinical groups show

arger regions of the tissue are captured, with the endothelial RCNs blending into
more heterogeneous structures that incorporate immune cells and stromal
components.
 43


4.2.4 Spatial distribution and enrichment of RCNs across clinical groups show
 consistent trends across methods and radii

Next, we analyzed the distribution of RCNs across distinct clinical groups to determine
whether spatial neighborhood composition is associated with clinical outcomes. By
comparing patients in the short and long PFS groups, we examined how spatial scale
and computational methods influence the detection of clinically relevant differences.

Hierarchical clustering of the relative abundances of RCNs revealed similar patient
clustering across both methods and radii, with a clear distinction between clinical
groups (Supplementary Figure 2).

In the spatial count method, enrichment patterns of specific RCNs in the short and
long PFS groups remained consistent across all radii (Fig. 9a). RCNs for “Stroma,
immune excluded”, “Endothelial”, and “CD8+ T cells” showed increasing enrichment
in the short PFS group as the radius increased. The observed enrichment of CD8+ T
cells in the short PFS group aligns with the earlier findings from the composition
analysis of immune cells. Additionally, immune-infiltrated stromal RCNs (“Stroma,
immune infiltrated”, “Stroma 1” and “Stroma 2”) were enriched in the short PFS group,
while “tumor core” and “TSI-tumor” RCNs were consistently enriched in the long PFS
group.

The LDA method produced similar enrichment trends across clinical groups (Fig. 9b).
Again, stromal RCNs were enriched in the short PFS group, while “tumor core” and
“TSI-tumor” RCNs were more abundant in the long PFS group. However, B cell-
associated RCNs displayed more variability.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
richment trends across clinical groups (Fig. 9b).
Again, stromal RCNs were enriched in the short PFS group, while “tumor core” and
“TSI-tumor” RCNs were more abundant in the long PFS group. However, B cell-
associated RCNs displayed more variability. At 13 µm, B cells clustered with
macrophages, while at 35 µm, they clustered with CD163+ DCs, leading to different
enrichment trends. B cell RCNs were generally enriched in the short PFS group.
However, when associated with CD163+ DCs, they showed enrichment in the long PFS
group, while B cells clustered with macrophages showed lower enrichment in the short
PFS group.
 44


Figure 9. a. Dotplot showing log₂ fold change in RCN enrichment between short and long PFS groups
at 13 µm, 25 µm, and 35 µm radii using spatial count. Positive values indicate enrichment in the long
PFS group, while negative values indicate enrichment in the short PFS group. b. Dotplot showing the
same analysis as (a) but using LDA. Similar to (a), positive values indicate enrichment in the long PFS
group, while negative values indicate enrichment in the short PFS group.

Overall, both methods and radii captured similar trends in RCN enrichment across
clinical groups, with the largest radius showing the most extreme enrichment in most
RCNs. Endothelial cells showed consistent enrichment in the short PFS group
(p<0.05) (Fig. 10). Furthermore, stromal RCNs were consistently enriched in the short
PFS group, reflecting the higher abundance of stromal cells observed in the global cell
type composition analysis. The spatial analysis further differentiated the stroma into
immune-excluded and immune-infiltrated regions, revealing that immune-infiltrated
stroma was significantly associated with the short PFS group across radii and methods
(p<0.05).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0049

vector_chunks.text:
on analysis. The spatial analysis further differentiated the stroma into
immune-excluded and immune-infiltrated regions, revealing that immune-infiltrated
stroma was significantly associated with the short PFS group across radii and methods
(p<0.05). In contrast, immune-excluded stroma showed a weaker enrichment trend in
the short PFS group.

The tumor core showed a modest increase in enrichment across methods and radii in
the long PFS group, while TSI-tumor RCNs, located at the tumor border, displayed a
stronger enrichment trend in the long PFS group. At the largest radius, TSI-tumor
further divided into two RCNs, with the one in closer proximity to the tumor core (TSI-
tumor 1 and TSI-1) showing a more pronounced enrichment in the long PFS group.
This trend was more pronounced in LDA (p=0.0082) compared to spatial count
(p=0.051).
 45


Figure 10. Comparison of RCN enrichment across radii and methods. For clarity, we focus on
comparing the smallest (13 µm) and the largest (35 µm) radii. Boxplots show the percentage abundance
of each RCN in short (light blue) and long (dark blue) PFS groups across five RCNs. For some spatial
scales, two similar annotated RCNs exist, both of which are included here. Wilcoxon p-values are shown
for each boxplot, indicating statistical significance of enrichment differences between clinical groups.
 46


5 Discussion

Studying the TME is crucial for understanding the complexity of disease progression
and therapeutic resistance in HGSC (Rodriguez et al., 2018). In particular, analyzing
the spatial organization within the TME can reveal clinically relevant features, such as
differences among patient subtypes (Launonen et al., 2022), treatment-induced
changes (Liu et al., 2021), or patient prognosis (Nirmal et al., 2022). Advances in highly

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0050
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ial organization within the TME can reveal clinically relevant features, such as
differences among patient subtypes (Launonen et al., 2022), treatment-induced
changes (Liu et al., 2021), or patient prognosis (Nirmal et al., 2022). Advances in highly
multiplexed imaging, coupled with computational approaches, now allow for the
detection of fine-grained spatial patterns that are often invisible to the human eye (T.
Fu et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2018). However, the most suitable approach for analyzing the
spatial architecture of the TME remains unclear.

In this study, we aimed to evaluate and compare two such methods, spatial count and
LDA, for their ability to identify relevant spatial structures across different spatial
scales. By phenotyping 6.47 million single cells, we identified distinct tumor, stromal,
and immune subtypes, revealing interpatient heterogeneity in both global cell
composition and immune microenvironments. These findings highlight the complexity
of HGSC and the importance of computational approaches to define spatially distinct
regions within the TME.

Spatial analysis revealed distinct differences between spatial count and LDA in how
cells were grouped into RCNs, with both methods being sensitive to the chosen radius
and the underlying cell size. Spatial count excelled at detecting small, well-defined
structures, such as TLSs. However, it also demonstrated high sensitivity to spatial
scale, while smaller radii preserved fine-grained distinctions, larger radii led to the
convergence especially of immune-related RCNs, highlighting the importance of
carefully selecting the radius.

In contrast, LDA generated more stable and homogeneous RCNs across spatial scales,
consistently identifying immune compartments even as the radius increased. This

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0051
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
f immune-related RCNs, highlighting the importance of
carefully selecting the radius.

In contrast, LDA generated more stable and homogeneous RCNs across spatial scales,
consistently identifying immune compartments even as the radius increased. This
robustness reflects LDA’s ability to capture smoothly transitioning microenvironments
and preserve coherent patterns, as shown in previous studies (Chen et al., 2020;
Nirmal et al., 2022). Such properties make LDA particularly useful for capturing more
dispersed immune patterns and maintaining neighborhood coherence in the TME.
 47


Notably, most structural changes of RCNs occurred between the 13-25 µm radii,
suggesting that RCN structures become more similar and less distinct as the radius
increases. Another noteworthy finding was that at the largest spatial radius (35 µm),
the clearest distinctions between long and short PFS groups emerged. Patients with
short PFS exhibited increased enrichment of stromal neighborhoods, suggesting that
stromal expansion is associated with poorer prognosis. This aligns with previous
studies showing that stromal cells are associated with immune exclusion and poor
treatment response (Zhang et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2023), further supporting the
diagnostic potential of RCN profiling. Interestingly, at larger radii stromal RCNs that
included immune cells were significantly more enriched in short-PFS patients,
potentially indicating immune suppression or ineffective infiltration into the tumor
area.

Both methods offer distinct insights into the TME, each with its own advantages and
limitations (Table 1). Spatial count provides detailed, structure-specific insights but is
sensitive to spatial scale and susceptible to noise. In contrast, LDA offers a more robust

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0052
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
er distinct insights into the TME, each with its own advantages and
limitations (Table 1). Spatial count provides detailed, structure-specific insights but is
sensitive to spatial scale and susceptible to noise. In contrast, LDA offers a more robust
view across scales and also detects disaggregated structures, such as less common
immune cells, though it may abstract regions, potentially reducing interpretability.
Choosing the most appropriate spatial method and radius should therefore be
hypothesis-driven and carefully considered.

 like TLSs and blood vessels subtle patterns in the TME
 48


This study is not without limitations. The relatively small cohort size increases
sensitivity to sample-specific effects especially when comparing clinical groups and
poses a risk of overfitting. Larger cohorts would help to validate the generalizability of
observed RCN patterns. Additionally, only a limited range of spatial radii was
evaluated, which may limit the ability to detect additional spatial patterns and
potential structural transitions. However, increasing the radius too much may lead to
excessive overlapping between cell neighborhoods, potentially blurring biologically
distinct regions.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf::chunk_0053
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Gronholm_Alva_Mastersthesis_2025.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ay limit the ability to detect additional spatial patterns and
potential structural transitions. However, increasing the radius too much may lead to
excessive overlapping between cell neighborhoods, potentially blurring biologically
distinct regions. Lastly, phenotyping was affected by challenges that are known to
affect highly multiplexed imaging analysis, including signal spillover, intermixed
staining patterns, and background fluorescence variability. These issues required
careful data processing, the use of multiple markers, and the addition of more
classification steps.

Emerging spatial approaches, such as spatial lag models, may offer additional insights
by detecting continuous gradients in protein expression across tissues, potentially
finding patterns that may be missed by methods like LDA and spatial count (Nirmal et
al., 2022). Additionally, the development of semi-automated or AI-driven tools for
segmentation and phenotyping, such as Tribus (Kang et al., 2025) and Mesmer
(Greenwald et al., 2022), holds potential for improving phenotyping accuracy and
reducing user bias. For broader biological interpretation, integrating spatial data with
other molecular profiling techniques, such as single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial
transcriptomics or genome sequencing, could provide a more comprehensive view of
tissue architecture and function. Studies combining such approaches have already
shown promising results in identifying clinically relevant spatial patterns in HGSC
(Launonen et al., 2024).

In conclusion, this thesis provides a comparative evaluation of spatial count and LDA
for profiling the TME in HGSC. Both methods offer complementary strengths, spatial
count effectively identifies localized microstructures, while LDA provides a more

n conclusion, this thesis provides a comparative evaluation of spatial count and LDA
for profiling the TME in HGSC. Both methods offer complementary strengths, spatial
count effectively identifies localized microstructures, while LDA provides a more
robust and comprehensive view of spatial organization. The choice of method and
parameters should be chosen based on the specific biological question and the spatial
resolution needed. Importantly, the study highlights the clinical relevance of spatial
profiling, supporting the use of computational tools in translational and diagnostic
workflows. Future efforts should aim to refine these methods and integrate them with
 49


other multi-omic data, potentially paving the way for more patient-tailored treatment
strategies in HGSC.
 50

Title
Deciphering the Functional Immune Composition and Its Clinical Impact in a Cohort of HGSC
HR- Proficient Interval Debulking Samples


Supervisors
Supervisor 1: Anniina Färkkilä, MD PhD
Principal investigator in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS),
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki.

Supervisor 2: Ada Junquera Mencía, MSc
Doctoral researcher in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS),
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki.


Unit
Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), Faculty of Medicine, University of
Helsinki


Scientific background
Ovarian cancer is one of the most prevalent gynecological cancers, known for its poor prognosis
and high mortality rate (Momenimovahed et al., 2019). This disease is highly heterogeneous
and includes several histological subtypes, with high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) being

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
e most prevalent gynecological cancers, known for its poor prognosis
and high mortality rate (Momenimovahed et al., 2019). This disease is highly heterogeneous
and includes several histological subtypes, with high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) being
the most lethal, responsible for 70-80% of ovarian cancer deaths (Bowtell et al., 2015). Its high
mortality rate is primarily due to the lack of effective treatment options and the delayed
diagnosis, often influenced by the late onset of symptoms, the tumor's asymptomatic growth,
and insufficient screening methods (Momenimovahed et al., 2019).

The standard treatment for HGSC is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by adjuvant
chemotherapy. For patients with advanced stages of the disease who are not suitable candidates
for PDS, the preferred option is neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval
debulking surgery (IDS) and adjuvant chemotherapy. (Armstrong et al., 2021) Although
patients initially respond well to chemotherapy, the majority eventually develop resistance to
the platinum-based chemotherapy (Böhm et al., 2016).


 1
Targeted therapies have emerged in this context as the main breakthrough within ovarian cancer
treatment. One of the most successful has been poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP)
inhibitors, which suppress DNA repair in cancer cells and have proven effective in tumors with
pre-existing defects in the homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair pathway (Bowtell et
al., 2015). However, patients with HR-proficient tumors do not benefit from PARP inhibitors.
In such cases, the primary goal of treatment is often to manage symptoms and relieve pain to
improve quality of life. Therefore, there is a critical need to further analyze the TME in HR-

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ith HR-proficient tumors do not benefit from PARP inhibitors.
In such cases, the primary goal of treatment is often to manage symptoms and relieve pain to
improve quality of life. Therefore, there is a critical need to further analyze the TME in HR-
proficient tumors to better understand its characteristics and role in disease progression,
potentially offering new treatment options for this patient subgroup. (Stiegeler et al., 2024)

Understanding the tumor microenvironment (TME) offers promising new targets for treating
HGSC and potentially improving existing therapies. The TME is a complex network in which
tumor cells interact with other cell types, such as fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and metabolites.
The TME plays a crucial role in the development of the primary tumor and the formation of
metastases. (Jiang et al., 2020) Immune cells within the TME have their own specific relevance,
contributing to both immune activation and immunosuppression. Tumor-infiltrating
lymphocytes (TILs), including both T cells and B cells, have been associated with longer
progression-free survival (PFS) (Rodriguez et al., 2018). In contrast, CD4+ regulatory T cells
(Tregs) and tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) of the M2 phenotype exhibit tumor-
promoting activity and immunosuppressive functions. The composition of immune cells within
the TME is strongly correlated with treatment responses and overall survival. (Hao et al., 2020)
Despite the promise of immunotherapies, they have largely failed to achieve significant results
in ovarian cancer, partly due to the complex immunosuppressive landscape of the tumor
immune microenvironment (TIME) (Rodriguez et al., 2018). Moreover, NACT treatment has
been shown to significantly influence both the immunostimulatory and immunosuppressive

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
n cancer, partly due to the complex immunosuppressive landscape of the tumor
immune microenvironment (TIME) (Rodriguez et al., 2018). Moreover, NACT treatment has
been shown to significantly influence both the immunostimulatory and immunosuppressive
functions of the TIME, affecting both the quantity and activity of immune cells (Xue et al.,
2023).

Further exploration of NACT’s effects on immune cells and their functional markers, especially
in HR proficient patient with limited treatment options, along with the integration of TME
analysis with genomic and molecular testing, is crucial (Jiang et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2023).


 2
Aims and objectives

Main aim
The primary objective of this master’s thesis is to investigate the tumor immune
microenvironment (TIME) in homologous recombination (HR) proficient HGSC IDS samples
using existing data from the ongoing Oncosys-OVA clinical trial. This analysis aims to provide
insights into TIME’s composition after chemotherapy and its impact on the clinical outcomes.

Sub aims
 1. Refining image-derived quantified data through data normalization and assignment of
 precise phenotype labels to the cells of the TME (accounting for cell type specific
 marker intensity), also identifying the most important functional states (determined by
 Ki67, pStat1, TIM3, FOXP3, PD-1).
 2. Exploring the composition of the tumor immune microenvironment by analyzing the
 immune functional states, their relative abundance across the cohort, as well as assessing
 the cellular diversity and the possible correlations within the cell types.
 3. Identify patient subgroups with similar immune profiles based on the samples’
 composition analysis and study the relationship between clinical factors (PFS, PFI,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf::chunk_0005

vector_chunks.text:
ssing
 the cellular diversity and the possible correlations within the cell types.
 3. Identify patient subgroups with similar immune profiles based on the samples’
 composition analysis and study the relationship between clinical factors (PFS, PFI,
 CRS) and the distinct immune cell types and the functional states.


Materials & methods

Materials
The data used in this thesis was collected from newly diagnosed patients in the Oncosys-OVA
clinical trial. 28 IDS samples were selected based on criteria such as progression-free survival
of more than six months and a minimum tumor purity of 10-15% according to an expert
pathologist. Surgical and clinical data were gathered, and BRCA1/2 mutational profiling along
with genomic scarring (ovaHRDscar) was conducted. This data enables a comprehensive
assessment of chemotherapy responses, genomic status, and disease progression.

From the same cohort, FFPE samples were collected and analyzed using tissue cyclic
immunofluorescence (tCycIF), a highly multiplexed immunofluorescence imaging technique.
In this antibody-based method, 3-4 antibodies are added in each cycle and imaged with a highly
reliable RareCyte CyteFinder II. Each sample underwent multiple imaging cycles with a panel
comprising 36 different markers to characterize cell populations in the TME, their functional

 3
states, cell cycle stages and DNA damage patterns. The image dataset to be utilized has already
been processed, segmented and quantified.

Methods
This thesis is entirely a bioinformatics project, with packages in Python and R employed
throughout its full extent.

Normalization of data
Normalization using state-of-the-art methods will be optimized in R.

Cell phenotyping with scimap

vector_chunks.text:
Methods
This thesis is entirely a bioinformatics project, with packages in Python and R employed
throughout its full extent.

Normalization of data
Normalization using state-of-the-art methods will be optimized in R.

Cell phenotyping with scimap

Utilizing specific bioimage analysis software called scimap in combination with Napari image-
viewer, cell type calling is performed on the images through the establishment of thresholds
and the creation of logic tables to assign the cell labels. Phenotypes are then identified by
analyzing the levels of the following markers: PanCK, PAX8, CD45, CD4, CD8a, CD20, IBA1,
CD163, CD11c, NKG2a, Vimentin, aSMA, FAP and CD31.

Data analysis

Data analysis and visualization will be performed using various packages in R and Python, such
as complexHeatmap, seaborn and pheatmap. The composition analysis will be performed using
different types of plots such as, bar plots, heatmaps, UMAPs and box plots. The clinical data
will then be correlated to the different subgroups of immune cells and the results from the
composition analysis.


Availability of required special equipment and materials

All computational infrastructure required for this thesis will be provided by Färkkilä lab. No
biological specimens are directly handled by the student and the clinical data will be stored
under the highest security standards as specified in the section below.


Ethical considerations

All data used in this thesis originates from the prospective Oncosys-Ova study and has been
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH)
and Helsinki Biobank.


 4
Patient IDs and other personal information in the clinical data will be handled with the highest

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ve Oncosys-Ova study and has been
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH)
and Helsinki Biobank.


 4
Patient IDs and other personal information in the clinical data will be handled with the highest
security and confidentiality on the HUH server, using the HUH Datalake at the Helsinki
Biobank. Access to clinical data is strictly restricted to authorized personnel for retrieval and
use. In publicly available data, each patient will be assigned a unique, randomized ID to prevent
any risk of patient identification.


Timetable
This project will be carried out between August 2024 and March 2025 (Figure 1) and is divided
into three aims, detailed in the sub-aims section. I will work part-time (80%) throughout the
thesis work. The project will start in August 2024 with Aim 1, which focuses on data
refinement. Aim 2, involving composition analysis of the TME, is planned for September and
October. Aim 3, investigating correlations between the composition analysis and clinical data,
is expected to start in November and be completed by January. The thesis writing is planned
for two months, from January to March 2025.


Figure 1. Timeline for master's thesis project. Figure created in Canva.


Supervision
I will receive ongoing guidance and feedback from my supervisors throughout the project.
Regular meetings will be held to review progress, discuss results, and adjust the project's
direction as needed to achieve the research goals.


References
Armstrong, D. K., Alvarez, R. D., Bakkum-Gamez, J. N., Barroilhet, L., Behbakht, K.,

 Berchuck, A., Chen, L., Cristea, M., DeRosa, M., Eisenhauer, E. L., Gershenson, D.

 5
 M., Gray, H. J., Grisham, R., Hakam, A., Jain, A., Karam, A., Konecny, G. E., Leath,

### word/document.xml
Processes to be targeted
Denoising/ Background correction
Signal enhancement filters
Segmentation metrics
Intersection over union (threshold)
F1 score 
Average precision
Precision recall curve
Data normalization 
Compression formats
###############################################################
Week 1: CREATING THE BENCHMARKING DATASET
Get familiar with tCycIF image handling and markers to be utilized
Crop relevant areas in tCycIF images - 4 (Tumor, Stroma, TLS, macrophage-rich)
Run Mesmer and Stardist on the crops to get some reference masks
Outcome: A 
dataset of annotated reference images
 for benchmarking.
Week 2-3: FILTER EXPLORATION AND PARAMETER TESTING
Select Preprocessing Filters to Evaluate
Suggestions include: 
Gaussian Blur, Unsharp Masking, CLAHE, Gamma correction: Decide on parameters to be tested and optimized to improve segmentation (over DAPI channel)
PENGUIN denoising: 
Welcome to PENGUIN’s documentation! — PENGUIN 0.1 documentation
Create code to test on this approach and benchmark the times of computation
Outcome:
Computational costs (RAM, time)
Preprocessed images with different filtering methods
Initial observations on how filters/parameters affect image features.
Week 4: SEGMENTATION METHODS
Select segmentation methods to be applied (Stardist,Cellpose,Mesmer)
Run on all filtered and unfiltered crops
Outcome
Segmentation results on crops for 
method combination
.
Image-based observations on how do these affect 
segmentation quality
signal range
are there cell-type-specific changes?
Week 5-6: PERFORMANCE ASSESSMENT 
Select Benchmark Metrics 
Evaluate the performance based on those benchmarks
Compare filtering vs unfiltered images
Compare across distinct combinations and parameters of filtering
Performance metrics for each

## Slide 1
CCNE
Antibody validation

## Slide 2
Order and results of the conducted experiments
Validation with Kauppi lab alliquote 1:1000 -> correct staining, we then ordered this vial
Exp2, full slide set,
CCNE 1:1000 + pChk1 1:200
-> CCNE did not show any good positive signal in nuclei, cytoplasmic signal in stroma and high background.
We realized it is actually not the same lot number as in Kauppi lab (but same clone and concentration)
Intermediate validation:
CCNE 1:1500 or CCNE1:2000.
Thought to check if
diluting more
our ab worked-> Really good pattern, 1:2000 had too low signal, so something in between 1:1600 was preferred
Exp3,
full slide set
, CCNE 1:1600 + pChk1 1:200
-> Fails again, multiple bubble-like structures and extremely low signal, but different from results in Exp2, here we have some nuclear signals. It is closer to the expected staining
Exp04, subselection of the slide set 4. Aimed to test a
cross reactivity
between CCNE and pChk1. Same dilutions were used (1:1600, 1:200) this time some of the slides were stained
only with CCNE and some in combination
-> We observed much better staining, more specific pattern and with much higher intensity, but also saw some aggregates and bubble-like structures
Additional validation with 7_01 slides from Zhihan (observe difference by using a different secondary, in this case
mouse 555
)

In human peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC), the equivalent to PMN-MDSC are defined as CD11b+CD14−CD15+ or CD11b+CD14−CD66b+ and M-MDSC as CD11b+CD14+HLA-DR−/loCD15−. CD33 myeloid marker can be used instead of CD11b since very few CD15+ cells are CD11b−

TANs expressing additional markers typically expressed on antigen-presenting cells, but not on circulating neutrophils, such as HLA-DR, CD14, CD206, CD86 and CCR7.

vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
We found differential NL (nuclear localized) and membrane-localized (MEM) patterns of Cyclin D1. 
The cyclin D1MEM was sufficient to induce G1–S cell-cycle progression, cellular proliferation, and colony formation
The cytoplasmic sequestration of cyclin D1 is important to maintain the non-proliferative state as nuclear enforced expression using a nuclear-localized form of cyclin D1 forces the cell into a proliferative state
Cyclin D1 has been identified in the cytoplasmic membrane and shown to bind and regulate the function of several cytoplasmic membrane-associated proteins including PACSIN II (Protein kinase C and Casein kinase Substrate In Neurons protein 2)23 also known as syndapin), Filamin A24 and paxillin21.
PACSIN (involved in vesicle trafficking and endocytosis)
Cytoplasmic-membrane associated cyclin D1 was observed in 5 of 6 IBC patient samples and 2 of 17 non-IBC patient samples had membrane-associated cyclin D1 (Fig. S3). All 6 IBC and 16 of 17 non-IBC patients had nuclear-localized cyclin D1
Cyclin D1 is known to promote cellular migration of fibroblasts and mammary epithelial cells
Cytoplasmic membrane-targeted cyclin D1 augments DNA synthesis and contact independent growth
Cytoplasmic membrane-targeted cyclin D1 augments estrogen-dependent Akt kinase activation via K112
Cyclin D1
-deficient fibroblasts show the same diameter size as wild-type cells, but attach and spread more rapidly after seeding on fibronectin-coated plates
26
,
28
Expression of Cyclin D1
MEM
, but not cyclin D1
NUC
, promoted cellular migratory velocity

Project Overview
Brief Description of Research Goals:
In this project, we want to establish a protocol for a new method that combines highly multiplexed imaging with proteomics analysis. For this we are using the picking function of the RarecyteFinder to isolate specific parts of the t-CycIF stained tissue. The cell samples are then sent to a collaboration partner for the proteomics analysis.
Moreover, an analysis pipeline for the analysis of the data that is collected during ProteoPicking experiments was created during this project. It analyses image data & proteomics data and in the integrates results from both parts of the project
Image Analysis:
- Illumination Correction
- Ashlar Stitching
- Segmentation (Stardist, Mesmer, Cellpose; different image filters)
- Quantification
- Phenotyping
- Compositonal Analysis
Proteomics Analysis:
- ProteinCount Comparisons
- Proteomicsdata exploration
- Differential Expression Analysis
Integration of Image and Proteomics Results

Challenges Encountered:
Some of the sectioned slides detached after the antigen retrieval step – and even after resectioning the problem remained
solution: choosing different sample blocks
There were no Ki67+ stroma cells
solution: we picked p21- cells as non-senescent stroma cells and p21+ cells as senescent stroma cells
Finding tumor core ROIs was sometimes difficult since some tumor parts were very small and therefore all the cells were close to stroma cells
solution: in these cases we chose the best that was possible, but maybe some of the tumor spots could be reevaluated as tumor-stroma-interface
During the picking process there were initially some problems, but we found a solution to pick with just one additional step - clicking on init or touch before clicking on pick

Challenges Encountered:
some channels do not overlap perfectly - problems with stitching? → no
segmentation with stardist did not work - 
sections are thicker than normal (10µm instead of 4µm), therefore there are a lot of clustered, overlapping cells that stardist was not able to mask
→ testing of other segmentation methods and image enhancing methods
Created mesmer segmentation code cannot segment full whole slide images - image needs to be tiled and tiles need to be segmented separately before the segmented tiles are stitched back together

other segmentation methods and image enhancing methods
Created mesmer segmentation code cannot segment full whole slide images - image needs to be tiled and tiles need to be segmented separately before the segmented tiles are stitched back together 
Created tiling code does not work flawlessly - segments tissue, but also segments background noise at the black edges of the images
→ instead of using the Tiling-Mesmer-Untiling code, we stick to the mesmer code without tiling that only segments smaller images and segment only smallcrops of the picks 
Phenotyping with SciMap was challenging - an error in the SciMap scaling function source code was found and a solution was found for this by changing parts of the source code

chromatogram ranges (in workspace processing)
🡪 in filter column of the chromatogram range table – use different voltages cv = 40 and cv = 60
peptides are usually double or triple charged, while impurities are usually single charged and spaced evenly
🡪 contamination comes mostly from voltage 40 in my sample
trypsin peak at 421.7 from preprocessing
small peaks next to the charge showing peak are the fragmentation pattern
- usually the most abundant ones overpower smaller peaks

AA should be regularly spaced with always the same difference (addition of one AA), while impurities can have differently spaced peaks

3.1.2 Patients & specimens

All tissue samples were obtained from the ONCOSYS-OVA prospective study collec-
tion or the Helsinki University Hospital. The ethics board of the Women’s Clinic,
Helsinki University Hospital (HUH), and Helsinki Biobank have granted approval for
the use of all samples and associated clinical data in this study. Patient identifiers
and personal information are securely managed within the HUH server, utilizing the
HUH Datalake infrastructure provided by Helsinki Biobank. Only authorized person-
nel have access to retrieve clinical data. To ensure patient confidentiality in publicly
available results, each individual is assigned a randomized publication ID, reducing
the risk of identification. All used samples for this project are derived from interval
debulking surgery after NACT from the omentum of HGSC patients. Table 3 contains
an overview of all used patient samples in this project.

3.2 Methods
For this Master’s thesis project two experiments, a pilot experiment and an optim-
ization experiment, were performed. Below, the experimental procedure of both ex-
periments and any differences between pilot and optimization are described. The
changes in the protocol were necessary since the analysis of the proteomics results of
the pilot experiment showed that the samples were contaminated with keratin and a
not identified type of polymer, which interfered to a certain degree with the analysis.
Figure 1 depicts the workflow of the method.


 18
Figure 1: Schematic overview of main steps of ProteoPicking.


3.2.1 Sample sectioning

Tissue samples were processed into formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) blocks
by the tissue preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Depart-
ment in Biomedicum, Universty of Helsinki. Of those FFPE blocks, tissue sections
were cut with a Microm HM 355 motorized rotary microtome. For the pilot ex-
periment, 8 µm thick sections were prepared, while the sections for the optimization
experiment were 10 µm thick. The sliced sections were transferred into a waterbath
set to 40°C to completely unfold, which takes approximately 1 min. Only then the
sections were scooped up with SuperFrost Plus Adhesion Microscope Slides, dried as

on
experiment were 10 µm thick. The sliced sections were transferred into a waterbath
set to 40°C to completely unfold, which takes approximately 1 min. Only then the
sections were scooped up with SuperFrost Plus Adhesion Microscope Slides, dried as
thoroughly as possible, placed onto a tray, baked at 56°C for 2 h, and dried overnight
at roomtemperature. Afterwards the samples were stored at 4°C until further usage.

During the optimization experiment special attention was paid on keeping everything
as dust free as possible. Gloves were used throughout the entire experiment. The
microtome, the tools for transferring the sections, work surface, and storage box of
the microscope slides were cleaned thoroughly beforehand with ethanol. The water
from the waterbath was replaced with water directly from the milliQ filtering system
and the waterbath was cleaned before the refilling. A new package of SuperFrost
microscope microscope slides was opened to prevent contamination through earlier
experiments and the slides were stored vertically in a plastic box with lid, which was
only removed during the baking step to allow the remaining water to evaporate from
the slides. In comparison, during the pilot experiments the slides were transported
and stored on wooden trays without proper cover.


 19
3.2.2 t-CycIF

The tCycIF protocol was adapted to be compatible with proteomic mass spectrometry
assays. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), the used buffer in the established standard
protocol, needed to be replaced. Instead tris-buffered saline (TBS) was used. Even
though, PBS and TBS differ in some aspects concerning antibody-based imaging,
their key roles are the same - maintaining the pH and osmolarity to preserve tissue
morphology and protein integrity (Visikol, 2024).

buffered saline (TBS) was used. Even
though, PBS and TBS differ in some aspects concerning antibody-based imaging,
their key roles are the same - maintaining the pH and osmolarity to preserve tissue
morphology and protein integrity (Visikol, 2024).

Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre-staining

First, slides were baked at 56°C for 30 min. Then, all slides were deparaffinized and
rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each followed by im-
mersing them for 3 min each in a series of ethanol solution with gradually decreasing
ethanol concentration (100%, 90%, 70%, 50%, and 30% in milliQ). Finally, the slides
were washed twice for 1 min in 1x TBS (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.6; 150 mM NaCl). In
a next step, the slides were submerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid buffer (10 mM
citric acid monohydrate, pH 6.0) and heated up to 95°C for 15 min in an Antigen
retrieval Chamber High Voltage. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid buffer were
taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room temperature and then they
were washed three times for 3 min each in 1x TBS. This is done to fully retrieve the
antigen molecules, which can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the form-
alin fixation. To minimize non-specific antibody binding of the tissue, 200-300 µL of
filtered Odyssey blocking buffer were pipetted onto the tissue samples, which were
then incubated at room temperature for 30 min in a moist Kartell Polystyrene Micro-
scope Slide Box incubation chamber to prevent evaporation and dehydration of the
tissue. Afterwards, the Odyssey blocking buffer was removed by vertically tapping the
slides on a tissue paper. The slides were then washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS.

Slide Box incubation chamber to prevent evaporation and dehydration of the
tissue. Afterwards, the Odyssey blocking buffer was removed by vertically tapping the
slides on a tissue paper. The slides were then washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS.

For the optimization experiment, close attention was again paid on keeping everything
as dust free as possible. Moreover, the use of plastic containers was avoided as much
as possible. Plastic slide holders and chambers were replaced with glass slide holders
and chambers, except for the antigen retrieval chamber that was only available in
a plastic version. The ethanol chain was freshly prepared, in contrast to the pilot
experiment where already prepared solutions were reused, and also fresh xylene was
used. Reagents and buffers were prepared in glass Schott flasks and the used water
was acquired directly from the milliQ filtering system.


 20
Antibody staining

Before the actual staining was performed antibody panels for both experiments were
designed that could be applied in three cycles (table ??) respectively two cycles
(table ??). The antibody panels used in this project had already been validated
and established in previous experiments, making further validation not necessary.
The antibodies were selected to detect tumor, stromal and immune cells as well as
different markers of the cell cycle.
For each cycle of staining, an antibody mastermix was prepared containing all an-
tibodies for one cycle and Hoechst diluted accordingly in filtered Odyssey blocking
buffer. 100 µL of the antibody mixture were pipetted per sample, with the slides being
incubated in a moist incubation chamber at 4°C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies were necessary

. 100 µL of the antibody mixture were pipetted per sample, with the slides being
incubated in a moist incubation chamber at 4°C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies were necessary
on the next day and the slides were directly washed three times in 1x TBS for 3 min
each. Afterwards, 80-100 µL of 70% glycerol in milliQ were pipetted onto the tissue
samples and the slides were covered with Epredia Menzel Microscope Coverslips for
the following imaging. After the imaging, the coverslip is removed again by placing
the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short period of time the coverslip detached
on its own and the coverless slide was either prepared for the next staining cycle
(fluorophore inactivation) or stored in 1x TBS at 4°C for further experiments.

For the pilot project, two of the three used tissue samples were only stained with
one cycle of antibodies (cycle 3), while the third tissue sample was stained with three
cycles. During the optimization all samples were stained with two cycles of antibodies.
For the coverslipping a new package of coverslips was opened to prevent contamina-
tion through earlier experiments.

Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching)

In between each staining cycle the slides must undergo fluorophore inactivation to
guarantee that no fluorescent signal from the previous cycle remains. For this, the
slides were placed in 50 mL freshly prepared bleaching solution (40 mL 1x TBS, 7.5 mL

 22
30% H2 O2 , 2.5 mL 480 mM NaOH). 50 mL are sufficient for 7 to 8 slides. The com-
pletely submerged slides were put between two LED lights for 90 min at room tem-
perature. During this time, the bleaching solution was replaced two times, once after
30 min and again after 60 min, after the first staining cycle and one time after 45 min
after every following staining cycle. Afterwards, the slides were washed four times in
1xTBS for 3 min each.

During this step of the protocol, for both experiments the slides were submerged in
bleaching solution in plastic dishes because there was no glass replacement that could
have been used for the optimizing experiment.

3.2.3 Image acqisition, processing, analysis and visualization

In figure 2 an overview of image acquisition (A) and image processing (B) is depicted.

Image acquisition

The images of the tissue samples were acquired using a CyteFinder II imaging instru-
ment. This is a high-throughput automated microscope platform for digital imaging
that enables multiplexed, high-resolution tissue imaging of the whole slide. It contains
a slide holder with space for two slides and multiple microscope lenses to scan the
slides at different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different channels up to
four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously. Additionally, in each cycle Hoechst is
scanned at 395 nm. Hoechst is used for nuclear staining, and it is especially important
to stain and image Hoechst in each cycle for the image processing afterwards. The
scanner is connected to a series of software to control and perform the imaging. The
RareCyte Instrument Controller helps to operate the microscope, the CyteHub is a
control center for scanning assays and image analysis, with the CyteFinder the scan
process can be set up and monitored, and the Imager allows real-time imaging and
picking control (see chapter picking).

The first step of the imaging process was placing the covered slides in the slide holder
upside down since the microscope lenses are inverted and positioned beneath the
slide holder. Then, a scanning assay needed to be created in CyteHub. In it all
necessary parameters for the scanning process were defined - slide type (“custom

e holder
upside down since the microscope lenses are inverted and positioned beneath the
slide holder. Then, a scanning assay needed to be created in CyteHub. In it all
necessary parameters for the scanning process were defined - slide type (“custom
ROI”), sample type (“tissue”), illumination type (“fluorescence”), imaging settings
such as magnification type (“10x” for slides from which after the imaging cells were
picked), binning (“1”, which merges pixels and reduces resolution), and scan overlap

channel the stitching of the images is based on afterwards (“BV421” which is the
channel of Hoechst), and scan channels, which assign the used antibodies and a
suitable exposure time to their respective channels (used exposure time see table
4 respectively table4). To test which exposure time is appropriate for each antibody
the Imager software was used beforehand.
After the assay was created, the slide scanning was started using CyteFinder. The
first time a slide was scanned a pre-scan needed to be done that identified the tissue
area on the slide. This allowed then to manually adjust the scanning area and select
focus points for the area to be scanned, which is necessary for a successful overlay of

 24
the various images from different cycles of one slide.

tified the tissue
area on the slide. This allowed then to manually adjust the scanning area and select
focus points for the area to be scanned, which is necessary for a successful overlay of

 24
the various images from different cycles of one slide. The chosen settings were stored
in CyteHub and were applied to every future scan of the slide.
The actual scanning process was performed automatically. For this, the area to be
scanned was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so called “tiles”, by the system. The
tiles had overlapping regions with their adjacent neighbour tiles to ensure a correct
stitching of the tiles during the image processing. The size and number of tiles de-
pended on the chosen magnification and are automatically calculated by the system.
The tiles were scanned one after another and only when all channels of one tile were
scanned, the scanning of the next tile started. After the whole selected area was
scanned the raw data was stored as a rcpnl file for further processing. Moreover, for
each scanning cycle an ome.tiff file was created from the CyteFinder II programme
that was used for the region of interest (ROI) selection (see Cell picking).

Image processing

The image processing mainly was conducted using parts of the already established
image processing workflow of the Färkkilä research group of the University of Hel-
sinki (Färkkiä lab, 2025). Only segmentation and phenotyping were performed using
Python scripts adapted from already existing code or completely self written. These
scripts are available on the Färkkilä Lab Proteopick GitHub (Färkkilä lab, 2025).
The workflow for processing t-CycIF images is depicted in figure ??.

Illumination of the rcpnl files generated by the CyteFinder programme is corrected

ten. These
scripts are available on the Färkkilä Lab Proteopick GitHub (Färkkilä lab, 2025).
The workflow for processing t-CycIF images is depicted in figure ??.

Illumination of the rcpnl files generated by the CyteFinder programme is corrected
using BaSiC ( Background and Shading Correction)(Peng et al., 2017). BaSiC per-
forms flat-field estimation by modeling illumination bias, non-uniform brightness from
the light source, and shading bias, edge darkening due to vignetting or optical ab-
errations, ensuring uniform image illumination. Uneven illumination, often causing
darker tile edges, can interfere with quantitative analysis and disrupt further pro-
cessing. The correction eliminates these variations, ensuring consistent fluorescence
intensity measurements and reliable downstream analysis.
To assemble all tiles of all channels and images of one slide, the open-source Python
package ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency
Registration)(Muhlich et al., 2022) was used to process the illumination corrected
rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps, stitching and registration, and
is based on the nuclear staining of the Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects
with high contrast, well defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching
step adjacent tiles of each imaging cycle were aligned, while during the registration


 25
tiles from different imaging cycles that cover the same region were aligned. Running
the ASHLAR programme results in the generation of an ome.tiff file, a multichannel
pyramidal stacked image consisting of multiple layers with different resolutions. This
format quickens visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only load-

AR programme results in the generation of an ome.tiff file, a multichannel
pyramidal stacked image consisting of multiple layers with different resolutions. This
format quickens visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only load-
ing a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each moment.

After the segmentation of the images, a python script was prepared that cut out small
crops that contained the selected ROIs of the images. All further processing steps
were performed using those small crops instead of the whole slide images.

The segmentation was performed using the nuclear segmentation model of Mesmer
(Mesoscopic Segmentation of Cellular and Nuclear Regions)(Greenwald et al., 2022).
Mesmer is a suite of pre-trained deep learning models designed for high-quality bio-
logical image analysis. It uses a Convolutional Neural Network to generate per-pixel
probability values for nuclear regions, cell boundaries, and background. A distance
transform and post-processing algorithm, similar to the watershed method, refine
these probability maps to accurately segment individual nuclei, even in densely packed
regions. Prior to segmentation, gamma correction and unsharp masking, two contrast
and sharpness enhancement methods, were applied to enhance the visibility of nuclei
by increasing background intensity and sharpening cell edges. These adjustments
improved the performance of Mesmer in segmenting the images. Segmentation was
performed based on the Hoechst channels. The used parameters for gamma correc-
tion and unsharp masking (γ = 1.5, σ = 1.0, strength = 2.5) were chosen based
on empirical testing. Outcome are cell masks for every image saved as ome.tif files.
The used Python script integrates code adapted from the Van Valen Lab’s DeepCell

rrec-
tion and unsharp masking (γ = 1.5, σ = 1.0, strength = 2.5) were chosen based
on empirical testing. Outcome are cell masks for every image saved as ome.tif files.
The used Python script integrates code adapted from the Van Valen Lab’s DeepCell
Applications GitHub (Van Valen Lab, 2025).

Single-cell information was extracted by quantifying the data using the scikit-image
Python package (Van der Walt et al., 2014) on the generated masks, producing a
data frame with multiple parameters for spatial analysis. This data frame includes
key features such as CellID, x and y coordinates, eccentricity, and median marker
intensity values for each object across all channels.

Phenotyping

The segmented cells were phenotyped using Scimap, a scalable toolkit for analyzing
spatial molecular data. The process operates on the single-cell data frame generated


 26
in the prior step, incorporating X and Y coordinates along with marker intensity val-
ues (Nirmal & Sorger, 2024). Gating was applied as a key step in the workflow, where
thresholds were set to categorize cells based on marker expression. A logic map, as
seen in table 6, was created to systematically specify these categories as phenotypic
classifications. All in all, this resulted in a classification of cells into specific phen-
otypes based on marker expression levels. The phenotype data was added to the
single-cell data frame.

While the images of the pilot experiment were only stitched for visualizing the tissue
samples, the generated images of the optimization experiment were processed follow-
ing every step of the described workflow.

Image analysis

The images were analysed using the single-cell data frame that was generated during
the image processing. A Python script was created that enabled accessing the data,
specifying cells from selected image areas (ROIs), and performing quantitative eval-
uations (Färkkilä lab, 2025).

Image visualization

The open-source multi-dimensional image viewer Napari (napari contributors, 2019)
was used for visualization throughout the image processing. It facilitated the visual-
ization and combination of different channels while allowing adjustments to contrast,


 27
intensity, opacity, and color to enhance image interpretation. Additionally, Napari
enabled the overlay of segmentation masks onto images, providing a clear depiction
of cell boundaries and aiding in quality control of the cell segmentation results. Its
interactive capabilities were particularly useful for visually inspecting and validating
each processing step, the phenotying results, and any analysis outputs ensuring the
accuracy.

3.2.4 Cell picking

ROIs selection

For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the ome.tiff files of the individual cycles were
opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. The desired cells were
identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter which corresponds to the
diameter of the microneedle that was used for the picking. The selected ROIs were
stored in CyteHub as a rcpickmap file.

Pricking preparations
The necessary needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL
PCR tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of the
cells from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the
collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells picked they either contained
20 µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 6 M Urea, pH 7.5-8, protease and phosphatase
inhibitor tablets) or 20 µL TFE lysis buffer (50% (v/v) 2,2,2-trifuoroethanol, 300 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, protease and phosphatase inhibitor tablets). When everything was
prepared, the retrieval module was placed into the right slide holder position in the
machine.
Next, the Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle was per-
formed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual position (x, y) of the
needle with the position displayed in the Imager and by assuring that the tip of the
needle actually touches the slide (z) during the further picking process. For the cal-
ibration, an empty slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder
position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a needle was
selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing solution. Afterwards the

bration, an empty slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder
position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a needle was
selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing solution. Afterwards the
needle was positioned above the slide for either an automatic calibration or a manual
one, where the parameters of the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were
adjusted.
In a last step, the created rcpickmap file that withholds the selected ROIs was im-

 28
ported into the Imager software with the Imager Main Control together with the
metadata file of the slide.

Picking & collection

In a first step, the picking parameters were set, needle type (40 µm diameter needles
were used), wet and draw volume (150 nL), stage wiggles (2), wiggle radius (15 µm),
and overdrive (5 µm). Then the slide the cells were to be picked from was put into
the left slide holder position facing upwards and its image was focused using the
Hoechst channel. Subsequently, the picking process was performed. It comprised
of a series of steps, see figure 3. The needle was flushed two to three times in the
rinsing solution, then it was filled with retrieval solution before the needle was moved
to the previously selected ROI. There the cells were checked again for the respective
biomarkers with the various channels ant then the cells were picked. After the picking,
the cells were deposited in the according collection tube. As soon as the cells were
collected the next cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide were
picked. Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the rinsing,
retrieval, and waste solution together with the collection tubes were exchanged or the

ext cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide were
picked. Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the rinsing,
retrieval, and waste solution together with the collection tubes were exchanged or the
picking process was finished by rinsing the needle three times in milliQ water and
then dropping it into the needle discard tray. The tissue that was left on the slide
the picking was performed on was scraped with a scalpel into a tube containing 50 µL
of TFE buffer (pilot experiment) or Urea buffer (optimization experiment). Finally,
all picked cell samples as well as the remaining tissue samples were snap frozen with
liquid nitrogen and stored in a –80°C freezer for further experiments.


Figure 3: Visualization of the four picking steps - positioning of the needle, lowering the
needle to dissect the tissue, detaching the tissue via wiggling, and collecting the tissue.


3.2.5 Proteomics analysis

Mass spectrometry analysis


 29
The mass spectrometry analysis of the picked cell samples was performed by the Turku
Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre, University of Turku. The
samples were analysed using a bottom-up approach, following the facility’s established
protocols. The used method was data independent acquisition shot gun proteomics
via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS).

Sample sectioning
Tissue samples were processed into paraffin blocks by the tissue preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Department in Biomedicum. Of those formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks 10 µm thick tissue sections were sliced with a Microm HM 355 rotary microtome, placed on SuperFrost micros 10cope glass slides and dried by first baking them in the oven at 56°C for 2 h for and then leaving them at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards they were stored at 4°C until further usage.

After the whole selected area was scanned the raw data was stored as a .rcpnl file for further processing.
Image processing
To assemble all tiles of all channels and images of one slide, the open-source Python package ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency Registration) was used to process the generated .rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps, stitching and registration, and is based on the nuclear staining of the Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects with high contrast, well defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching step adjacent tiles of each imaging cycle were aligned, while during the registration tiles from different imaging cycles that cover the same region were aligned. Running the ASHLAR programme results in the generation of an ome.tiff file, a multichannel pyramidal stacked image consisting of multiple 
layers with different resolutions. This format quickens visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only loading a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each moment.
Image visualization
With Napari.
Cell picking from the scanned slides
ROIs selection

This format quickens visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only loading a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each moment.
Image visualization
With Napari.
Cell picking from the scanned slides
ROIs selection
From the antibody-stained slides three types of cells were picked, tumor cells in M-phase, tumor cells in S-phase, and stromal cells. The identification of corresponding cells was based on the fluorescence intensity of different biomarkers. Tumor cells were recognized by PanCK and PAX8
. 
Stroma cells were identified by the biomarker Vimentin. Tumor cells that expressed pH3 were chosen for the M-phase cells, while cell cycle biomarker pRb marked the S-phase cells.
For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the imaging results (ome.tiff file) were opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. By turning on the channels of the respective biomarkers the desired cells were identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter which corresponds to the diameter of the microneedle that was used for the picking. The selected ROIs were stored in CyteHub as a rcpickmap file.
Picking preparation
First, the needle holder was prepared. Next to space for six needles it can hold six tubes and has a built-in needle discard tray. The necessary needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL PCR tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of the cells from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells picked they either contained 20 µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris, 6 M Urea, protease and phosphatase inhibitors, pH 7.5-8) or TFE lysis buffer ().

ste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells picked they either contained 20 µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris, 6 M Urea, protease and phosphatase inhibitors, pH 7.5-8) or TFE lysis buffer (). When everything was ready, the needle holder was placed into the right slide holder position in the machine.
Next, the Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle was performed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual position (x, y) of the needle with the position displayed in the Imager and by assuring that the tip of the needle actually touches the slide (z) during the further picking process. For the calibration, an empty slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a needle was selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing solution. Afterwards the needle 
was positioned above the slide for either an automatic calibration or a manual one, where the parameters of the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were adjusted.
In a last step, the created .rcpickmap file that withholds the selected ROIs was imported into the Imager software with the Imager Main Control together with the .metadata file of the slide.
Picking & collection
In a first step, the picking parameters were set, like needle type (40 µm diameter needles were used), wet and draw volume (100 nL), stage wiggles (2), and wiggle radius (15 µm). Then the slide the cells were to be picked from was put into the left slide holder position facing upwards and its image was focused using the Hoechst channel. Subsequently, the picking process was performed. It comprised of a series of steps.

5 µm). Then the slide the cells were to be picked from was put into the left slide holder position facing upwards and its image was focused using the Hoechst channel. Subsequently, the picking process was performed. It comprised of a series of steps. The needle was flushed two to three times in the rinsing solution, then it was filled with retrieval solution before the needle was moved to the previously selected ROIs. There the cells were checked again for the respective biomarkers with the various channels prior to getting picked. After the picking, the cells were deposited in the according collection tube. As soon as the cells were collected the next cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide were picked.
Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the rinsing, retrieval, and waste solution together with the collection tubes were exchanged or the picking process was finished by rinsing the needle three times in milliQ water and then dropping it into the needle discard tray.
The tissue that was left on the slide the picking was performed on was scraped with a scalpel into a tube containing 50 µL of TFE.
Finally, all picked cell samples as well as the remaining tissue sample were snap frozen with liquid nitrogen and stored in a –80°C freezer for further experiments.

Background: 
Here’s a categorized list of genes for Cell Cycle Dysregulation, Checkpoint Dysfunction, and Proliferation Markers:
1. Cell Cycle Dysregulation
These genes are key regulators of the G1/S and G2/M transitions, often altered in high-grade serous ovarian cancer.
TP53: Frequently mutated; regulates cell cycle arrest.
BRCA1/BRCA2: Implicated in homologous recombination (HR); loss affects G2/M progression.
CCNE1: Amplification disrupts G1/S transition control.
RB1: Loss of function removes G1/S checkpoint control.
CDKN2A/CDKN2B: Loss or mutation results in unchecked CDK4/6 activity.
MYC: Amplified; drives G1/S progression.
FOXM1: Overexpressed transcription factor critical for cell cycle regulation.
2. Checkpoint Dysfunction
These genes are crucial for DNA damage response and maintaining proper cell cycle checkpoints.
ATM/ATR: DNA damage sensors; mutations impair G1/S and G2/M checkpoint activation.
CHK1/CHK2: Mediators of checkpoint signaling; mutations lead to cell cycle checkpoint bypass.
BUB1/BUB1B: Spindle assembly checkpoint regulators; mutations result in chromosomal instability.
AURKA/AURKB: Regulate mitotic spindle formation and chromosomal segregation.

AURKA/AURKB: Regulate mitotic spindle formation and chromosomal segregation.
PLK1: Involved in spindle assembly and mitotic progression.
RAD51: Key for DNA repair; dysfunction leads to checkpoint failure.
3. Proliferation Markers
These genes are often overexpressed, driving unchecked tumor growth and aggressive proliferation.
FOXM1: Master regulator of mitotic genes and cell cycle-related processes.
PLK1: Promotes centrosome maturation and spindle formation; overexpression supports mitosis.
TPX2: Enhances spindle assembly; often amplified.
MYC: Potent driver of proliferative signaling pathways

GSEA Hallmarks
The GSEA Hallmarks dataset analysis revealed several key findings:
DNA Repair
: Upregulated, indicating a heightened need for DNA repair mechanisms to support active progression through the S phase. This is essential for cells to replicate DNA efficiently during cell cycle progression.
MYC Targets V1 & V2
: Both pathways are upregulated, driving active progression from G1 to S phase. These pathways are associated with increased glucose uptake, altered oxidative phosphorylation, and the promotion of cancer stem-like properties.
MTORC1 Signaling
: Promotes cell growth and proliferation by regulating cell cycle progression. Additionally, it influences glucose metabolism and immune suppression.
E2F Targets
: These targets are active when phosphorylated Rb protein fails to inhibit E2F, further driving the transition from G1 to S phase.
G2M Checkpoint
: Indicates activation of processes facilitating the transition into the G2 phase, supporting cell cycle progression.
Overall, the analysis suggests active cell cycle progression, particularly the transition from G1 to S phase, driven by various molecular pathways involved in growth, metabolism, and DNA repair.

ilar things.

 via break-induced replication is activated when replication forks collapse during DNA replication, facilitating repair mechanisms to maintain genomic integrity.

 and 
kinetochore organization
 suggest active preparation for mitosis, ensuring proper chromosome alignment and segregation.
K6-linked ubiquitination
 regulates BRCA1/2-mediated DNA repair, specifically in double-stranded break (DSB) repair through homologous recombination, critical for maintaining genome stability during cell division.

Expansion CyCIF 
Experiment planning:
OncosysOVA IDS samples were taken. If the samples were used in GeoMX or Xenium experiments, they were automatically included in the expansion CyCIF. To homogenize the cohort, omental samples were chosen for the rest of the cohort. Then, omental samples were checked for quality through HE stainings. If the tumor purity on HE staining was more than 10% and no hemorrhage or tumor necrosis was observed, the tissue was added to the experiment. 
A total of 72 samples were included in the expanded CyCIF analysis. Of these, 27 were previously used in the discovery CyCIF.
Tissues were sourced as follows: 47 from GeoMx’s prior experiments, 8 from recent Xenium cuts, and 17 freshly cut prior to the experiment.
Regarding homologous recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.
The main objective of expanding tCyCIF is to enable deep phenotyping and mapping of cancer cell cycle states across a larger cohort.

us recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.
The main objective of expanding tCyCIF is to enable deep phenotyping and mapping of cancer cell cycle states across a larger cohort. By increasing the patient sample size, we aim to gain a better understanding of clinical associations and enhance phenotyping of cell cycle states using an improved functional panel. With the extended cohort, we will also be able to integrate multiple data modalities, including spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA sequencing, and genomics.
