1. Experimental Timeline
Monday, May 25:
 Procurement of supplies from FIMM, inventory verification, and finalization of logistical coordination.
Wednesday, May 27:
 Preparation of slides with the UV and formulation of required reagents, protocol finalizing, start preparation of reagents for the experiment 
Thursday, May 28:
 Sectioning of Formalin-Fixed Paraffin-Embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks, preparation of reagents for the experiment continues
Friday, May 29:
 Tissue pre-processing, deparaffinization, and immunofluorescence staining.
Saturday, May 31:
 Fluorescence scanning and digital imaging of stained sections.
Monday, June 1:
 Preliminary systems test at the FIMM facility.
Tuesday, June 2:
 Initiation of the formal pilot LCM experiment at FIMM.

### word/document.xml
LCM-Proteomics Pipeline: Troubleshooting and DVP Replication
1. Root Cause Analysis: Low Protein Yield and Polymer Contamination
The previous experimental run experienced significant polymer contamination and suboptimal protein identification. When comparing the executed workflow against the Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) pipeline, several critical discrepancies emerge as the primary sources of these issues:
Plate Material and High-Heat Decrosslinking:
 Standard 4titude 96-well PCR plates were utilized. These plates are manufactured with plasticizers and mold-release agents. Subjecting these plates to high-heat FFPE thermal decrosslinking in the presence of urea aggressively leached these polymers into the microscopic sample volume, causing massive mass spectrometer interference. Furthermore, standard polypropylene intrinsically binds proteins, leading to heavy sample loss when dealing with low-abundance inputs.
Slide Membrane Chemistry:
 The use of PET (Polyethylene terephthalate) membranes rather than PEN (Polyethylene naphthalate) or PPS (Polyphenylene sulfide) contributed to the background. 
Buffer Selection:
 Collecting directly into 20 μL of high-concentration urea is chemically aggressive. The DVP pipeline utilizes Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC), which is a volatile, MS-compatible buffer that evaporates cleanly during vacuum drying without stripping plastics or leaving residual salts.
Commercial Blocking Buffers:
 The application of proprietary blockers (like Odyssey) during staining introduces exogenous proteins (e.g., casein, BSA) and polymers that can mask the delicate endogenous peptide signals of the tissue.
In Lina’s experiments - PCR tubes were utilized, FG-021 (Nippon Genetics)
2. Protocol Comparison: Executed Experiment vs. DVP Standard

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ous proteins (e.g., casein, BSA) and polymers that can mask the delicate endogenous peptide signals of the tissue.
In Lina’s experiments - PCR tubes were utilized, FG-021 (Nippon Genetics)
2. Protocol Comparison: Executed Experiment vs. DVP Standard
A. Refined Summary of the Previously Performed Experiment (Helsinki Lab)
Samples were sectioned onto metal-frame PET membrane slides (pretreated with UV-light) and baked at 60°C. Staining was performed using a custom fluorescence panel and a commercial Odyssey blocking buffer. To minimize tissue detachment risks, aggressive pressure-cooker antigen retrieval was bypassed. Laser capture microdissection (LCM) was conducted at FIMM utilizing fluorescence guidance to bypass lengthy Whole Slide Imaging (WSI). Dissected tissue elements were catapulted directly into a standard 4titude 96-well PCR plate pre-filled with 20 μL of urea buffer. Following collection, the plate was snap-frozen, shipped for mass spectrometry, and subjected to FFPE thermal decrosslinking, resulting in extensive polymer leaching.
B. Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) Pipeline (Lengyel Lab)
FFPE tissues are sectioned onto 1.0 PEN membrane slides pre-treated with UV-light and VECTABOND reagent to ensure tissue adhesion during harsh treatments. Following deparaffinization and rehydration, samples undergo heat-induced epitope retrieval and are stained avoiding complex protein blockers. High-resolution imaging and machine-learning-driven segmentation define the cutting contours. The LCM catapults designated cell populations into 
dry
, empty wells of an MS-grade 384-well plate (e.g., Eppendorf Protein LoBind). In-well lysis and thermal decrosslinking are executed simultaneously using an Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC) buffer at 95°C.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.docx
vector_chunks.text:
LCM catapults designated cell populations into 
dry
, empty wells of an MS-grade 384-well plate (e.g., Eppendorf Protein LoBind). In-well lysis and thermal decrosslinking are executed simultaneously using an Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC) buffer at 95°C. Digestion follows using a mixture of LysC and Trypsin in an ABC/Acetonitrile buffer. Finally, peptides are vacuum-dried, resuspended, and injected directly into the LC-MS via an autosampler.
3. Checklist for Future DVP-Aligned Experiments
Category
Action Required
Rationale for Lengyel Lab Pipeline Alignment
Consumables
Procure Eppendorf 384-well Protein LoBind plates.
Eliminates leachable plasticizers; prevents proteins from sticking to well walls, maximizing yield.
Slides
Switch to 1.0 PEN membrane slides (VECTABOND treated) or PPS.
PEN/PPS are standard for DVP and do not generate the MS-interfering polymer vapor associated with PET.
Buffer Strategy
Replace Urea collection buffer with Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC).
ABC is volatile, MS-compatible, and allows for safe high-heat decrosslinking without degrading consumables.
Collection State
Collect tissue into DRY wells, not liquid-filled wells.
Ensures tissue drops directly to the bottom without splashing or adhering to the upper walls of the well.
Staining
Eliminate commercial protein blocking buffers (e.g., Odyssey).
Prevents exogenous proteins from dominating 
the
 mass spectra and suppressing low-abundance targets.
Decrosslinking
Perform FFPE decrosslinking at 95°C for 60 mins in ABC.
Crucial for FFPE tissues; breaks formalin crosslinks to allow enzymatic access to proteins.
4. Standard Operating Procedure: Replicating the DVP Pipeline
Slide Preparation:
 Treat 1.0 PEN membrane slides with VECTABOND. Mount FFPE sections (5-7 μm) and bake to ensure adherence.

vector_chunks.text:
aks formalin crosslinks to allow enzymatic access to proteins.
4. Standard Operating Procedure: Replicating the DVP Pipeline
Slide Preparation:
 Treat 1.0 PEN membrane slides with VECTABOND. Mount FFPE sections (5-7 μm) and bake to ensure adherence.
Deparaffinization & Staining:
 Deparaffinize using xylene and rehydrate through graded ethanol. Perform necessary antigen retrieval and immunofluorescence staining. Strictly avoid complex, undefined commercial blocking agents.
Laser Capture Microdissection (LCM):
 Target the defined regions of interest (e.g., HGSC subsister cells). Dissect and catapult the tissue directly into the dry wells of an Eppendorf 384-well Protein LoBind plate.
In-Well Lysis and Decrosslinking:
 Centrifuge the plate briefly to ensure all tissue is at the bottom. Add the MS-compatible lysis buffer (e.g., 20 μL of Ammonium Bicarbonate). Seal the plate tightly and heat at 95°C for 60 minutes to decrosslink the FFPE matrix.
Enzymatic Digestion:
 Cool the plate. Add the secondary digestion buffer (e.g., Acetonitrile in ABC) and heat briefly at 75°C. Introduce LysC and Trypsin for overnight digestion at 37°C.
Quench and Dry:
 Halt the digestion by adding 1% TFA. Vacuum dry the plate completely to remove all volatile buffers.
Mass Spec Preparation:
 The dry peptides can be safely stored or immediately resuspended in LC-MS loading buffer (e.g., 2% Acetonitrile, 0.1% TFA) for direct autosampler injection.
Phase 1: Sample Preparation & Collection (Helsinki Lab)
Slide Prep:
 FFPE tissue samples were sectioned onto 
PET
 (Polyethylene terephthalate) metal-frame slides and baked at 60°C.
Staining:
 The slides were deparaffinized, rehydrated, and stained using a custom fluorescence panel along with the commercial 
Odyssey blocking buffer
.

vector_chunks.text:
les were sectioned onto 
PET
 (Polyethylene terephthalate) metal-frame slides and baked at 60°C.
Staining:
 The slides were deparaffinized, rehydrated, and stained using a custom fluorescence panel along with the commercial 
Odyssey blocking buffer
. High-heat antigen retrieval was skipped to prevent tissue detachment.
Dissection:
 Using fluorescence-guided LCM at FIMM, specific tissue regions were catapulted directly into a 
standard 4titude 96-well PCR plate
.
Collection Medium:
 The wells of the 4titude plate were pre-filled with 
20 µL of high-concentration urea buffer
.
Shipping:
 The 96-well plate was snap-frozen and shipped via UPS to Otto for proteomics processing.
Phase 2: Mass Spectrometry Processing (Otto’s Lab)
Thermal Decrosslinking (The primary failure point):
 Because the samples were FFPE, they required high heat to break the formalin crosslinks. This high heat was applied to the samples 
while they were still in the 4titude standard PCR plates
 in the presence of the hot urea buffer. This aggressive combination chemically leached the manufacturing plasticizers directly out of the plate walls.
"One-Tip" Protocol Application:
 Otto then applied the streamlined "One-Tip" methodology to the samples. This likely involved adding a master mix containing TEAB buffer, MS-compatible detergents (like DDM), and the enzymes Trypsin and Lys-C to simultaneously lyse and digest the proteins.
Data Acquisition:
 The processed peptide mixtures were injected into the LC-MS system for reading.
Phase 3: The Results (Current Status)
Severe Polymer Contamination:
 The mass spectrometer readings were completely overwhelmed by polymer interference.

vector_chunks.text:
ata Acquisition:
 The processed peptide mixtures were injected into the LC-MS system for reading.
Phase 3: The Results (Current Status)
Severe Polymer Contamination:
 The mass spectrometer readings were completely overwhelmed by polymer interference. This background noise originated from two sources: the vaporized PET membrane from the LCM cutting, and the leached plasticizers from the 4titude plates reacting with the hot urea.
Low Protein Identification:
 Very few endogenous tissue proteins were identified. This is due to a combination of factors:
The massive polymer peaks masking the delicate peptide signals.
Sample loss from proteins irreversibly sticking to the standard polypropylene walls of the 4titude plate.
Potential masking from the exogenous proteins present in the Odyssey blocking buffer.

### word/document.xml
Student:
 MSc Nika Mikhailava
Principal Investigator:
 Professor Anniina Färkkilä 
Project Title:
Single-cell spatial proteomic profiling of subsister cell phenotypes in HGSC
Introduction & Preliminary Findings
First-line therapies in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) often fail to completely eradicate the tumor, leaving a reservoir of cells that survive chemotherapy - termed "
subsister cells
". We hypothesize that these spatially distinct and phenotypically heterogeneous cell populations drive divergent clinical outcomes, including tumor relapse.
To investigate this, we performed highly multiplexed 
t-CycIF (40 markers, n=27)
 and 
Xenium spatial transcriptomics (430 genes, n=16)
 on Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS) samples. Our preliminary spatial analysis resolved these subsister cells into phenotypically distinct and spatially segregated niches, identifying Highly Proliferative, CCND1-dominant, and Heterogeneous clinically relevant patient archetypes. Crucially, the
 enrichment of the
 Highly Proliferative niches (Ki67+/pRb+/pH3+)
 significantly correlated with shorter Progression-Free Intervals (PFI)
. This finding suggests that these specific spatial niches actively drive tumor relapse.
Motivation for Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)
While our targeted approaches (t-CycIF, Xenium) successfully mapped the architecture of these niches, they are limited by predefined antibody and gene panels. To discover actionable therapeutic vulnerabilities, we need an unbiased characterization of their full proteomic landscape. 
Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)
 perfectly bridges this gap, enabling the precise excision of these specific subsister niches to uncover their proteome in a spatially preserved context.
Study Cohort

characterization of their full proteomic landscape. 
Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)
 perfectly bridges this gap, enabling the precise excision of these specific subsister niches to uncover their proteome in a spatially preserved context.
Study Cohort
DVP will be executed on FFPE tumor samples from 
8-16 IDS patients
. To evaluate the epithelial tumor compartments, we will select 3–6 regions of interest (ROIs) per tissue section, laser-microdissecting approximately 200 tumor cells per ROI.
These samples were specifically chosen because they constitute our "integrated multi-omics" cohort. By utilizing serial sections from these exact patients, we can anchor the new DVP data directly to our existing profiles (matched t-CycIF, Xenium, scRNA-seq, and WES). If resources permit, spatial proteomic profiling will be expanded to additional samples from our broader t-CycIF cohort 
(up to 69 IDS samples)
.
Internship Aims & Tasks
Main Aim:
To explore the spatial proteomic landscape of clinically relevant subsister niches.
Pre-arrival Phase (Helsinki):
Conduct pilot laser capture microdissection (LCM) to validate protocols and assess infrastructure readiness.
Explore clinical associations of subsister phenotypes with bulk RNA-seq data.
Internship Phase (Chicago):
Acquire hands-on technical proficiency in the complete DVP methodology.
Perform image-guided LCM to isolate predefined subsister niches from a scalable cohort ranging from 8 (multi-omics cohort) to 69 (t-CycIF cohort) patient samples.
Conduct high-sensitivity mass spectrometry to comprehensively profile the proteome of these isolated regions and define targetable vulnerabilities.

### word/document.xml
Goal
: Spatial profiling of therapeutically targetable, cell cycle-related molecules in Post-NACT HGSC HRP patients 
Strengths: 
Cell cycle-related spatial biology has never been studied within HGSC
Many vulnerabilities (CCNE amp, DDR) are key targets that could potentially succeed in the treatment
Most of the usable markers are nuclear stainings, which eases the process
Cell fate is found within the intersection of many other processes that can be also studied in the same cohort (metabolism, DNA damage etc)
Potential risks of treatments of interest: 
1) off-target inhibitory effect on close family members of target protein
 2) acquired drug resistance due to mutants on target or upstream regulators
 3) many of these drugs are still under development
Technical considerations
(DNA vs S phase marker, dimensionality reduction with the cyclins?):
System to classify arrested/cycling/proliferating cells 
Classification of cell cycle stages
Profile immune activation/inactivation/escape/exclusion
Non-canonical patterns need to be adressed - Ilastik?
We are working on 2D slides (cannot quantify full cell content/morphology)
Elements of interest:
Basic TME phenotyping + immune checkpoints
Cyclins/cdk’s:
CDK4/6 Inhibitors
 - I believe are promising in combination with IO treatments
Palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib
4619625
156163
Rb intact tumors are more sensitive
mTOR pathway can cause resistance
presence/accumulation of cyclin E and CDK2 are adaptations that cause resistance (BC)
In PALOMA trials they concluded that it:
Causes cell cycle arrest
Decreases CD4+ FOXP3+ T reg/CD8+ T cell ratio
Increases IFN III levels and T cell activation
Increases PD-L1 and impairs DDR through HR
CCND1-related treatments
GSK3b is crucial for its degradation
Metformin

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx
vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
HGSC is often diagnosed at advanced stages due to the lack of effective screening strategies, together with unspecific symptomatology. First-line chemotherapy regimens have evolved over the past three decades. Single-agent melphalan chemotherapy was initially replaced, in the 1970s, by the combination of 
cyclophosphamide with the anthracycline doxorubicin
 and, in the 1980s, was replaced by concurrent 
cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin and cisplatin
, or 
cyclophosphamide and cisplatin only
. 
Carboplatin 
was also introduced in the 1980s and was found to have comparable efficacy to cisplatin, but with significantly less toxicity. In the early 1990s, 
paclitaxel 
was shown to be the most effective agent in patients with relapsed platinum-refractory disease, and this established its role in the treatment of ovarian cancer. Since then, a number of trials have evaluated whether paclitaxel can contribute to platinum-based first-line chemotherapy. (
https://www.nature.com/articles/nrc1123
)
CYCLOPHOSPHAMIDE 
Mechanism of action
: 
It is a pro-drug that gets activated by cytochrome P450 (CYP) 2B6 enzyme
It is a potent alkylating agent, causes intra and inter cross-link breaks
Use: 
Currently this drug is only utilized in relapsed onsets or as palliative care. 
(
https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/JCO.2024.42.16_suppl.5517
) 
PLATINUM-BASED CHEMOTHERAPIES
Cisplatin 
was the first platinum-based chemotherapy to be used in ovarian cancer. 
4667250
251413
6000750
316088
Structure
It has four “molecular limbs” surrounding a Pt atom
Mechanism of action: Inter and intra-cross-strand links
-409574
361950
Two of those are Cl- and the other two are NH3
One chloride ion is displaced by water to give the aquo complex, in a process termed aquation

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx
vector_chunks.text:
olecular limbs” surrounding a Pt atom
Mechanism of action: Inter and intra-cross-strand links
-409574
361950
Two of those are Cl- and the other two are NH3
One chloride ion is displaced by water to give the aquo complex, in a process termed aquation
The water molecule gets displaced by the N-heterocyclic bases on DNA. Guanine preferentially binds
4748213
120062
Carboplatin 
acts similarly to cisplatin but with less side-effects (no nephrotoxicity)
It has a bidentate dicarboxylate (CBDCA) ligand in place of the two chloride ligands
In the 1980s Carboplatin tested to be effective in ovarian cancer treatment 
TAXANES: PACLITAXEL
Paclitaxel targets β-tubulin
. Paclitaxel-treated cells have defects in mitotic spindle assembly, chromosome segregation, and cell division. Chromosomes are unable to achieve a metaphase spindle configuration. Prolonged activation of the mitotic checkpoint triggers apoptosis or reversion to the G0-phase of the cell cycle without cell division. In addition, taxane induces cancer cell death by activating the cleavage of procaspases and proapoptotic markers.
It limits the development of cancer cells by causing a stop in the cell cycle phases, specifically at the G2/M phase
Treatment combination:
1996: A phase III clinical trial led by Dr. Robert J. C. T. at Gynecologic Oncology Group (GOG) tested the combination of carboplatin + paclitaxel in women with advanced ovarian cancer. The trial compared this combination to older regimens that used cyclophosphamide and cisplatin.
Results
: The trial demonstrated a significant improvement in survival rates for patients receiving the carboplatin-paclitaxel combination. 
Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were notably better
 compared to the older regimens.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.docx
vector_chunks.text:
esults
: The trial demonstrated a significant improvement in survival rates for patients receiving the carboplatin-paclitaxel combination. 
Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were notably better
 compared to the older regimens. It showed a higher response rate (i.e., the percentage of patients whose tumors shrank or disappeared).
This trial was a game changer. It not only confirmed the efficacy of the combination but also established carboplatin and paclitaxel as the standard of care for first-line treatment of ovarian cancer, including HGSC.
By the early 2000s, clinical practice guidelines from major cancer organizations such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) and the European Society of Gynaecological Oncology (ESGO) endorsed carboplatin and paclitaxel as the standard first-line treatment for ovarian cancer, particularly for advanced stages
Over time, while 
carboplatin-paclitaxel
 remains the backbone of treatment, 
maintenance therapy
 with newer agents like 
Bevacizumab
 (anti-angiogenesis) and 
PARP inhibitors
 (for patients with BRCA mutations) have been added to help extend remission and improve survival.
Chemotherapy resistance:
Resistance can into primary and secondary: 
primary is caused by tumor cells before therapy exposure
secondary is tumor adaptation to the treatment, eg high expression of target proteins
Breast, ovarian, gastric, and uterine cancers become chemo-resistant after being treated with chemotherapy for a long time, especially paclitaxel.

vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
What is cell cycle? 
Cell cycle is the process through which a cell is able to effectively divide 
Cell cycle and antitumor immunity
Senescence
Multiple events in the microenvironment, as DNA damage or deprivation of nutrients can induce senesgence. Senesence is a form of arrest in with not only the cell division is shut down, but also triggers the SASP (senescence-associated secretory phenotype), linked to secretion of multiple characteristic proteins (such as chemokines, cytokines or proteases). Depending on the biological context, senescence could limit or support the tumor progression: 
Within cancer cells
SASP may facilitate the proliferative signalling of the surrounding cancer cells
Senescence may be just a transitory state, possibly associated with therapy resistance. Since the treatments often target proliferating cells, this form of dormancy might confer protective effects to tumor cells. 
CAFs (Cancer associated fibroblasts) can become senescent 
How is senescence traced? 
Traits associated in ovca with cancer cell senescence (with doxo treatment) are:
expression of senescence-associated ß-galactosidase
micronuclei
G1-arrest
Many researchers target for SA b-galactosidase + p21 (p16INK4A) expression as well as IL-6 and IL-1alpha

validation strategies

10 minute spin in the minispinner

### word/document.xml
Welcome to the Cell Cycle project!!
The objective is to study the CellCycle IDS samples, primarily HRP, with some HRD samples included. CycIF experiments were conducted and analyzed for this cohort, enabling the identification of multiple cell phenotypes—immune, stromal, and tumor—based on specific markers. Additionally, functional markers related to the cell cycle, such as cyclins, p21, Ki67, and PCNA, were examined. The study also encompasses tumor meta-clustering and spatial analysis. Furthermore, we are developing a technique to isolate cells using the RareCyte scanner, followed by mass spectrometry analysis (results are available in this folder).
Project lead: Ada Junquera
Project members:
Ada Junquera (CycIF and project coordination)
Sara Palomino (bulk RNAseq + xenium + multiomics integration)
Nika Mikhailava (CycIF + GeoMx)
Hanna Elomaa (experimentalist - xenium + post Xenium cycif)
